explain_prompt.system:
You are a senior software architect explaining the failure mode that GO1B1-05 — Refresh the ground-truth harness on a declared cadence to reflect changes in production inputs and usage patterns — prevents, using a single illustrative scenario from a real industry.

THE PRINCIPLE

The ground-truth evaluation harness established under GO1B1-01 must be refreshed on a cadence declared in a versioned artefact in the workload repository (this catalogue's reference implementation: `eval/refresh/config.yaml`). Each refresh adds, updates, or retires scenarios so the harness continues to represent the inputs and usage patterns the workload sees in production. CI gates enforce both: the cadence config exists and the last refresh was within the declared interval; and any PR touching agent code after a drift-monitor alert (GO1B1-04) must include a refresh entry or a linked ADR justifying why the alert does not warrant refresh.

THE FAILURE SHAPE

This is a cumulative-drift failure in the environment-shift sub-shape: a static evaluation harness surrounded by an evolving production world. The team has a workload in production with all four sibling disciplines in place — harness committed (GO1B1-01), stratified (GO1B1-02), with encapsulated metrics (GO1B1-03), and a drift monitor firing (GO1B1-04). Per-PR CI gates pass. But the team never refreshes the harness against current production patterns. Customer behaviour evolves, new product categories appear, upstream data shifts, vocabulary drifts. The harness still scores high on every PR — because it tests against patterns from deployment day, not against patterns from production today. The drift monitor fires every quarter alerting that production has diverged from baseline; nobody adds new scenarios in response; the monitor continues to fire against a baseline that's never updated.

By the time the failure surfaces, the harness has been silently irrelevant for months. CI green; production decisions increasingly out of sync. The trigger is always external: a customer-success quarterly review noticing decisions on the new product category are wrong; an audit finding the eval set hasn't been refreshed in 18 months; a senior joiner reviewing the harness and finding scenarios referencing product types the company retired long ago.

The principle's value is forcing the team to declare, at deployment time, a refresh cadence with audit-inspectable refresh logs and explicit drift-alert coupling. Drift detection alone doesn't fix the harness — refresh closes the loop.

APPLICABILITY

GO1B1-05 is mandatory for agent (autonomous, tool-using, multi-step) workloads — paired with GO1B1-01, which is also agentic-only because the harness entry shape (input + expected decision + expected evidence trace) is agent-specific. If the chosen Implementation type is not 'Agent', open use_case_background with one italicised sentence noting the principle is most acute for autonomous agents and that the example below is illustrative of how the same harness-staleness failure would manifest in the chosen pattern (once sibling harness principles are authored for that pattern).

YOUR TASK

You will be given an Industry and an Implementation type. Write an illustrative example for that combination demonstrating GO1B1-05's failure mode.

OUTPUT CONTRACT

Respond with a single valid JSON object of exactly this shape and nothing else:

{
  "use_case_background": "string — 150–250 words. Describe a workload in production with all four sibling disciplines in place: harness committed, stratified, metrics encapsulated, drift monitor running. Name the team, the workload, the integrating systems (with plausible invented names). Establish the baseline at deployment — what the harness covered, what production looked like. Make clear the team is competent and doing everything the first four principles ask. Do NOT describe the failure here.",
  "issue_detail": "string — 200–350 words. Describe how the harness drifts silently from current production reality. Walk through chronologically: name what changes in production (customer behaviour shifts, new product categories, vocabulary drift, upstream data evolution) and how each change is invisible to the harness because the harness still tests against the deployment-time set. Mention the drift monitor firing without anyone refreshing the harness in response. Describe the external trigger that finally surfaces it — quarterly business review, customer-success escalation, audit, senior joiner reviewing the eval set. Make the consequence concrete: weeks or months of stale decisions, manual remediation, time spent reconstructing what production looks like now vs what the harness assumes. The failure must demonstrate something a refresh cadence with audit log would have caught at the point of drift, not a generic 'the model got worse over time'."
}

STYLE RULES

- Concrete over abstract. Name systems, roles, decisions. 'The returns triage agent' not 'the AI system'.
- Match the implementation type honestly: RAG retrieves before generating; Agent reasons over multiple steps and calls tools; LLM Only is single-turn or chained without retrieval or tools; ML is classical with no LLM in the loop.
- Match the industry honestly. Use vocabulary the industry actually uses.
- Use specific numbers where they sharpen the story, but signpost invented precision honestly ('roughly 40% of Q4 volume', 'about three quarters before the pattern surfaced').
- Do not invent regulatory specifics. Generic framing only.
- Both sections are prose paragraphs. No bullet lists, no headings, no markdown emphasis other than the optional italicised opening sentence described above.
- Do not include the principle's solution, the fix, or recommendations. Show the problem only.
- Do not write any text outside the JSON object.

standards.u_principle: A model never moves under a system unnoticed; every change is known and re-proven before it ships.

references.title: GPT-5.1 Was Retired on March 11 — Here's What Broke in Your LLM App
references.note: A provider silently auto-falls-back a retired model alias to a different model — no error, no warning, no version bump, subtle output-format drift — the exact silent model-swap this principle's pin + re-eval-on-change gate catches.

references.title: The Silent Versioning Problem in AI Inference
references.note: Documents one endpoint string returning meaningfully different model behaviour over time (e.g. the GPT-4o sycophancy regimes) with no code change on the caller's side — why pinning and re-evaluation on model change are mandatory.

dependencies.reason: The re-eval-on-model-change gate re-runs GO1B1-01's harness against the new model version and gates on its baseline.

references.question: GENOPS01 — How do you achieve and verify consistent model output quality?
references.best_practice: GENOPS01-BP01 — Periodically evaluate functional performance
references.note: Anchors to GENOPS01-BP01's implementation-guidance directive rather than one of its seven numbered steps (which the GO1B1-01..05 eval-harness family already concretises). The verbatim directive: 'Run these evaluations when new candidate models are available, or when model customization techniques are applied. For example, fine-tuned and customized models should be subject to the same evaluation criteria and cadence as non-customized models.' (verified against the live AWS Lens page, fetched 2026-06-04). This is the 'future model-change gate' the catalogue earmarked when GENOPS01-BP01 was first walked. AWS states the trigger (re-evaluate when the model changes) but leaves four things unspecified that this principle concretises: (1) how a model change is detected at all — AWS assumes you know the model changed, but a workload on a floating alias does not; (2) the pinning + approved-model-catalog mechanism that makes a change detectable and reviewable; (3) the runtime SDK contract refusing un-pinned model calls; (4) the pre-merge gate coupling a model-identifier change to a mandatory eval-harness re-run. Cross-references: the pin/catalog half is the model-side twin of GO3B1-01 (prompt registry) and concretises the 'model versioning' clause named in the GENOPS03 question ('How do you maintain traceability for your models, prompts, and assets?'), which ships no dedicated BP; it also concretises the GenAI Lens Reliability design principle 'Standardize resource management through catalogs: maintain centralized catalogs for prompts and models ... version control ... updates or rollbacks.' implementation_step is null per the schema's whole-BP/guidance-clause exception; the verbatim directive is quoted above.

references.domain: Domain III: Understanding How to Govern AI Development
references.competency: III.B: Govern the AI Model Lifecycle (Design & Build)
references.note: Provisional mapping based on subject-matter proximity — pinning model versions and gating model changes on re-evaluation is a model-lifecycle governance control, aligning with III.B's scope (the same anchor as the cost siblings GC1B1-01 / GC2B2-01 / GC3B1-01). Distinct from the data-governance III.A the rest of the GO1B1 eval family maps to, because this principle governs the model artefact's version lifecycle, not the ground-truth data. Needs a side-by-side review against the AIGP III.B competency definition to promote to verified.

statement.title: Know which model you're running, and re-prove behaviour before you change it
statement.description:
A model can change underneath a workload without warning, through provider roll-forwards, deprecations, or default upgrades.

Which version is in use should be a deliberate, explicit choice, and any change to it should be re-checked against known-good behaviour before it ships.

problem.description:
Without pinning model references to immutable versions and gating model changes on re-evaluation, the foundation model can change underneath a workload without anyone deciding it should. A team that calls the model through a floating alias, a provider `-latest` pointer, or a shared-gateway default inherits whatever the provider or platform ships next — a roll-forward, a deprecation-and-migration, a quietly upgraded default.

The agent's own code, prompts, and tests are untouched, so no PR is opened and no per-PR or drift gate fires; infrastructure dashboards stay green because the model is responding, just differently. The behaviour shifts — tone, verbosity, formatting, refusal rate, a few-shot pattern the prompt relied on — and the first signal is customers, not CI.

This is distinct from the gradual cumulative drift GO1B1-04 watches for: a model swap is a discrete step-change at a single moment, which a window-to-window drift threshold can miss entirely and which has no per-PR trigger at all.

Examples below are illustrative — synthesised scenarios; specific numbers are not from cited evidence.
examples: A retail customer-support chatbot calls the model via the provider's `-latest` alias; overnight the provider rolls the alias to a newer, more cautious model; by morning the bot hedges every answer with disclaimers and refuses routine account questions it used to handle; no PR was opened, infra dashboards are green, and the team only learns of it from a CSAT drop a week later.

A workload pins the exact model in dev but reads the model name from an env var in prod that defaults to the gateway's current default; a platform-wide gateway upgrade swaps that default; the prod agent silently runs a different model than dev, and the divergence is invisible until output differences are reported by a downstream team.

A provider deprecates the dated model snapshot a workload used and auto-migrates its calls to the successor; response formatting changes just enough to break a downstream JSON parser the agent's outputs feed; the breakage is attributed to 'the model getting worse' rather than to an un-pinned version that moved.

A team upgrades the model deliberately by editing one line, assuming the newer model is strictly better; it regresses on a minority class of cases the eval harness already covers, but nothing forces the harness to re-run on a model change, so the regression ships and surfaces as customer complaints weeks later.

A model swap lands a clean overnight step-change in behaviour that falls inside a single drift-monitor window, so GO1B1-04's window-to-window threshold never flags it; with no gate on the model identifier itself, there is no control positioned to catch the change at its source.

solution.approach:
Pin every model reference to an exact, immutable version drawn from an approved model catalog, and gate model changes on re-evaluation:

- Maintain a model catalog of approved pinned model identifiers (convention `models/catalog.yaml`, platform-owned), each entry an exact version — a dated provider snapshot or a self-hosted weights digest — never an alias.
- A pre-merge lint scans the workload for every model reference (config, code, env defaults) and fails the PR unless each resolves to a catalog entry; floating aliases (`-latest`, `default`) and un-catalogued identifiers fail.
- The central model client / SDK refuses at runtime to call any model not resolved to a catalog entry, closing the dynamically-resolved-alias hole the static lint cannot see — the same contract shape as GO3B1-01's SDK refusing inline prompts, applied to model identifiers.
- A pre-merge gate detects any change to a pinned model identifier (diff vs the merge base) and requires the workload's evaluation harness (GO1B1-01) to re-run and clear its baseline bar before the PR can merge; a model change that fails the harness blocks.
- Because the version is pinned in config, rollback after a bad model is a one-line revert to the prior catalog entry.

The model catalog, the catalog-membership lint, and the SDK refusal are platform-team-owned; the workload owns which catalogued version it pins and the eval result on change. This principle gates the model-change trigger; it reuses GO1B1-01's harness for the evaluation itself and does not re-implement evaluation machinery.
key_benefits: Makes a model change impossible without a reviewed PR and impossible to ship without re-evaluation — the silent overnight provider roll-forward that today surfaces as a CSAT drop becomes a blocked, evaluated, deliberate change.

Closes the hole the drift monitor can miss — a model swap is a discrete step-change, not the gradual sub-threshold drift GO1B1-04 watches for; this gate catches the change at its source (the model identifier) rather than after the behaviour has already shifted in production.

Catalog membership enforces pinning by construction — the catalog holds only exact versions, so 'must be in the catalog' means 'must be pinned'; the runtime SDK refusal extends the guarantee to env-default and dynamically-resolved references the static lint cannot see (the GO3B1-01 SDK pattern, applied to models).

Re-evaluation reuses the existing eval harness — no new evaluation machinery; the model-change gate simply re-runs GO1B1-01 against the new version and gates on the baseline, turning 'we assumed the new model was better' into 'the new model passed our bar'.
Rollback is a one-line config revert — because the version is pinned, recovering from a bad model is reverting one catalog reference, not a scramble to reconstruct what changed and when.

gates.check: On any PR, a lint scans the workload for every foundation-model reference (model identifiers in config such as `eval/config.yaml` or `models/*.yaml`, in `src/`, and in environment-variable defaults) and fails the PR unless each reference resolves to an entry in the approved model catalog (`models/catalog.yaml` or the platform-owned catalog it points to). Floating aliases (`-latest`, `default`, an undated provider pointer) and identifiers absent from the catalog fail, since every catalog entry is an exact immutable version (a dated provider snapshot or a self-hosted weights digest). Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

On any PR that changes a pinned model identifier (diff vs the merge base on any model reference the catalog-membership lint tracks), the workload's evaluation harness (GO1B1-01) must re-run against the new model version and clear its declared baseline bar; the PR fails if the harness has not re-run for the new identifier, or if it re-ran and did not pass the baseline, unless a linked ADR under `docs/adrs/` justifies the exception. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

changes.author: Dheeraj
changes.summary: Initial authoring against AWS GENOPS01-BP01 (Periodically evaluate functional performance), anchored on its implementation-guidance directive 'Run these evaluations when new candidate models are available, or when model customization techniques are applied' (verbatim verified against the live AWS Lens page, fetched 2026-06-04) — the 'future model-change gate' the catalogue earmarked when GENOPS01-BP01 was first walked. Sixth principle in the GO1B1 eval family. Prompted by a customer-experience failure example: a support chatbot whose model silently moved under it (provider rolled a floating alias forward), degrading answers with no PR, no CI signal, and green infra dashboards. step_promotion rubric 3/3/3/3 (enforceable artefacts: approved model catalog + catalog-membership lint + re-eval-on-change gate; architecturally distinct from GO1B1-04 drift — discrete model-swap step-change vs gradual cumulative drift, named with the window-miss argument; in-bp-scope: GENOPS01-BP01 guidance literally directs re-evaluation on model change; not vendor menu: Bedrock/SageMaker tooling stripped, generic pin-and-re-eval mandate survives). ANCHOR NOTE: no dedicated GenAI Lens BP exists for model versioning — GENOPS03's question names 'model versioning' but ships only prompt-management (GO3B1-01) and tracing (GO3B2) BPs; the model-change re-eval directive lives in GENOPS01-BP01's implementation guidance, not a numbered step (the 7 numbered steps are already concretised by GO1B1-01..05). implementation_step set null per the schema's guidance-clause/whole-BP exception with the verbatim quoted in the note; the pin/catalog half is cross-referenced as the model-side twin of GO3B1-01 and to the Reliability 'standardized catalogs for prompts and models' design principle. SCOPE: one principle — pinning + approved-model-catalog membership is the enabling mechanism (a change is undetectable on a floating alias), and the re-evaluation gate is the principle; the runtime SDK refusal of un-pinned models (GO3B1-01 pattern applied to models) is carried in solution.approach as a runtime control, not a separate pre_merge gate, parallel to how GO3B1-01 framed its runtime refusal. Hand-scored against section rubrics: statement 3/2/3/2/3; problem 3/3/3/3/3/3 (every example is a discrete model-substitution failure that per-PR gates miss and the drift monitor can miss); solution 3/3/3/3/3/2; gates 3/3/3/3/3/3 (two pre_merge required-status-check gates: catalog-membership; re-eval-on-model-change). tier rubric D1=no (model-lifecycle governance; borderline yes in regulated model-risk-management regimes, same hedge as GO1B1-04) / D2=3 (model catalog + membership lint + runtime-refusing SDK + re-eval gate wiring + rollback tooling — substantial platform engineering reused across projects) → recommended_centralise → enterprise; validator project_architect, audit_mode self_attestation_with_mechanical_evidence, arb_role dashboard_and_spot_check parallel to GO1B1-04. maturity_level scaling — pays off at project #2: needs the GO1B1-01 eval-harness substrate to gate against and a deployed model to change, and the enforcement infra (central model catalog + SDK) amortises across the portfolio; foundational rejected because the gated discipline (not the bare pin) needs the harness substrate and platform tooling, parallel with GO1B1-04. serving_paradigm all four mandatory — AWS-billed workloads face provider roll-forwards / deprecations / gateway-default swaps; self-hosted workloads still need the re-eval-on-change gate when new weights are deployed (the pin is trivially under their control there, but the gate is not). impact_level High parallel to the GO1B1 family and AWS's HIGH risk rating for GENOPS01-BP01; the worst-case is a silent customer-facing regression, a High-shape consequence. applicability { llm/rag/agentic: mandatory, ml: nice_to_have } — classical ML typically already has model-registry/version discipline via MLOps tooling. AIGP III.B (Govern the AI Model Lifecycle) unverified — note the deliberate shift from the III.A data-governance anchor the rest of the GO1B1 family uses, because this principle governs the model artefact's version lifecycle, not the ground-truth data. explain_prompt compiled with setup_absence failure shape (the pin + re-eval discipline was never built; a provider/gateway model change makes the absence visible) — distinguished from GO1B1-04's cumulative_drift shape because this is a discrete model-substitution event, not gradual environmental drift. RI README at data/ri/GO1B1-06/README.md following the GO1B1-04 template. OPEN ITEMS: (1) JSON parse not run in-session — the sandbox could not reach the workspace (UNC mount); verify with `python3 -c "import json; json.load(open('data/principles.json'))"` from a terminal before pushing. (2) No dedicated AWS BP for model versioning is itself a documented Lens gap worth recording in lens_mapping under GENOPS03; the model-registry twin of GO3B1-01 may warrant its own extension principle later.

explain_prompt.system:
You are a senior software architect explaining the failure mode that GO1B1-06 — Pin every model call to an immutable, catalogued version and re-run the evaluation harness before any version change ships — prevents, using a single illustrative scenario from a real industry.

THE PRINCIPLE

Every model reference in a workload must resolve to an exact, immutable model version drawn from an approved model catalog — never a floating alias (a `-latest` pointer) and never a runtime-resolved default. A pre-merge lint fails any model reference that is not a catalogued pinned version; the central model SDK refuses at runtime to call an un-pinned model; and any change to a pinned model identifier must re-run the workload's evaluation harness and clear its baseline bar before it can merge. Because the version is pinned, rollback is a one-line config revert.

THE FAILURE SHAPE

This is a setup-absence failure: the pin-and-re-evaluate discipline was never built, and a discrete model-substitution event makes the absence visible. The team has a workload in production that calls the foundation model — but through a floating alias, a provider `-latest` pointer, or a shared-gateway default rather than a pinned, catalogued version. Nothing watches the model identity, and no gate couples a model change to re-evaluation. Locally everything looks fine: the model responds, latency and error-rate dashboards are green, and the team has not changed their own code. The discipline that nobody set up is the one that makes a model change a deliberate, reviewed, evaluated event instead of something that can happen TO the workload from outside.

The absence becomes visible at a discrete triggering moment that is outside the team's control: the provider rolls the `-latest` alias forward to a newer model overnight; the provider deprecates the dated snapshot the workload used and auto-migrates its calls to a successor; a platform-wide gateway upgrade changes the default model the workload's env var resolves to. The agent's code, prompts, and tests are untouched, so no PR is opened and no per-PR or drift gate fires. But the model underneath has changed, and its behaviour shifts — more verbose, more cautious, different formatting, a higher refusal rate, a few-shot pattern the prompt relied on no longer honoured. The first signal is not CI; it is customers — a CSAT drop, a spike in complaints, a downstream parser breaking on the new output format, a class of requests quietly mishandled. The team cannot point to a change they made, because they did not make one; the change happened to them, and there was no control positioned to catch a foundation model moving underneath a workload that was otherwise standing still.

The principle's value is forcing the model to be a pinned, catalogued artefact that cannot move without a reviewed PR, and cannot ship without re-evaluation — so a model change is always deliberate, always evaluated, and always one revert away from rollback.

APPLICABILITY

GO1B1-06 is mandatory for LLM Only, RAG, and Agent workloads (anywhere a foundation model is called and can be moved by a provider, a deprecation, or a gateway). It is nice_to_have for traditional ML workloads, which typically already carry model-version discipline through established MLOps registries. If the chosen Implementation type is ML, open use_case_background with one italicised sentence noting the principle is most acute for foundation-model workloads where the model is a provider-served dependency that can change outside the team's control, and that the example below is illustrative of how the same setup-absence failure would manifest if an ML workload's model registry did not pin and re-validate on version change.

YOUR TASK

You will be given an Industry and an Implementation type. Write an illustrative example for that combination demonstrating GO1B1-06's failure mode.

OUTPUT CONTRACT

Respond with a single valid JSON object of exactly this shape and nothing else:

{
  "use_case_background": "string — 150–250 words. Describe an enterprise with a workload in production that calls a foundation model. Be specific about the industry and the implementation type. Name the team, the workload, the integrating systems (with plausible invented names), and how the model is referenced — crucially, an UN-pinned way: a provider `-latest` alias, an env var defaulting to a shared gateway's current model, a model name copied between repos. Hint offhandedly that nobody watches model identity, that the budget/quality was validated once at launch, that model upgrades have never been a review-time concern. The reader should picture a confident team that does not realise the model is a moving dependency. Do NOT describe the failure here.",
  "issue_detail": "string — 200–350 words. Describe the moment the model moves underneath the workload. Walk through chronologically: name the trigger (the provider rolls the `-latest` alias forward; the provider deprecates the dated snapshot and auto-migrates; a gateway upgrade swaps the default). Emphasise that this is OUTSIDE the team's control and that NO PR is opened, so per-PR and drift gates never fire and infra dashboards stay green. Then describe how the behaviour shifts (verbosity, caution, formatting, refusal rate, a broken downstream parser) and how the team discovers it — a CSAT drop, customer complaints, a downstream team reporting output differences — not from CI. Name roles (the platform engineer who eventually correlates it to the model change; the support lead escalating; the on-call who finds the version moved days ago). Make the consequence concrete — degraded customer experience across every interaction for days, a scramble to identify what changed, a rollback that is hard because nothing was pinned. The failure must demonstrate something a pinned, catalogued model plus a re-eval-on-change gate would have caught at the moment of change — not a generic 'the model got worse' regression, and specifically NOT gradual drift (this is a discrete swap)."
}

STYLE RULES

- Concrete over abstract. Name systems, roles, decisions. 'The claims-triage chatbot' not 'the AI system'.
- Match the implementation type honestly: RAG retrieves before generating; Agent reasons over multiple steps and calls tools; LLM Only is single-turn or prompt-chained without retrieval or tools; ML is classical with no LLM in the loop.
- Match the industry honestly. Use vocabulary the industry actually uses.
- Use specific numbers where they sharpen the story, but signpost invented precision honestly ('roughly a week before anyone correlated it', 'about a 20% jump in refusals').
- Do not invent regulatory specifics. Generic framing only ('the firm's quarterly service review', 'the platform team's incident review').
- Both sections are prose paragraphs. No bullet lists, no headings, no markdown emphasis other than the optional italicised opening sentence described above.
- Do not include the principle's solution, the fix, or recommendations. Show the problem only.
- Do not write any text outside the JSON object.

standards.u_principle: The instructions that drive a system are owned and accountable, not scattered and forgotten.

references.title: The Definitive Guide to Prompt Management Systems
references.note: Describes prompts hardcoded and scattered across multiple files and repos with no way to track changes or roll back — the fragmentation and silent divergence this principle's central prompt registry eliminates.

references.title: A Taxonomy of Prompt Defects in LLM Systems
references.note: Catalogues the defect classes that arise when prompts are unmanaged in production — evidence for governing prompts as registered, versioned, addressable artefacts rather than inline strings.

references.question: GENOPS03 — How do you maintain traceability for your models, prompts, and assets?
references.best_practice: GENOPS03-BP01 — Implement prompt template management

implementation_step.title: Set up Amazon Bedrock Prompt Management
implementation_step.verbatim_text: Set up Amazon Bedrock Prompt Management. Create the initial prompt templates by developing a foundational set of prompt templates tailored to your use case. Incorporate variables within prompts to enhance flexibility and adaptability. Implement a robust versioning system to track changes and iterations of prompt templates.

references.note: AWS step 1 mandates a versioned prompt template management system with variables, expressed in vendor-specific terms (Amazon Bedrock Prompt Management). This principle absorbs the architectural mandate — every prompt is a registered, versioned, addressable template with declared variables — and adds four concretisations AWS leaves unspecified: (1) the SDK call-signature contract `(template_id, variables)` refusing inline strings as runtime enforcement; (2) the manifest schema declaring per-template metadata (id, version, body, variables, output_schema, model, runtime_token_budget, owner, status); (3) two-mode storage neutrality (file-based + manifest vs hosted TMS) with mode-parameterised CI lints; (4) the no-inline-call AST lint banning direct provider-SDK invocations from workload code. AWS step 4 (Integrate prompts into applications — use Bedrock SDK during inference) is absorbed into this principle: the SDK call-signature contract is inseparable from the template registry. Steps 2 / 3 / 5 / 6 are non-promoted — step 2 (baseline performance eval) and step 3 (A/B variant testing) absorbed by the GO1B1 eval-harness family; step 5 (periodic review) is process advice without a commitable artefact; step 6 (cross-team collaboration) is the mechanical consequence of step 1's central registry.

references.domain: Domain IV: Understanding How to Govern AI Deployment and Use
references.competency: IV.A: Establish Governance Policies, Processes, and Standards
references.note: Primary mapping. The registered template registry plus the SDK call-signature contract IS the governance standard for prompt content at the technical layer — every prompt has an owner, a version, a declared lifecycle status, and a contract for how it's accessed. IV.A's expectation that governance policies be operationalised through standards and processes is directly served by the manifest schema and the SDK enforcement. Needs side-by-side review against the AIGP IV.A competency definition to promote to verified.

references.domain: Domain IV: Understanding How to Govern AI Deployment and Use
references.competency: IV.B: Implement Ongoing Risk Assessments and Monitoring
references.note: Secondary mapping. The registry's addressable template_id is the substrate that prompt-layer monitoring keys on — token-cost dashboards, content classification, safety classification, prompt-drift detection all require an addressable thing to attach to. Without GO3B1-01 in place, GO3B2-01's observability emission has nothing structured to correlate at the prompt layer. Needs review against AIGP IV.B to verify.

statement.title: Treat prompts as governed assets, not scattered strings
statement.description:
Prompts buried inline across the codebase cannot be tracked, reviewed, or rolled back.

They should be managed as deliberate, versioned assets — a known source of truth the rest of the system can rely on.

problem.description:
Without registered, versioned prompt templates accessed via a central SDK that refuses inline strings, prompt content lives as f-strings scattered across the codebase — invisible as discrete artefacts, untrackable by template identity, and unauditable as anything other than git archaeology over the assembled runtime view.

The failure compounds because the path of least resistance for any deadline-pressured engineer is the inline call, and once even one workload bypasses the registry, the SDK's contract becomes voluntary across the portfolio rather than mandatory.

Every downstream gate that would key on template identity — token budgets, PII content review, safety classification, model-routing policies, prompt-drift monitoring — collapses, because there is no addressable thing for the gate to attach to. Versioning becomes git-history reconstruction across unrelated commits rather than a first-class field, making rollback after a bad model upgrade or prompt change a manual reconstruction exercise.

The central platform team cannot enforce the contract retroactively because the entry point that should refuse non-registered calls has already been permissive — within a release cycle every team builds its own pattern and drift sets in.

(Examples below are illustrative narratives, not documented incidents.)
examples: A classifier prompt is assembled at runtime from three f-string fragments concatenated across `src/agents/triage.py`, `src/agents/prompts.py`, and `src/utils/policy_text.py`; when a compliance reviewer asks for the exact prompt currently routed to the model, an engineer has to run the assembly in a debugger and paste the result, with no guarantee the captured form matches the next invocation.

A workload keeps prompt bodies under `prompts/` in the repo but the central SDK still accepts raw strings; a deadline-pressured engineer inlines a new endpoint's prompt directly into the handler, the registry silently goes out of date, and the discrepancy is invisible until a portfolio-wide audit months later flags untracked template-shaped content in `src/`.

Six months after the catalogue ships a token-budget principle keyed on `template_id`, a workload still using inline-string prompts cannot register any budgets — the budget gate has no addressable thing to bite on — and the principle is unenforceable on that workload until a multi-week prompt-extraction project is funded.

A model upgrade requires rewriting every prompt for the new context-window shape; with no version field on the prompts, the rollback path after a regression is `git revert` across 14 unrelated commits, and the previous prompt content is reconstructed from memory by the engineer who originally wrote it, shipping the rollback with subtle drift.

A regulator asks for the exact prompt and variable schema in production for the customer-facing summarisation endpoint as of a specific date; with no manifest, no version field, and prompt fragments scattered across handlers, the answer is reconstructed by grepping git history and reading multiple file diffs against the requested date — a several-hour archaeology exercise that becomes a recurring tax across audits.

solution.approach:
All AI workload prompts flow through a single enterprise SDK with a `(template_id, variables)` call signature; the SDK refuses inline-string prompts at runtime and rejects any call referencing a `template_id` not present in the workload's registry.

Every registered template carries a stable metadata schema: `id`, `version`, body content, declared `variables`, `output_schema` (when applicable), `model`, `runtime_token_budget` (input + output), `owner`, and `status` (draft / active / deprecated / archived).

The principle is neutral on storage backend; the workload picks one of two registration modes, both producing the same SDK-visible contract:

- **Mode A (file-based + manifest)** — bodies at `prompts/<template_id>.{md,j2}`, metadata rows in `prompts/manifest.yaml`; git is the version backend; CI lints walk `prompts/` and `src/` for template_id ↔ manifest row ↔ body file consistency. Fit for single-repo workloads with engineering-owned prompt content.
- **Mode B (hosted TMS)** — bodies and metadata in a hosted prompt management service; the SDK fetches at build time (cached) or at runtime; CI lints query the service API for the same consistency checks. Specific vendor services and their adapter implementations are listed in `framework_mappings` notes. Fit for multi-repo enterprises sharing templates cross-workload, or for workloads with business / product stakeholders editing prompt content through a UI.

Project teams in either mode call `sdk.generate(template_id="X", variables={...})`; the SDK resolves the template_id from the configured backend, composes system + user content, validates supplied variables against the declared schema, tokenises the assembled prompt and the response using the model's own tokenizer to log actual-vs-declared budget, and dispatches the API call with `max_tokens` set to the declared output budget. Direct calls to provider SDKs (`anthropic.Anthropic().messages.create`, `openai.OpenAI().responses.create`, the Bedrock / Azure equivalents) from workload code are prohibited; providers are reached only via the central SDK's adapter layer.

The central platform team owns the SDK, the registration metadata schema, the CI lint suite (parameterised per mode), the tokenizer integration, and the TMS-adapter set if Mode B is supported organisation-wide; project teams own the prompt body content, the metadata field values, and the budget rationale in their change history. An escape-hatch `dev_only=True` flag permits unregistered template calls against dev model endpoints only, so prototype iteration does not require a registration round-trip.
key_benefits: Prompt content becomes an addressable artefact — `template_id` keys everything downstream (cost dashboards, content review, safety classification, drift monitoring); the substrate the entire prompt-governance stack depends on exists by mandate, not by accident.

Versioning is first-class — the registry's `version` field plus the body's content-addressed history make rollback a one-row revert rather than a 14-commit reconstruction; regulator answers come from one registry query, not from grep + reasoning over assembled f-strings.

Token budgets become enforceable — the registry declares `input_token_budget` and `output_token_budget` per template; the SDK measures actual on every call via the model's own tokenizer and emits overrun signals; the downstream GENCOST03-BP01 budget principle has an addressable thing to bite on.

Inline-string bypass is mechanically impossible — the SDK refuses raw-string calls at runtime, the CI lint blocks direct provider-SDK imports from workload code, and the no-orphan-template lint catches drift between registry and body content; the contract is enforced at three layers rather than relying on developer discipline.

Ownership and lifecycle are explicit — every template has a named `owner` (CODEOWNERS-style routing for reviews and incidents) and a `status` field that lets dev and prod environments diverge cleanly (draft templates rejected in prod; deprecated templates log warnings and feed a migration queue).

gates.check: Lint walks `src/` and extracts every `template_id` referenced in calls to the central LLM SDK (AST scan for `sdk.generate(template_id=...)`, `sdk.call(template_id=...)`, and equivalent entry points). Each extracted `template_id` must resolve to a registry entry: Mode A workloads require both a row in `prompts/manifest.yaml` AND a matching body file at the declared `body` path; Mode B workloads require the configured TMS API to return a record for that id. Conversely, every body file under `prompts/` (Mode A) or every record in the workload's TMS inventory (Mode B) must have a matching reference in `src/`, and every registry row must declare non-empty values for `id`, `version`, `body`, `variables`, `model`, `runtime_token_budget.input`, `runtime_token_budget.output`, `owner`, and `status` ∈ {draft, active, deprecated, archived}. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

AST-grep lint scans `src/` for direct invocations of provider model SDKs: `anthropic.Anthropic().messages.create()`, `openai.OpenAI().responses.create()` and `openai.OpenAI().chat.completions.create()`, `boto3.client("bedrock-runtime").invoke_model()`, Azure OpenAI client `.chat.completions.create()` and `.responses.create()`, plus any others on the prohibited-imports list at `prompts/prohibited_calls.yaml`. The list is maintained by the platform team and pinned with the central LLM SDK version. Any direct invocation found in workload code outside the central LLM SDK package fails the PR. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

Lint verifies the workload pins the central LLM SDK at a version ≥ the floor declared in `prompts/sdk_floor.txt`, maintained by the platform team and bumped at each registry-schema cutover (new required metadata fields, breaking changes to the SDK call signature, deprecated tokenizer adapter). Fails if the pinned version is below the floor, preventing deprecated registration schemas or call signatures from reaching production. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

explain_prompt.system:
You are a senior software architect explaining the failure mode that GO3B1-01 — Route every model call through a registered, versioned prompt template via the central SDK — prevents, using a single illustrative scenario from a real industry.

THE PRINCIPLE

Every prompt used in production lives as a registered, versioned template addressable by `template_id`, with its body, declared variables, output schema, model, token budgets, owner, and lifecycle status declared in a workload registry (file-based + manifest, or a hosted prompt management service). The central LLM SDK refuses inline-string prompts at runtime and accepts only `(template_id, variables)` call signatures; direct invocations of provider model SDKs (Anthropic, OpenAI, Bedrock, Azure) from workload code are prohibited. A central platform team owns the SDK, the registration metadata schema, the CI lint suite (template_id consistency, no-inline-call ban, version floor), and the tokenizer integration; project teams own the prompt body content and the metadata field values.

THE FAILURE SHAPE

This is a setup-absence failure that becomes visible the moment the enterprise needs to answer a portfolio-level question about its prompts, or the moment a downstream gate (token budgets, safety classification, prompt-drift monitoring) tries to operate on a `template_id` that doesn't exist. Multiple project teams have shipped AI workloads, each writing its prompts inline as Python f-strings, concatenated YAML, or string templates scattered across handlers and utility modules. Each team's setup looks fine locally: the prompts work, the model returns sensible output, the on-call engineer can find the file. The discipline that nobody built is the central one: there is no template registry, no SDK contract refusing raw strings, no versioning of prompts as discrete artefacts, no addressable `template_id` for downstream tooling to key on.

The absence is invisible until a triggering event forces it into view: finance commissions a portfolio-wide prompt-cost dashboard and discovers cost attribution cannot be done without manually identifying every prompt in every workload; a model upgrade requires rewriting prompts and the rollback path after a regression turns into a multi-day git-archaeology exercise per workload; the catalogue ships a token-budget principle keyed on `template_id` and workloads with inline prompts cannot register any budgets because there is no addressable thing to bind a budget to; a regulator asks for the exact prompt and variable schema that produced a specific customer-facing decision and the answer requires running the assembly in a debugger and pasting the result, with no guarantee the captured form matches what the next invocation would produce.

At that moment the enterprise discovers that the cost of retrofitting prompt registration — extracting inline strings into template files, declaring variables, refactoring every call site to use the SDK, building the lint suite, training every team on the contract — is many times the cost of having mandated the SDK contract before the second workload shipped. The principle's value is forcing the registry and the SDK contract to exist before the second workload is built, so prompt cost attribution, prompt versioning, prompt content review, downstream safety classification, and audit-time reconstruction become emergent properties of the system rather than retrofits paid for in audit findings, finance reconciliation hours, and multi-quarter remediation projects.

APPLICABILITY

GO3B1-01 is mandatory for LLM Only, RAG, and Agent workloads — anywhere prompts exist as a first-class artefact mediating model behaviour. It is nice-to-have for traditional ML workloads, which typically have no prompt-shaped artefacts beyond model cards or feature descriptions. If the chosen Implementation type is ML, open `use_case_background` with one italicised sentence noting the principle is most acute for LLM-based and agentic workloads where prompts ARE the primary artefact, and the example below is illustrative of how the same setup-absence failure would manifest inside an ML pipeline whose closest analogue is unmanaged feature-engineering text or experiment configuration.

YOUR TASK

You will be given an Industry and an Implementation type. Write an illustrative example for that combination demonstrating GO3B1-01's failure mode.

OUTPUT CONTRACT

Respond with a single valid JSON object of exactly this shape and nothing else:

{
  "use_case_background": "string — 150–250 words. Describe an enterprise that has multiple AI workloads in production. Be specific about the industry and the implementation type. Name two or three workloads owned by different teams, the integrating systems (with plausible invented names), and how each team's prompts are currently stored (inline f-strings in handlers, ad-hoc YAML files without registration, copy-paste between repos). The reader should picture a confident enterprise where each individual team's prompts work fine — the model responds, the feature ships, the team's on-call can debug. Crucially: hint at the absence offhandedly — note that field names for prompts differ across teams, that there is no central catalogue of what prompts are in production, that prompt versioning relies on git history of the file that happened to contain the string. Do NOT describe the failure here.",
  "issue_detail": "string — 200–350 words. Describe the moment the absent registry becomes visible. Walk through the scenario chronologically: name the triggering event (finance asks for portfolio-level prompt-cost attribution; a model upgrade forces a prompt rewrite and rollback fails; a token-budget gate is mandated by the catalogue but cannot bite because there are no template_ids; a regulator asks for the exact prompt in production as of a specific date). Then describe what the enterprise tries to do and where it falls apart — emailing teams, grepping repositories, reconstructing assembled prompts in debuggers, reasoning over git diffs to identify the prompt that was live on the requested date. Name roles (the platform engineer trying to build a prompt-cost dashboard; the SRE on-call trying to rollback a model upgrade; the compliance officer fielding a regulator's prompt-content request). Make the consequence concrete — measurable hours lost per audit request, a budget principle that cannot be applied to half the portfolio, a regulator commitment that cannot be met from the current evidence, a retrofit project that is now multi-quarter. The failure must demonstrate something a central SDK + registered template registry + version field would have caught or prevented — not a generic 'we don't document our prompts well' regression."
}

STYLE RULES

- Concrete over abstract. Name systems, roles, decisions. 'The triage classifier prompt in src/agents/triage.py' not 'the AI prompt'.
- Match the implementation type honestly: RAG retrieves before generating; Agent reasons over multiple steps and calls tools; LLM Only is single-turn or prompt-chained without retrieval or tools; ML is a classical supervised/unsupervised model with no LLM in the loop.
- Match the industry honestly. Use vocabulary the industry actually uses.
- Use specific numbers where they sharpen the story, but signpost invented precision honestly ('about six teams', 'roughly two weeks per audit request').
- Do not invent regulatory specifics. Generic framing only ('the firm's internal compliance review', 'the regulator's quarterly model-risk audit').
- Both sections are prose paragraphs. No bullet lists, no headings, no markdown emphasis other than the optional italicised opening sentence described above.
- Do not include the principle's solution, the fix, or recommendations. Show the problem only.
- Do not write any text outside the JSON object.

standards.u_principle: What a system did is never a mystery after the fact.

references.title: The AI Engineer's Guide to LLM Observability with OpenTelemetry
references.note: Shows generic telemetry lacks AI-specific context (model, version, token counts, prompt) so agents act without inspectable traces and cannot be debugged or audited — the gap this principle's standardized trace-emission contract fills.

references.question: GENOPS03 — How do you maintain traceability for your models, prompts, and assets?
references.best_practice: GENOPS03-BP02 — Enable tracing for agents and RAG workflows

implementation_step.title: Collect and aggregate trace data
implementation_step.verbatim_text: Implement a system to collect trace data from agents involved in your parallel processing workflow. After running an Amazon Bedrock Agent, view the trace in real-time as your agent performs orchestration. When making an InvokeAgent request to the Amazon Bedrock runtime endpoint, set the enableTrace field to TRUE. This will include a trace field in the InvokeAgent response for each chunk in the stream. Store this data in a centralized location, such as Amazon S3 or Amazon CloudWatch Logs, for quick access and analysis.

references.note: AWS step 1 calls for centralised collection and aggregation of agent trace data but expresses the mandate in vendor-specific terms (Bedrock InvokeAgent enableTrace flag, S3 or CloudWatch Logs as the persistence target). This principle absorbs the architectural mandate — every model call, tool invocation, and retrieval query emits a correlated span under a shared trace ID, persisted to a centrally-managed observability backend — and adds three concretisations AWS leaves unspecified: (1) the canonical event schema and header/payload split that makes traces from different teams comparable, (2) the central SDK and adapter pattern that makes the backend swap-able rather than locked to one AWS service, and (3) the merge-gate enforcement that prevents direct backend calls. AWS step 2 (Secure trace data — PII access control) and the implicit cost-attribution use case are also absorbed; step 1 is the closest AWS-verbatim anchor for the emission mandate.

references.domain: Domain IV: Understanding How to Govern AI Deployment and Use
references.competency: IV.B: Implement Ongoing Risk Assessments and Monitoring
references.note: Primary mapping. Centralised trace emission with mandatory header fields (project_id, workload_pattern, model_id, compliance_tier) is the technical substrate that makes ongoing monitoring possible at portfolio scale — without it, IV.B's expected risk-assessment cadence cannot be operated because evidence is fragmented across team-specific backends. Needs a side-by-side review against the AIGP IV.B competency definition to promote to verified.

references.domain: Domain IV: Understanding How to Govern AI Deployment and Use
references.competency: IV.C: Establish Security, Access Control, and Incident Management
references.note: Secondary mapping. The central PII pre-scrub, the approved-backends allow-list (gate 1), and trace-correlation across team boundaries directly serve IV.C's access-control and incident-management scope — incidents that cross team boundaries become reconstructable from a single trace_id instead of timestamp-guessed across fragmented logs. Needs review against AIGP IV.C to verify.

statement.title: Make every AI workload observable through one consistent channel
statement.description:
When each workload emits telemetry its own way, nothing can be debugged or audited across the estate.

AI observability should flow through one shared, AI-aware path that guarantees consistent context on every call.

problem.description:
Generative AI workloads emit observability data — traces, metrics, logs — that the enterprise needs for cost attribution, incident reconstruction, audit evidence, and cross-team correlation.

When each project team picks its own backend, writes its own emit code, and self-defines field names, the resulting fragmentation makes portfolio-level questions unanswerable:

- Cost attribution requires reconciling four price schemes by hand.
- PII leakage becomes per-team risk rather than a single chokepoint.
- Incidents that cross team boundaries are reconstructed by timestamp guessing instead of trace IDs.
- Schema drift between projects silently kills any chance of a cross-project dashboard.

The failure modes only surface at multi-project scale, and by then the retrofit cost is large.
examples: Fragmented backends with no shared billing — Project A ships to Datadog, Project B to Langfuse, Project C self-hosts ClickHouse. Finance gets three invoices in three currencies with no per-project breakdown; allocating GenAI observability spend to teams takes a 2-week manual exercise each quarter.

PII leaks into logs because every team rolls its own redactor — a RAG project logs raw retrieved chunks to CloudWatch; one chunk contained an unredacted SSN from a support ticket. Discovered three months later in a SOC2 audit. No central redactor existed to catch it before emit.

Recurring cost queries that never converge — Finance asks 'what did GenAI cost us last month?'. Answering requires emailing six teams, reconciling four token-pricing schemes (prompt caching, batch, on-demand, fine-tuned), and accepting ±30% confidence. The number changes 15% each time it's recomputed because nobody owns the canonical answer.

Trace IDs do not propagate across team boundaries — a customer reports an agent gave wrong info; the agent called a retrieval service owned by a different team; the two systems use different correlation IDs. Reconstruction relies on timestamp guessing. The incident takes 4 hours instead of 15 minutes.

Schema drift makes cross-project dashboards impossible — Project A logs `tokens_in`, Project B logs `prompt_tokens`, Project C logs `input_token_count`. Building a portfolio cost dashboard needs bespoke ETL per project; no team owns it; it never gets built.

solution.approach:
All AI workload observability emission flows through a single enterprise SDK. The event is split into a header (central-owned, schema-controlled) and a payload (project-owned, free-form). Project teams call `sdk.emit(project_id, workload_pattern, target_backend, payload)`.

The SDK validates the call, enriches the header (cost_center, compliance_tier, sdk_version, dollar_cost computed from a central price table, trace correlation IDs, PII pre-scrub), maps the canonical event to the requested backend's schema (Langsmith runs, Datadog LLM Observability spans, etc.), and emits asynchronously.

Direct calls to any observability backend from workload code are prohibited. An escape-hatch `custom_payload` field accepts opaque blobs the team owns end-to-end, so workload-specific shapes do not queue behind central schema changes.
key_benefits: Cross-team trace correlation works — one `trace_id` survives a request that crosses three teams' workloads, so multi-team incidents become reconstructable instead of timestamp-guessed.
Cost attribution is canonical — finance answers 'what did GenAI cost us?' from one price table applied uniformly, with per-project breakdown produced automatically rather than reconciled quarterly.
PII leakage gets caught at one chokepoint — the central pre-scrub runs before any backend emit, regardless of team or backend choice.
Backend swaps become a single SDK release — adding Langfuse or migrating off Datadog does not require N parallel team migrations.
Schema drift is eliminated — `prompt_tokens` means the same thing in every project's traces, so portfolio-level dashboards are buildable.

gates.check: Lint scans the workload codebase for direct calls to observability backends (Datadog `ddtrace` / `datadog.api`, `langsmith.Client`, `langfuse.Langfuse`, AWS `boto3.client('logs')`, raw HTTPS POSTs to observability ingest endpoints) and fails the PR if any are found outside the central SDK's adapter layer. The allow-list of permitted import paths is read from `observability/allowed_imports.yaml`, maintained by the platform team and pinned with the SDK version. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate.

Lint inspects every call to `observability_sdk.emit()` and verifies the required header parameters are supplied with non-empty values: `project_id` (matches the project registry), `workload_pattern` ∈ {`rag`, `llm`, `agent`}, `target_backend` ∈ the SDK's supported-backends enum. Fails on any call missing a required parameter, supplying an unrecognised `workload_pattern`, or naming a `target_backend` not on the supported list. The mandatory-fields contract is read from the SDK package's pinned schema version. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate.

Lint verifies the workload pins the central observability SDK at a version ≥ the current floor declared in `observability/sdk_floor.txt`, maintained by the platform team and bumped at each schema deprecation cutover. Fails if the pinned version is below the floor, preventing deprecated schema versions from reaching production. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate.

explain_prompt.system:
You are a senior software architect explaining the failure mode that GO3B2-01 — Centralised Observability SDK for AI Workloads — prevents, using a single illustrative scenario from a real industry.

THE PRINCIPLE

Every AI workload — every model call, tool invocation, and retrieval query — must emit its observability data (traces, metrics, logs) through a single enterprise SDK rather than calling observability backends directly. The SDK splits the event into a central-owned header (project_id, workload_pattern, target_backend, enrichment fields like cost_center, compliance_tier, dollar_cost, trace correlation IDs, PII pre-scrub) and a project-owned payload. Direct calls from workload code to any observability backend are prohibited; backends are reached only through the SDK's adapter layer. A central platform team owns the canonical schema, the price table, the adapter set, and the PII pre-scrub; project teams own the payload content and the workload-specific context inside it.

THE FAILURE SHAPE

This is a setup-absence failure that becomes visible the moment the enterprise needs to answer a portfolio-level question. Multiple project teams have shipped AI workloads, each picking its own observability backend (one team on Datadog, another on Langfuse, a third self-hosting in CloudWatch Logs), each writing its own emit code, each naming the same field differently (tokens_in vs prompt_tokens vs input_token_count), each writing its own PII redactor or skipping redaction entirely. Locally, every team's logging works — incidents inside a team are debuggable, dashboards inside a backend are useful, on-call engineers can ship fixes. The discipline that nobody built is the central one: there is no canonical event schema, no shared cost attribution, no single chokepoint where PII is filtered, no cross-team trace correlation. Each team has solved its own observability problem; collectively the enterprise has none.

The absence is invisible until a triggering event forces it into view: finance asks what GenAI cost the company last month and the answer requires emailing six teams and reconciling four pricing schemes; a SOC2 audit finds an unredacted SSN inside a CloudWatch log from one team that had no redactor; a customer-reported incident crosses two team boundaries and reconstruction takes hours because trace IDs do not propagate between the teams' systems; a regulator asks for the full reasoning chain behind a particular agent decision and the chain is fragmented across two backends with different schemas. At that moment the enterprise discovers that the cost of unifying observability after the fact — migrating multiple production workloads onto a common SDK, renaming fields, deprecating proprietary integrations, retroactively classifying historical traces by compliance tier — is many times the cost of having built the SDK before the second workload shipped.

The principle's value is forcing the central SDK and canonical schema to exist before the second workload is built, so cost attribution, PII control, and cross-team trace correlation are emergent properties of the system rather than retrofits paid for in audit findings and incident hours.

APPLICABILITY

GO3B2-01 is mandatory for LLM Only, RAG, and Agent workloads. It is nice-to-have for traditional ML workloads, which typically use MLflow or SageMaker Model Monitor and have a different cost-attribution shape. If the chosen Implementation type is ML, open use_case_background with one italicised sentence noting the principle is most acute for LLM-based and agentic workloads and the example below is illustrative of how the same setup-absence failure would manifest inside an ML pipeline.

YOUR TASK

You will be given an Industry and an Implementation type. Write an illustrative example for that combination demonstrating GO3B2-01's failure mode.

OUTPUT CONTRACT

Respond with a single valid JSON object of exactly this shape and nothing else:

{
  "use_case_background": "string — 150–250 words. Describe an enterprise that has multiple AI workloads in production. Be specific about the industry and the implementation type. Name two or three workloads owned by different teams, the integrating systems (with plausible invented names), and the observability backends each team independently chose. The reader should picture a confident enterprise where each individual team's observability is working fine — dashboards exist, on-call engineers can debug their own incidents, leaders know their team's metrics. Crucially: hint at the absence offhandedly — note that the field names differ between teams, that the PII redactor is each team's responsibility, that cost attribution today comes from invoice splits done by finance. Do NOT describe the failure here.",
  "issue_detail": "string — 200–350 words. Describe the moment the absent SDK becomes visible. Walk through the scenario chronologically: name the triggering event (finance asks for portfolio-level cost attribution; an audit finds PII in logs that one team's redactor missed; an incident crosses two teams and reconstruction stalls because trace IDs do not propagate; a regulator asks for the full reasoning chain on a specific decision). Then describe what the enterprise tries to do and where it falls apart — emailing teams, reconciling field names, hand-correlating timestamps across backends. Name roles (the platform engineer trying to build a portfolio dashboard; the privacy officer who has to explain the audit finding; the on-call engineer trying to debug across two team boundaries). Make the consequence concrete — measurable hours lost, an audit finding logged, a regulatory commitment that cannot be met from the existing fragmented logs, a retrofit project that is now multi-quarter. The failure must demonstrate something a central SDK with canonical schema and PII pre-scrub would have caught or prevented — not a generic 'we don't log enough' regression."
}

STYLE RULES

- Concrete over abstract. Name systems, roles, decisions. 'The fraud-triage agent' not 'the AI system'.
- Match the implementation type honestly: RAG retrieves before generating; Agent reasons over multiple steps and calls tools; LLM Only is single-turn or prompt-chained without retrieval or tools; ML is a classical supervised/unsupervised model with no LLM in the loop.
- Match the industry honestly. Use vocabulary the industry actually uses.
- Use specific numbers where they sharpen the story, but signpost invented precision honestly ('about six teams', 'roughly two weeks per quarter').
- Do not invent regulatory specifics. Generic framing only ('the firm's internal compliance review', 'the regulator's quarterly model-risk audit').
- Both sections are prose paragraphs. No bullet lists, no headings, no markdown emphasis other than the optional italicised opening sentence described above.
- Do not include the principle's solution, the fix, or recommendations. Show the problem only.
- Do not write any text outside the JSON object.

standards.u_principle: Data a system captures stays protected — seen by few, kept no longer than it's needed.

references.title: How to Prevent PII Leaks in AI Systems
references.note: Maps PII flowing into traces and log sinks, putting the trace store in scope for GDPR deletion and access control — the read-side access governance and retention this principle enforces on already-emitted traces.

references.title: Prevent Sensitive Data Leakage with LLMs: Essential Strategies
references.note: Argues for WORM/immutable audit-log storage and governed, access-controlled logging of prompts, responses, and callers — the retention-policy and access-policy artefacts this principle mandates.

dependencies.reason: Governs read access and retention on the traces GO3B2-01 emits, keyed on the `compliance_tier` header GO3B2-01 sets; without the emit contract there is no classified trace to govern.

references.question: GENOPS03 — How do you maintain traceability for your models, prompts, and assets?
references.best_practice: GENOPS03-BP02 — Enable tracing for agents and RAG workflows

implementation_step.title: Secure trace data
implementation_step.verbatim_text: Secure trace data. Implement appropriate access controls to verify that only authorized personnel can view trace data. Be mindful of any sensitive information that might be included in traces and handle it according to your organization's security policies.

references.note: AWS step 2 mandates access controls so only authorised personnel can read trace data and that sensitive information in traces be handled per the organisation's security policies. The emission-time slice of this step (PII pre-scrub before any backend write) is absorbed by sibling principle GO3B2-01. This principle concretises the remaining post-emission slice — read-side access governance and retention — and adds three concretisations AWS leaves unspecified: (1) the compliance_tier classification flowing from emit (set in GO3B2-01) to read (this principle) as the keying field that ties the two halves of the audit trail together, (2) the central access policy file + retention policy file as the single source of truth backend IAM bindings and lifecycle rules are derived from, (3) the pre-merge IaC lint that blocks project-local IAM grants on observability backends, preventing the most common bypass. AWS itself nests this step within GENOPS03-BP02 (Traceability) rather than within GENSEC; the placement here matches the AWS source. Option B (defer to a future GENSEC walk under P22 Data Protection or P21 IAM) was considered and rejected on the audit-trail argument: compliance_tier flows from emit to read across the GO3B2 pair, and splitting governance across pillars would fragment the regulator-facing walk.

references.domain: Domain IV: Understanding How to Govern AI Deployment and Use
references.competency: IV.C: Establish Security, Access Control, and Incident Management
references.note: Primary mapping. Read-side access control on observability traces, centrally-governed principal-to-compliance_tier authorisation, retention bounds keyed on compliance_tier, and read-side audit logging directly serve IV.C's access-control and incident-management scope. The sibling pair GO3B2-01 (IV.B primary, IV.C secondary) and GO3B2-02 (IV.C primary, IV.B secondary) covers both competencies from both directions — the centralisation gives IV.B its monitoring substrate, the access governance here gives IV.C its enforcement. Needs side-by-side review against the AIGP IV.C competency definition to promote to verified.

references.domain: Domain IV: Understanding How to Govern AI Deployment and Use
references.competency: IV.B: Implement Ongoing Risk Assessments and Monitoring
references.note: Secondary mapping. Read-side audit logs on regulated- and sensitive-tier trace accesses feed the ongoing-monitoring loop IV.B expects — anomalous read patterns, off-hours access, and access by deprecated principals all become detectable signals against a central audit stream rather than per-backend silences. Needs review against AIGP IV.B to verify.

statement.title: Traces can hold sensitive data, so govern who reads them and how long they live
statement.description:
Prompts and responses captured in observability traces may contain personal or confidential data.

Who can read those traces, and how long they are kept, should be governed centrally rather than left to each workload to decide for itself.

problem.description:
AI observability traces stored across multiple backends contain residual sensitive metadata, business-relevant payload contents, and compliance_tier-classified data — none of which should be readable by every engineer with platform access.

When read-side access controls are set up per project (team-local IAM grants on each backend) and retention is decided ad-hoc by whoever owns the backend, the post-emission half of the audit trail falls apart. A regulator asking 'show me who accessed PII-tagged trace X over the last 90 days, and prove the trace was deleted within the retention window' walks an evidence path that crosses N team boundaries with no central record.

Per-project access controls drift silently: contractors lose access on one backend but retain it on another; former employees' role bindings outlast their employment; cross-team incident reconstructions stall on backend-by-backend access tickets. Retention windows that should be 30 days for sensitive traces end up at 13 months (vendor default) or indefinite (no lifecycle rule) because nobody owns the lifecycle.

The failure surfaces at audit time, when retrofit cost is the highest.
examples: Contractor offboarded from project A; team A's IAM grant revoked, but team B's separate Langsmith read role for cross-team incident support is never touched; the contractor's existing token fetches sensitive traces for 6 weeks before a SOC2 spot-check catches the second grant.

Retention drift across backends — project A keeps Datadog spans at 30 days (vendor default), project B keeps Langsmith runs at 13 months (vendor default), project C never set a CloudWatch log-group retention rule so traces accumulate indefinitely; a GDPR DSAR for a customer whose production data was deleted 2 years ago turns up 4-year-old trace references in project C.

Cross-team incident reconstruction blocked at backend boundary — SRE investigating a cascading agent failure has read on team X's traces (Datadog) but not team Y's (Langfuse); the IAM grant ticket queues through team Y's manual approval for 3 days; by the time it lands, the live trace data in team X has rolled past the 30-day retention window so end-to-end correlation is no longer possible.

Read-side audit absent — an external auditor asks 'list everyone who read regulated-tier traces in Q1'; each project team's backend defaulted access logging to off; the answer is 'we don't know, and we can't reconstruct it.' Cited as a SOC2 deficiency.

compliance_tier honoured at emit, ignored at storage — GO3B2-01 stamps compliance_tier=regulated on a healthcare workload's traces, but the backend bucket has a single retention policy covering all tiers at 13 months, well beyond the 30-day cap the workload's compliance team had committed to in the SOC2 narrative; discovered during the SOC2 audit, requiring narrative remediation.

solution.approach:
A central access policy file (`observability/access_policy.yaml`) declares, per compliance_tier and per project, the principals (roles, groups, contractors) authorised to read traces, with optional region scoping.

A central retention policy file (`observability/retention_policy.yaml`) maps each compliance_tier to a maximum retention window, and central IaC owned by the platform team translates the policy into backend-specific lifecycle rules (S3 lifecycle, Datadog retention API, Langsmith TTL, CloudWatch log-group retention).

Project workloads declare their `compliance_tier` in the existing `observability/config.yaml` (introduced by sibling principle GO3B2-01) and may not grant IAM read on observability backend resources directly — a pre-merge IaC lint blocks any project-local grant against the central observability ARN allow-list.

Read-side audit logs from every backend feed a central security team queue; access requests for new backends or compliance_tier reclassifications go through a documented central security review with a 1-week SLA.
key_benefits: Audit trail is centrally walkable — 'who accessed trace X under what compliance_tier and when' answers from a single query against the central policy plus the read-audit log, instead of timestamp-reconciling across N team backends.

Retention drift is impossible by construction — compliance_tier is set at emit (GO3B2-01); the central retention policy translates that tier into backend lifecycle rules automatically; project teams cannot accidentally over-retain because they don't own the rules.
Contractor or role offboarding propagates from one place — removing a principal from the central policy file re-syncs every backend; no per-team revocation tickets, no orphan grants.

Cross-team incident reconstruction works because the access policy expresses incident-response roles centrally — SRE on-call gets read scoped to an active incident's `trace_id` family across all backends, no per-team IAM friction.
Read-side governance reuses the compliance_tier that already flows from emit (GO3B2-01) — no duplicated classification, no fragmenting of the post-emission audit trail across pillars or focus areas.

gates.check: On any PR, `observability/config.yaml` at the workload repo root must declare `compliance_tier` ∈ {regulated, sensitive, standard}, AND the declared value must match the workload's compliance_tier in the central project registry. Pre-merge lint fails on missing field, unrecognised value, or mismatch against the registry. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

On any PR, lint scans the workload's infrastructure-as-code (Terraform, CloudFormation, Pulumi, k8s manifests, or equivalent) for direct IAM grants on observability backend resources — AWS IAM policies or role attachments referencing `arn:aws:s3:::observability-*`, `arn:aws:logs:*:*:log-group:/aws/observability/*`, or equivalent; Datadog / Langsmith / Langfuse API tokens with read scope on observability orgs; cross-account observability role assumptions. Any grant present outside the central policy file fails the PR. The allow-list of central-managed observability resources lives at `observability/iam_managed_by_central.yaml`, maintained by the platform team and pinned with the central policy package version. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

Lint verifies the workload pins the central observability policy package at a version ≥ the floor declared in `observability/policy_floor.txt`, maintained by the platform team and bumped at each access-policy or retention-schema cutover. Fails if the pinned version is below the floor, preventing deprecated access-policy schemas from reaching production. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

Framework mappings anchor against AWS step 2 verbatim (verified) and AIGP IV.C primary / IV.B secondary (unverified) — the GO3B2 pair now covers IV.B and IV.C from both directions (GO3B2-01 IV.B/IV.C; GO3B2-02 IV.C/IV.B). impact_level High parallel to GO3B2-01; Critical considered and rejected to keep parity with the broader catalogue's conservative bar on Critical. explain_prompt compiled manually with audit_time_discovery failure shape — accumulated access-control and retention drift surfacing at a discrete audit / DSAR / incident-reconstruction moment, distinguished from GO3B2-01's setup_absence shape because the SDK in GO3B2-01 was never built but the read-side controls in GO3B2-02 exist per-team and drift silently.
