explain_prompt.system:
You are a senior software architect explaining the failure mode that GO3B2-02 — Govern read access and retention on AI observability traces through a centrally-owned policy — prevents, using a single illustrative scenario from a real industry.

THE PRINCIPLE

All AI observability traces emitted via the central SDK (sibling principle GO3B2-01) are governed at read time by a centrally-owned access policy file keyed on the trace's compliance_tier (regulated / sensitive / standard), declared as code in the platform repository. A central retention policy file maps each compliance_tier to a maximum retention window and is enforced through backend lifecycle rules generated by platform-team-owned IaC. Project workloads declare their compliance_tier in their observability config but may not grant IAM read on observability backend resources directly — a pre-merge IaC lint blocks any project-local grant against the central allow-list. Every read of regulated- or sensitive-tier traces is captured in a read-side audit log fed into a central security queue. The platform/security team owns the policy files, the lifecycle IaC, the read-audit pipeline, and a backend-access review workflow with a one-week SLA.

THE FAILURE SHAPE

This is an audit-time-discovery failure that accumulates silently across team boundaries and surfaces at a discrete triggering moment with disproportionate retrofit cost. Multiple project teams have AI workloads emitting traces (the SDK from GO3B2-01 is in place — traces are correctly emitted, tagged with compliance_tier, and PII-pre-scrubbed). The fragmentation is on the read side. Each team configured its own IAM grants on its own backend at workload onboarding. Each team accepted the vendor default for retention (30 days on Datadog, 13 months on Langsmith, indefinite on CloudWatch if no log-group retention rule was set). Each team's backend defaulted read-access logging to off. Locally, each team's setup looked fine at onboarding: the people who needed access had it, retention was 'whatever the vendor gave us,' and nobody asked about read audit logs because there was no incident to investigate.

The drift accumulates invisibly over months or years. Contractors get added to incident-response roles on backend A but never to the corresponding role on backend B; when they offboard, backend A revokes them but backend B's role still resolves to the trace bucket. Engineers move teams and their old grants outlive their need-to-know. Backend retention quietly carries traces well past the compliance_tier window the workload's onboarding compliance review committed to. Nobody is doing anything wrong on any single PR — the system is drifting because nobody owns the read side centrally.

The failure surfaces at a discrete triggering moment: an external auditor asks 'list everyone who read regulated-tier traces last quarter' and the answer is 'we cannot reconstruct that'; a GDPR DSAR for a customer whose production data was deleted two years ago turns up four-year-old traces in a backend with no lifecycle rule; a cross-team incident requires a trace reconstruction the SRE on-call cannot perform because the IAM grant ticket takes three days and by the time it lands the live data has rolled past one team's retention window; a contractor offboarded six weeks ago is found in a SOC2 spot-check to have continued read access through a stale grant on a second backend. In every case the principle's value is forcing the read side to be governed centrally before the second backend or the second team is onboarded, so contractor offboarding, retention bounds, read-audit coverage, and cross-team incident-response access are emergent properties of one policy file rather than drift that surfaces in a SOC2 finding or a regulator's question.

APPLICABILITY

GO3B2-02 is mandatory for LLM Only, RAG, and Agent workloads (wherever GO3B2-01 emits). It is nice-to-have for traditional ML workloads. If the chosen Implementation type is ML, open use_case_background with one italicised sentence noting the principle is most acute for LLM-based and agentic workloads where rich trace data lands in observability backends, and the example below is illustrative of how the same audit-time-discovery failure would manifest inside an ML model-monitoring pipeline.

YOUR TASK

You will be given an Industry and an Implementation type. Write an illustrative example for that combination demonstrating GO3B2-02's failure mode.

OUTPUT CONTRACT

Respond with a single valid JSON object of exactly this shape and nothing else:

{
  "use_case_background": "string — 150–250 words. Describe an enterprise that has multiple AI workloads in production, each emitting traces through the central SDK from GO3B2-01 (do not labour this — assume the reader knows the SDK is in place). Be specific about the industry and the implementation type. Name two or three workloads owned by different teams, the integrating systems (with plausible invented names), and the observability backends each team's traces land in (Datadog, Langsmith, Langfuse, CloudWatch, S3 archive — any plausible mix). Crucially: hint at the drift offhandedly — note that each team configured its own IAM grants at onboarding, that retention is 'whatever the vendor defaults to,' that read-access logging was never explicitly turned on, that contractors and team-movers have accumulated incidental access nobody has audited. The reader should picture a confident enterprise where emission is solved and read-side governance has never been a board-room conversation. Do NOT describe the failure here.",
  "issue_detail": "string — 200–350 words. Describe the discrete triggering moment when the accumulated drift surfaces. Walk through the scenario chronologically: name the trigger (an external auditor asks 'who read regulated-tier traces last quarter'; a GDPR DSAR for a deleted customer turns up multi-year-old trace references in a backend with no retention rule; a cross-team incident reconstruction stalls on IAM tickets while live data rolls past retention; a SOC2 spot-check finds an offboarded contractor still holding read access on a second backend; a compliance officer discovers regulated traces are being retained 13 months when the SOC2 narrative committed to 30 days). Then describe what the enterprise tries to do and where it falls apart — pulling backend-by-backend access reports, reconciling IAM grants across vendors with different role models, reconstructing read history from logs that were never turned on. Name roles (the security engineer compiling the audit response; the privacy officer fielding the DSAR; the SRE on-call blocked from cross-team traces; the compliance officer realising the retention narrative does not match reality). Make the consequence concrete — an audit finding logged, a DSAR commitment that cannot be met, an incident reconstruction abandoned, a multi-quarter retrofit project to centralise access governance after the fact. The failure must demonstrate something a central access policy plus centrally-generated retention lifecycle rules plus read-side audit logging would have caught or prevented — not a generic 'we have too many IAM roles' problem."
}

STYLE RULES

- Concrete over abstract. Name systems, roles, decisions. 'The fraud-triage agent's traces in Langsmith' not 'the AI system's logs'.
- Match the implementation type honestly: RAG retrieves before generating; Agent reasons over multiple steps and calls tools; LLM Only is single-turn or prompt-chained without retrieval or tools; ML is a classical supervised/unsupervised model with no LLM in the loop.
- Match the industry honestly. Use vocabulary the industry actually uses.
- Use specific numbers where they sharpen the story, but signpost invented precision honestly ('about six teams', 'roughly four years of accumulated traces').
- Do not invent regulatory specifics. Generic framing only ('the firm's internal compliance review', 'an external auditor', 'a data-subject access request', 'a quarterly security audit').
- Both sections are prose paragraphs. No bullet lists, no headings, no markdown emphasis other than the optional italicised opening sentence described above.
- Do not include the principle's solution, the fix, or recommendations. Show the problem only.
- Do not write any text outside the JSON object.

standards.u_principle: What a system costs is a decision someone made on purpose, not a default no one questioned.

references.title: GPT-5.4 vs Mini vs Smaller Models — Cost and Performance
references.note: Shows teams default to a premium model when a smaller one clears the quality bar, and recommends escalating only when evaluation proves the cheaper model fails — the right-sizing decision this principle requires recorded as an ADR.

references.question: GENCOST01 — How do you select the appropriate model to optimize costs?
references.best_practice: GENCOST01-BP01 — Right-size model selection to optimize inference costs

implementation_step.title: Select the most cost-efficient model based on the prioritized cost dimensions (like hosting paradigm, model size, or token cost)
implementation_step.verbatim_text: Select the most cost-efficient model based on the prioritized cost dimensions (like hosting paradigm, model size, or token cost).

references.note: AWS step 3 calls for the model selection to be made cost-aware against the prioritized cost dimensions (hosting paradigm, model size, token cost). AWS leaves unspecified: where the decision record lives, what shape it takes, what content elements it must capture, and how it is enforced as a versioned artefact in CI. This principle concretises all four. It also absorbs the substance of GENCOST01-BP01 steps 1 (Identify minimum performance requirements) and 2 (Determine models meeting the bar) as content elements WITHIN the decision record — those AWS steps are not_promoted as standalone principles in this catalogue (see lens_mapping.md GENCOST01-BP01 ledger).

references.domain: Domain III: Understanding How to Govern AI Development
references.competency: III.B: Govern the AI Model Lifecycle (Design & Build)
references.note: Provisional mapping based on subject-matter proximity — model-selection decision records are a design-and-build governance artefact, aligning with III.B's scope (same domain GO1B1-03 maps to). Needs a side-by-side review against the AIGP III.B competency definition to promote to verified.

statement.title: Choose a model deliberately, and record why
statement.description:
Model choice should be a reasoned decision against stated requirements and cost, not a default taken unquestioned.

The reasoning should be written down so the choice can be revisited as requirements and the model landscape change.

problem.description:
Without a versioned model-selection decision record in the workload repository, the choice of foundation model lives in tribal knowledge — the senior engineer who set the workload up, a Slack thread, a notebook that was never committed.

The failure surfaces not at selection time (the workload runs fine) but at the first exposure event that demands defence of the choice: finance flagging the bill, the original architect leaving, a cheaper model arriving, an audit asking for justification.

At that point, the team cannot reconstruct what minimum bar was set, what alternatives were evaluated, or what cost-vs-quality trade-off was accepted — every comparison becomes a green-field decision instead of a revisitable one.

Examples below are illustrative — synthesised scenarios; specific numbers are not from cited evidence.
examples: A team picks a frontier model for an insurance underwriting agent because the original architect prefers it; six months later finance flags the workload at roughly $80K/month and asks if a smaller model would suffice; nobody can produce the original minimum performance bar against which the choice was made, so the comparison is run from scratch.

A team uses a frontier model for a customer-service workflow that turns out to need only sentiment classification — a $0.30-per-call workload a 7B model could handle for fractions of a cent. The decision happened in a Slack thread three quarters ago and the cost ratio was never reconciled until volume scaled.

An external auditor asks the team to justify the model choice for a regulated decisioning workload; the original architect has moved teams; the new owner can produce no requirements spec, no evaluated alternatives, no decision rationale; the audit logs "model selection not documented" as a remediation item.

A new model class is released and the platform team needs to evaluate cost-vs-quality migration across ten workloads; for none of them can they find the original selection rationale, so every migration becomes a green-field decision rather than an update to a known prior choice.

A workload is on a self-hosted 70B model at ~$250/day uptime; a managed alternative would be ~$80/day at current token volume; the team cannot find who originally decided self-hosting was right or what assumptions (volume, latency, privacy) drove the call, so they cannot evaluate whether those assumptions still hold.

key_benefits: Foundational dependency for periodic re-evaluation (GC1B1-02) — the re-evaluation principle cannot fire usefully against a workload whose original selection rationale is missing; the decision record is the baseline that re-evaluation compares forward from.
Production knowledge survives team changes; the decision lives in the workload repository, not in any individual's head, Slack thread, or local notebook.

Cost-aware rationale tied to the three prioritized cost dimensions makes the choice defensible to finance and procurement at any review moment — the bill is no longer surprising because the cost basis is documented from day one.

The decision record absorbs the substance of AWS steps 1 (requirements) and 2 (candidates evaluated) as content elements within a single artefact, eliminating the proliferation of separate requirements specs and shortlist documents that would otherwise drift apart.

gates.check: On any PR, the workload repository must contain a versioned model-selection decision record (convention: `model-selection/decision.md` or equivalent), with all four required content elements present: minimum performance requirements considered, candidate models evaluated, chosen model, and cost-aware rationale referencing the prioritized cost dimensions (hosting paradigm, model size, token cost). Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

On any PR that adds, changes, or removes a foundation-model reference under `src/` (model identifier, vendor, hosting paradigm), the PR must also modify `model-selection/decision.md` OR include a linked ADR under `docs/adrs/` justifying why no decision-record update is required. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

explain_prompt.system:
You are a senior software architect explaining the failure mode that GC1B1-01 — Maintain a versioned model-selection decision record in the workload repository declaring requirements, candidate models, chosen model, and cost-aware rationale — prevents, using a single illustrative scenario from a real industry.

THE PRINCIPLE

Every foundation-model selection (initial choice or replacement) must be captured as a versioned decision record in the workload repository, naming the minimum performance requirements the model must meet, the candidate models evaluated against those requirements, the chosen model, and the cost-aware rationale tying the choice to the prioritized cost dimensions (hosting paradigm, model size, token cost). The record is enforced as a versioned artefact in CI: any PR that adds or changes a model reference must update it.

THE FAILURE SHAPE

This is a setup-absence failure that becomes visible at the first exposure event that demands defence of the model choice. The team has a workload in production using a foundation model the original architect picked. The choice was made informally — a Slack thread, a notebook, a hallway conversation — and never committed as a structured decision record. The workload runs; the choice is not actively wrong. The absence is invisible until a triggering event forces it into view: finance flags the workload's bill and asks if a smaller model would suffice; a cheaper model class is released and procurement wants a cost-vs-quality comparison; an auditor (internal compliance, external regulator, or platform-team review) asks the team to justify the choice; the original architect leaves and the new owner inherits a model whose selection rationale lives in nobody's head. At that moment the team cannot reconstruct what minimum bar was set, what alternatives were evaluated, or what trade-off was accepted. Every comparison becomes a green-field decision rather than a revisitable one. The consequence is a forced choice: stall the workload while the missing record is reconstructed from memory, defend the choice with rationale invented after the fact, or migrate without proper comparison and inherit the cost-of-being-wrong at production scale.

The principle's value is forcing the selection to be a first-class versioned artefact from day one — committed, with the four content elements, wired to CI so any model change touches it. Without it, the cost basis of every model decision lives in tribal knowledge and breaks the moment that knowledge moves on.

APPLICABILITY

GC1B1-01 is mandatory for LLM, RAG, and agentic workloads where the model is a foundation-model-as-a-service decision with real cost stakes. It is nice_to_have for classical ML workloads where model selection follows well-established statistical discipline. If the chosen Implementation type is not one of the mandatory three (or if it is ML), open use_case_background with one italicised sentence noting the principle is most acute for foundation-model selection at production cost scale, and that the example below is illustrative of how the same setup-absence failure would manifest in the chosen pattern.

YOUR TASK

You will be given an Industry and an Implementation type. Write an illustrative example for that combination demonstrating GC1B1-01's failure mode.

OUTPUT CONTRACT

Respond with a single valid JSON object of exactly this shape and nothing else:

{
  "use_case_background": "string — 150–250 words. Describe a workload that is in production with a chosen foundation model. Be specific about the industry, the implementation type, the team, the workload's role, and the integrating systems (with plausible invented names). Name the model that was chosen (use a plausible model class — frontier vs mid-tier vs small-tier — without fabricating specific commercial model names where avoidable). Crucially: name how the choice was made — the informal mechanism (a Slack thread, a notebook the original architect ran on their laptop, a hallway agreement at workload kick-off). The reader should picture a confident team unaware that the selection record does not survive contact with change. Do NOT describe the failure here.",
  "issue_detail": "string — 200–350 words. Describe the moment the missing selection record becomes a problem. Walk through chronologically: name the triggering event (finance escalation on the bill; the original architect leaving; a cheaper model class released; a procurement review; an internal compliance audit; a quarterly model-risk review). Then describe what the team tries to do and where it falls apart — they cannot produce the original requirements bar, cannot list the alternatives that were considered, cannot defend the cost-vs-quality trade-off that justified the chosen paradigm (managed vs self-hosted; larger vs smaller model). Make the consequence concrete: a forced green-field comparison that stalls a release; a migration that ships without proper cost comparison; a compliance finding that opens a remediation backlog; finance pulling budget while the selection rationale is reconstructed. The failure must demonstrate something a committed, four-element decision record with CI coupling would have caught or prevented — not a generic 'the model performed badly' regression."
}

STYLE RULES

- Concrete over abstract. Name systems, roles, decisions. 'The claims-triage workload' not 'the AI system'.
- Match the implementation type honestly: RAG retrieves before generating; Agent reasons over multiple steps and calls tools; LLM Only is single-turn or prompt-chained without retrieval or tools; ML is classical with no LLM in the loop.
- Match the industry honestly. Use vocabulary the industry actually uses.
- Use specific numbers where they sharpen the story, but signpost invented precision honestly ('roughly $80K/month', 'about a fortnight before the comparison closed').
- Do not invent regulatory specifics. Generic framing only ('the firm's internal compliance review', 'the platform team's quarterly cost review').
- Both sections are prose paragraphs. No bullet lists, no headings, no markdown emphasis other than the optional italicised opening sentence described above.
- Do not include the principle's solution, the fix, or recommendations. Show the problem only.
- Do not write any text outside the JSON object.

standards.u_principle: A system is built to the work it actually does, not the largest size that felt safe.

references.title: Enterprise GPU utilization: why 95% of AI infrastructure spend is wasted
references.note: Documents inference GPUs sitting at 20–40% utilization with idle capacity billed at full rate and no signal raised — the overprovisioning this principle's right-sizing baseline and utilization alarms catch.

references.title: AI Cloud Cost Optimization: GPU Spending Guide 2026
references.note: Reports the average AI team wastes 35–60% of GPU cloud spend, with right-sizing GPU type and scale-to-zero as the fixes — the hosting-infrastructure discipline this principle enforces.

dependencies.reason: The serving-paradigm choice GC1B1-01 records determines whether there is hosting infrastructure to right-size; GC2B2-01 gates on `infra/` independently of that ADR.

references.question: GENCOST02 — How do you select a cost-effective pricing model (for example, provisioned, on-demand, hosted, or batch)?
references.best_practice: GENCOST02-BP02 — Optimize resource consumption to minimize hosting costs

implementation_step.title: Optimize the hosting infrastructure in accordance with the workload's demands, and select the most cost optimized infrastructure that meets performance requirements
implementation_step.verbatim_text: Optimize the hosting infrastructure in accordance with the workload's demands, and select the most cost optimized infrastructure that meets performance requirements.

references.note: AWS step 4 calls for the hosting infrastructure to be right-sized to the workload's demand profile with the most cost-optimized configuration that meets performance requirements. AWS leaves unspecified: where the right-sizing baseline lives, where utilization alarms are declared, how the declared baseline is enforced against actual deployed infrastructure (the most common bypass), and how reserved-capacity / scheduling / quantization decisions are made auditable. This principle concretises all four — `infra/right-sizing/config.yaml` as the baseline artefact, `infra/monitoring/utilization-alarms.yaml` as the alarms artefact, two pre-merge CI gates (existence + content; code-vs-record consistency lint comparing IaC against the YAML and verifying deployed alarms), and runtime alarm enforcement post-deploy. The principle absorbs the substance of GENCOST02-BP02 steps 1 (demand identification — content WITHIN the YAML's utilization_targets), 2 (deploy on acceptable infrastructure — pre-condition), and 3 (establish demand profile — observation work product absorbed by the right-sizing config's baseline). AWS's implementation guidance is heavily vendor-menu (SageMaker AI HyperPod, EKS, Slurm, EC2 Reserved Instances, Inference Recommender, quantization techniques) but the step wording itself is a generic mandate; vendor specifics belong here in framework_mappings notes rather than in the principle's solution.approach.

references.domain: Domain III: Understanding How to Govern AI Development
references.competency: III.B: Govern the AI Model Lifecycle (Design & Build)
references.note: Provisional mapping based on subject-matter proximity — hosting-infrastructure right-sizing is a build-and-design discipline against the deployed workload, aligning with III.B's lifecycle scope. Same anchor as GC1B1-01 and GO1B1-03. Needs a side-by-side review against the AIGP III.B competency definition to promote to verified.

statement.title: Size AI infrastructure to real demand, not to the safe maximum
statement.description:
Over-provisioned hosting quietly wastes large amounts of spend, because the safe default is almost always bigger than the workload needs.

Infrastructure should be matched to the workload's actual demand, and sustained under-use should be visible rather than left to drift unnoticed.

problem.description:
Without utilization alarms committed as code and a right-sizing baseline tied to the workload's observed demand profile, oversized hosting infrastructure runs invisibly — the workload performs correctly, just expensively.

No per-PR CI gate catches this because nothing in the PR is wrong; the wrong choice was made at first deploy or has drifted since. The only signal is the monthly bill, which arrives 30 days late, lands with finance not engineering, and triggers an audit that takes weeks to land an optimization PR. Meanwhile the workload continues paying the premium.

Examples below are illustrative — synthesised scenarios; specific numbers are not from cited evidence.
examples: Self-hosted Llama runs on `ml.g5.12xlarge` sized for projected peak; actual production peaks at 18% GPU utilization; no alarm declared in `infra/monitoring/`; engineering only notices when finance flags the workload during quarterly cost review six months in.

Inference endpoint runs 24/7 on a workload whose traffic is bounded to UK business hours; no scheduled-scaling or shutdown policy committed; the workload spends 70% of compute time idle, with no signal raised because the workload itself is healthy.
Workload runs 18 months past first deploy with no Reserved Instance or Savings Plan coverage; on-demand premium accumulates; no alarm fires against `RI coverage < workload-age threshold`.
Self-hosted model deployed at FP16 on a model class that supports INT8 quantization with negligible quality impact; quantization is never applied because no alarm declares the eligibility check.

Reserved-capacity purchase sized to projected demand 18 months ago; actual usage has stayed at 40% of reserved; no alarm declares a coverage-vs-usage check; the reserved overspend is invisible because RIs look like a cost optimization on the surface.

solution.approach:
Commit a hosting-infrastructure right-sizing config at a known path in the workload repository (convention `infra/right-sizing/config.yaml`), declaring:

- the right-sizing baseline — target utilization band per resource (GPU / CPU / memory), instance ceiling, scheduling windows where the workload may shut down, and reserved-capacity coverage target as a function of workload age;
- utilization alarms as code (convention `infra/monitoring/utilization-alarms.yaml`), each alarm naming the resource, the metric, the threshold, the evaluation window, and the alert routing.

Wire two pre-merge CI gates:

- an existence + content-completeness gate on both files;
- a code-vs-record consistency lint that parses the workload's IaC (Terraform, CloudFormation, k8s manifests, docker-compose — whichever the workload uses) and compares declared GPU instance count, GPU type, model identifier, and quantization precision against the YAML's corresponding fields, AND verifies that every alarm declared in the YAML is actually deployed via the same IaC as an active monitoring resource.

The PR fails if any mismatch is present without an accompanying ADR. The alarms themselves do the runtime enforcement once deployed — they fire when actual production utilization drifts outside the declared band, when reserved-capacity coverage expires, or when scheduled shutdown windows are violated.
key_benefits: Catches the silent-waste failure mode that monthly bills surface 30 days late and aggregate cost reports cannot attribute — alarms fire at the workload level the moment utilization drifts outside the declared band, not at billing close.

Builds on GC1B1-01 without overlap: GC1B1-01 records the paradigm/model choice gated on `src/` changes; GC2B2-01 enforces within-paradigm right-sizing gated on `infra/` changes. Different file scope, different lifecycle moment, complementary coverage.

Code-vs-record consistency lint (Gate 2) closes the bypass loophole that pure documentation-discipline principles cannot — declared baseline is binding against deployed infrastructure, not honour-system, so the YAML cannot quietly drift from reality.

Workload-age-anchored reserved-capacity coverage check forces the RI / Savings Plan decision rather than leaving it as an indefinite open item; long-running workloads with no commitment surface as alarm-eligible.

Workload-agnostic across in-scope serving paradigms — declare baseline, declare alarms, gate on infra-config drift — applies whether self-hosted on managed inference endpoints, self-hosted on unmanaged clusters, or on committed-capacity managed APIs (where the commitment IS a right-sizing decision); only the specific resources and thresholds differ.

gates.check: On any PR, `infra/right-sizing/config.yaml` and `infra/monitoring/utilization-alarms.yaml` must both exist and be content-complete. The right-sizing config must declare: `resources.gpu` (type, count, ceiling), `utilization_targets` (compute and memory bands per session state), `scheduling` (active_windows, idle_policy with auto_suspend_after_idle and full_shutdown_after_idle, weekly_max_active_hours), and `model` (identifier, precision, precision_review_due). The alarms config must declare at minimum a gpu_compute_oversized alarm, an idle_shutdown_violated alarm, and a quantization_review_overdue alarm. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

On any PR, a lint must parse the workload's infrastructure-as-code (Terraform, CloudFormation, k8s manifests, docker-compose, or equivalent) and verify two things: (a) the declared hosting infrastructure matches `infra/right-sizing/config.yaml` — actual GPU instance count must be ≤ `resources.gpu.ceiling`; actual GPU type must equal `resources.gpu.type`; actual served model identifier must equal `model.identifier`; actual quantization precision must equal `model.precision`; (b) every alarm declared in `infra/monitoring/utilization-alarms.yaml` must be deployed via the same IaC as an active monitoring resource (CloudWatch alarm, Prometheus rule, or equivalent depending on the workload's monitoring stack). The lint fails the PR if any mismatch is present without an accompanying ADR under `docs/adrs/` justifying the temporary divergence. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

changes.author: Dheeraj
changes.summary: Initial authoring against AWS GENCOST02-BP02 implementation step 4 (Optimize the hosting infrastructure in accordance with the workload's demands, and select the most cost optimized infrastructure that meets performance requirements). Seventh principle in the catalogue; second in the GENCOST pillar; first to use the new schema v1.9 `serving_paradigm` field with a narrowed scope (omitting api_on_demand since pure-API workloads have no infrastructure to right-size). Decomposition: GENCOST02-BP01 closed 0 promoted (all four steps absorbed by GC1B1-01 which already covers hosting-paradigm choice in its model-selection ADR); GENCOST02-BP02 decomposition under the step_promotion rubric: steps 1/2/3 not_promoted (decision-process advice / pre-condition / observation work products); step 4 promoted as this principle. Statement: 'Right-size hosting infrastructure to the workload's demand profile and commit utilization alarms as code' — derives from AWS step 4's operative verbs (Optimize, select) rather than the documentation-shaped 'maintain a decision record' interpretation initially considered. Two pre_merge CI gates, both required status checks on the integration branch: (1) existence + content-completeness on right-sizing config + utilization alarms YAMLs; (2) code-vs-record consistency lint comparing IaC against the YAML baselines AND verifying deployed alarm presence — closes the documentation-discipline bypass that GC1B1-01 acknowledged but deliberately left open. impact_level Medium (matches AWS BP rating; cost failures recoverable via re-optimization, parallel with GC1B1-01's principled outlier from the GO1B1 family's High). applicability { llm/rag/agentic: mandatory, ml: nice_to_have } matching GC1B1-01's broad shape. serving_paradigm { self_hosted_managed: mandatory, self_hosted_unmanaged: mandatory, api_provisioned: nice_to_have } — api_on_demand omitted (no infrastructure to right-size); api_provisioned nice_to_have because commitment sizing IS a right-sizing decision but provider handles underlying instance optimization. maturity_level scaling — three reasons: (1) Gate 2's code-vs-record consistency lint is real platform-team engineering (IaC parser, cross-reference logic, cloud-API verification of deployed alarms), not first-project capability; (2) the principle only applies to self-hosted or committed-managed workloads per serving_paradigm, and self-hosting is rarely a first AI project choice — teams typically start on API on-demand and migrate to self-hosted only at scaling tier when cost or data-residency pressures arrive; (3) runtime alarm enforcement needs a deployed instance to fire against, same logic that put GO1B1-04 (drift monitor) at scaling. The day-1-declarability of the YAMLs is necessary but not sufficient — maturity_level captures when enforcement is meaningful. AIGP cross-reference set to Domain III.B (Govern the AI Model Lifecycle — Design & Build) with mapping_state: unverified pending side-by-side competency review. See decisions.md 2026-05-29 entries for the schema v1.9 serving_paradigm addition and the GENCOST02 walkthrough.

changes.author: Dheeraj
changes.summary: PATCH. maturity_level corrected scaling → mature (hardcoded override). Reasoning: Gate 2's code-vs-record consistency lint plus deployed-alarm verification against cloud APIs is genuine portfolio-tier engineering — IaC parser, cross-workload monitoring stack assumption, platform-team capability — not the per-workload capability scaling-tier orgs typically have. explain_prompt.system:
You are a senior software architect explaining the failure mode that GC2B2-01 — Right-size hosting infrastructure to the workload's demand profile and commit utilization alarms as code — prevents, using a single illustrative scenario from a real industry.

THE PRINCIPLE

Every workload that self-hosts a foundation model for inference (or runs on committed managed capacity such as Bedrock Provisioned Throughput) must declare a versioned right-sizing baseline and a versioned set of utilization alarms in the workload repository, both as code. The baseline names the target utilization band per resource (GPU / CPU / memory), instance ceiling, scheduling and idle policy, reserved-capacity coverage target as a function of workload age, and the model identifier and quantization precision. The alarms fire when production utilization, scheduling discipline, reserved-capacity coverage, or quantization-review cadence drift from the declared baseline. CI gates enforce: (a) both YAMLs exist and are content-complete; (b) the actual deployed infrastructure-as-code matches the YAML — declared GPU count / type / model / precision must equal what IaC deploys, and every declared alarm must be deployed as an active monitoring resource.

THE FAILURE SHAPE

This is a setup-absence failure that becomes visible at the first exposure event that demands defence of the hosting cost. The team has a workload running in production on self-hosted infrastructure. The original architect picked an instance size when first deploying — informed by projected peak load, rough back-of-envelope estimates, or simply 'this is what the spike chart said we'd need.' The workload runs fine. The instance is oversized for the actual production load; the workload runs 24/7 even though traffic only hits business hours; no Reserved Instance was ever purchased because the team meant to evaluate it later; the model was deployed at FP16 even though the model class supports INT8 with negligible quality loss. None of these are visible from inside the workload — the workload is healthy. The bill is the only signal, and the bill is 30 days late, lands with finance not engineering, and gets absorbed as 'AI infrastructure cost.'

The absence becomes visible at a triggering event: a quarterly cost review surfaces the workload as a top-N spend item; an internal audit asks why the workload runs on the instance class it does; the original architect leaves and the new owner inherits an instance whose sizing rationale lives in nobody's head; a procurement review asks if Reserved Instances would help and nobody can produce the utilization data to make the call; a release notes scan flags that INT8 quantization is widely supported now and the team has not evaluated it. At that moment the team cannot reconstruct what utilization band was assumed, what scheduling policy was supposed to be in place, what reserved-capacity coverage was intended. Every optimization becomes a green-field investigation rather than an alarm-driven micro-decision. The consequence is months of accumulated overspend that compounds with each quarter the discipline does not exist.

The principle's value is forcing the right-sizing baseline AND its enforcement to be first-class versioned artefacts from day one — committed, with explicit utilization bands and scheduling policies and reserved-capacity targets, AND with a CI gate that verifies actual deployed IaC matches the declaration. Without both, the discipline is honour-system: the YAML can exist while the deployed infrastructure quietly drifts past it.

APPLICABILITY

GC2B2-01 is mandatory for workloads self-hosted on managed infrastructure (SageMaker AI endpoints, Vertex AI endpoints, Azure ML managed endpoints) or on unmanaged infrastructure (EC2 + vLLM, EKS / Slurm / HyperPod clusters, on-prem GPUs), and nice_to_have for workloads on committed managed-API capacity (Bedrock Provisioned Throughput, Azure OpenAI PTUs) where the commitment-sizing decision IS a right-sizing decision but the provider handles the underlying instance optimization. The principle does not apply at all to pure-API on-demand workloads (OpenAI, Anthropic, Bedrock on-demand) — there is no hosting infrastructure to right-size; cost optimisation in that paradigm is at the prompt layer (GENCOST03). If the chosen Implementation type's typical serving paradigm is api_on_demand, open use_case_background with one italicised sentence noting GC2B2-01 does not apply to pure-API workloads and that the example below is illustrative of how the same setup-absence failure would manifest if the team self-hosted instead.

YOUR TASK

You will be given an Industry and an Implementation type. Write an illustrative example for that combination demonstrating GC2B2-01's failure mode.

OUTPUT CONTRACT

Respond with a single valid JSON object of exactly this shape and nothing else:

{
  "use_case_background": "string — 150–250 words. Describe a workload self-hosted (or on committed managed capacity) in production. Be specific about the industry, the implementation type, the team, the workload's role, and the integrating systems (with plausible invented names). Name the foundation model in use (use a plausible open-weight model class — Llama / Mistral / Qwen — or a committed-managed equivalent) and the hosting choice (an instance size for self-hosted, a committed-throughput unit for managed). Crucially: name how the instance/commitment was sized — the informal mechanism (the original architect's back-of-envelope, the projected peak load from launch planning, a copy of another workload's sizing). The reader should picture a confident team unaware that the sizing rationale and the enforcement around it do not survive contact with production reality. Do NOT describe the failure here.",
  "issue_detail": "string — 200–350 words. Describe the moment the missing right-sizing baseline and alarms become a problem. Walk through chronologically: name the triggering event (quarterly cost review surfacing the workload as a top-N spend; internal audit asking why the chosen instance class; the original architect leaving; procurement review asking about Reserved Instances; release notes flagging new quantization variants). Then describe what the team tries to do and where it falls apart — they cannot produce the utilization band that was assumed, cannot show what scheduling policy was supposed to be in place, cannot defend the reserved-capacity coverage decision, cannot tell whether the workload is over-sized or under-utilised because no alarm tells them. Make the consequence concrete: months of accumulated overspend; finance pulling budget; a green-field re-investigation that stalls a release; a Reserved Instance purchase made on guesswork rather than data. The failure must demonstrate something a committed right-sizing baseline + utilization alarms + IaC-consistency lint would have caught at the moment of drift — not a generic 'the bill was too high' regression."
}

STYLE RULES

- Concrete over abstract. Name systems, roles, decisions. 'The claims-triage workload' not 'the AI system'.
- Match the implementation type honestly: RAG retrieves before generating; Agent reasons over multiple steps and calls tools; LLM Only is single-turn or prompt-chained without retrieval or tools; ML is classical with no LLM in the loop.
- Match the industry honestly. Use vocabulary the industry actually uses.
- Use specific numbers where they sharpen the story, but signpost invented precision honestly ('roughly $25K/month overspend', 'about a quarter before the pattern surfaced').
- Do not invent regulatory specifics. Generic framing only ('the firm's quarterly cost review', 'the platform team's infrastructure audit').
- Both sections are prose paragraphs. No bullet lists, no headings, no markdown emphasis other than the optional italicised opening sentence described above.
- Do not include the principle's solution, the fix, or recommendations. Show the problem only.
- Do not write any text outside the JSON object.

standards.u_principle: What each request costs stays under deliberate control, not left to creep unnoticed.

references.title: LLM Cost Optimization: Token Efficiency, Caching, and Prompt Design
references.note: Documents this principle's cumulative-drift failure almost verbatim: a system prompt ballooning from 300 to 1,800 tokens over six months of edits as engineers append edge-case instructions, with no review moment catching the per-call cost growth.

references.title: Token bloat: the silent cause of LLM cost spikes
references.note: Names the invisibility this principle attacks — 'reliability can stay green while token usage doubles' — attributing spikes to prompt scaffolding, RAG context creep, and tool-output bloat that no per-PR review flags.

references.title: How We Cut LLM Costs by 59% With Prompt Caching
references.note: Named-company case of uncapped prompt-scaffolding footprint: agent system prompts grown to 2,500+ lines of YAML / 20K+ tokens per agent, a single task burning ~60M tokens — the unbounded-template-size failure this principle's ceiling prevents.

references.title: Debugging Sudden LLM Cost Spikes: A Diagnostic Guide
references.note: Lists 'a prompt regression that ballooned token counts' as one of the six standard cost-spike buckets, and records an API-format change driving 200x baseline token rate before anyone noticed — the no-pre-merge-gate failure.

references.title: Prompt Bloat: Causes, Costs & Fixes for LLM Apps
references.note: Quantifies the waste this principle's declared ceiling prevents: a 1,200-token system prompt trimmable to 400 wastes 800 tokens per request, with token bloat causing a 10x cost jump for identical results.

dependencies.reason: Reuses GO3B1-01's per-template manifest row, `runtime_token_budget.input` field, and tokenizer adapters; the budget gate needs an addressable `template_id` to bite on.

references.question: GENCOST03 — How do you optimize the cost of your generative AI workload's prompting?
references.best_practice: GENCOST03-BP01 — Optimize prompt token length

implementation_step.title: Engineer the prompt to reduce the token count
implementation_step.verbatim_text: Engineer the prompt to reduce the token count, trimming as many unnecessary words as possible.

references.note: AWS GENCOST03-BP01 'Optimize prompt token length' tells teams to reduce prompt token count (step 2: 'trimming as many unnecessary words as possible') but leaves the discipline as coaching — it specifies no artefact, no ceiling, and no gate. This principle concretises step 2 into an enforceable contract: a declared per-template input-token ceiling (reusing GO3B1-01's runtime_token_budget.input field) plus a pre-merge gate that computes the template's declared-worst-case footprint and fails the build when it exceeds the ceiling, plus a budget-inflation governance gate. It absorbs step 1 ('Identify a verbose prompt which could be optimized') — a template that breaches its ceiling IS the identified verbose prompt, surfaced mechanically rather than by manual hunting. Steps 3 (use a separate LLM / Amazon Bedrock Prompt Optimization to shorten) and 4 (zero-shot / chain-of-thought / tree-of-thought / least-to-most technique menu plus 'continue testing') are not_promoted: step 3 is a vendor/technique suggestion with no architectural mandate surviving extraction, step 4 is a prompt-engineering technique menu plus process advice. The exact GENCOST03 question-stem wording is approximated from the verified focus-area title 'Cost-aware prompting' pending a question-page verification pass; the BP title and step-2 verbatim_text are verified against the live AWS Lens page (fetched 2026-06-04). Vendor specifics (Amazon Bedrock Prompt Optimization) are kept here rather than in solution.approach.

references.domain: Domain III: Understanding How to Govern AI Development
references.competency: III.B: Govern the AI Model Lifecycle (Design & Build)
references.note: Provisional mapping based on subject-matter proximity — a declared, enforced prompt-token budget is a design-and-build-time governance control over the model-call cost surface, aligning with III.B's lifecycle scope. Same anchor as cost siblings GC1B1-01 and GC2B2-01. Needs a side-by-side review against the AIGP III.B competency definition to promote to verified.

statement.title: Keep the cost of every prompt under deliberate control
statement.description:
Prompts grow silently as instructions are appended over time, inflating per-call cost with no natural review moment.

Each prompt's cost should be deliberately bounded and watched, so growth is caught early instead of surfacing on the inference bill.

problem.description:
Without an enforced input-token ceiling per prompt template, prompt scaffolding grows unreviewed — an extra few-shot exemplar here, an expanded system instruction there, a widened retrieval-context ceiling — and the cost lands only on the inference bill: thirty days late, attributed to finance rather than engineering, with no review moment that flags 'this template just got 40% more expensive per call.'

GO3B1-01 already requires every template to declare a `runtime_token_budget.input`, but nothing checks that number against the template's actual footprint — it is set once at registration and never enforced, so it drifts into fiction while the prompt underneath it bloats.

The failure is cumulative: no single PR is wrong, each token addition is locally reasonable, and the aggregate cost regression is invisible to per-PR review and to every other cost gate, because model-selection and hosting right-sizing do not look at prompt length.

Examples below are illustrative — synthesised scenarios; specific numbers are not from cited evidence.
examples: A triage classifier template's system prompt grows from roughly 200 to 900 tokens across three separate PRs as engineers append edge-case instructions; each PR reads as a reasonable fix in isolation; the manifest's declared `runtime_token_budget.input: 400` is never compared against the body, so the ceiling is fiction and per-call input cost more than doubles before anyone notices at the quarterly cost review.

A RAG summarisation template's `context` variable has its `typical_max` bumped from 2,000 to 8,000 tokens to fix a truncation bug; the one-line manifest edit ships uncontested; nobody computes that the template's declared-worst-case input now exceeds its `runtime_token_budget.input` threefold, and the cost only surfaces a quarter later when retrieval volume scales.

A few-shot extraction template carries twelve exemplars where three would meet the quality bar; the nine redundant exemplars add several thousand input tokens to every call; no gate compares the assembled scaffolding against the declared ceiling, so the waste is invisible to CI and shows up only as an unexplained line on the inference bill.

A team raises a template's `runtime_token_budget.input` from 1,000 to 6,000 in the very same PR that introduces a verbose new prompt body, because nothing requires a recorded rationale for inflating a budget; the ceiling silently tracks the bloat instead of constraining it, and the change passes review as a routine manifest edit.

Finance attributes a 30% inference-cost rise to 'model usage growth'; the real driver is prompt-scaffolding creep across six templates; the workload's GC1B1-01 model-selection ADR and GC2B2-01 hosting right-sizing both pass cleanly — neither looks at prompt length — so the actual cost driver is invisible to every existing cost gate and to GO3B1-01's existence-only budget check.

solution.approach:
The baseline mandate (Option A — declared-worst-case, manifest-derivable) reuses the prompt registry GO3B1-01 already requires: every template's input ceiling lives in the existing `runtime_token_budget.input` field of `prompts/manifest.yaml` (Mode A) or the TMS metadata (Mode B), and it must hold a real cap rather than a placeholder.

A central lint (shipped as part of the GO3B1-01 llm-sdk lint suite; convention `prompts/budget_check`) computes each template's declared-worst-case input footprint = static scaffolding tokens (the body at `prompts/<template_id>.{md,j2}` tokenised with the model's own tokenizer named in the manifest `model` field, `{{variable}}` placeholders excluded) + the sum of each declared variable's `typical_max`, and fails the PR when that footprint exceeds the declared `runtime_token_budget.input` or when the budget is absent or zero.

A second lint governs budget inflation: any PR raising a template's `runtime_token_budget.input` or a variable's `typical_max` above its previous committed value must carry a recorded cost rationale (a manifest `change_history` entry or a linked ADR under `docs/adrs/`), so a ceiling cannot be silently widened to absorb bloat. Both gates are manifest-derivable — no fixtures, no deployed baseline, no runtime — so they fire at review time.

Two alternative reference implementations are documented for workloads that want tighter coupling to real cost and MAY be adopted in addition to (not instead of) the Option A baseline:

- **Option B (measured-from-fixtures)** runs each template through the workload's GO1B1 eval-harness fixtures, measures the assembled input tokens with the model tokenizer, and gates the measured typical footprint against the declared budget — closer to real cost, at the price of coupling to fixture representativeness.
- **Option C (runtime alarm)** wires GO3B1-01's existing per-call actual-vs-declared token signal into an alarm that pages or dashboard-flags when production input tokens exceed the declared budget over a window — catches pathological runtime fills Options A and B cannot see, but is post-deploy observability rather than a pre-merge gate, so it complements rather than replaces the CI gate.

The reference implementation at `data/ri/GC3B1-01/README.md` is built on Option A.
key_benefits: Turns GO3B1-01's declared-but-toothless `runtime_token_budget.input` into a binding ceiling — the silent scaffolding creep that today surfaces only on the inference bill thirty days late now fails the PR that introduces it.

Manifest-derivable and deterministic — the gate reuses GO3B1-01's tokenizer integration and the per-variable `typical_max` ceilings already in the manifest, so it bites at review time with no fixtures, no deployed baseline, and no runtime dependency.
The budget-inflation governance gate closes the dodge loophole: a ceiling cannot be quietly widened to track bloat, because every upward change leaves an auditable cost-rationale trail.

Builds on GO3B1-01 without overlap and covers the cost gates' blind spot — GO3B1-01 mandates the budget field exists, GC1B1-01 records the model choice, GC2B2-01 right-sizes hosting; none of them constrain prompt length, which is the cost driver this principle catches (different failure mode, different focus area).

Paradigm-neutral by construction — because prompt length drives cost whether the model is billed per token or via self-hosted compute time (AWS GENCOST03-BP01 verbatim), the same declared-ceiling gate applies across all four serving paradigms, and parameterises cleanly over both GO3B1-01 storage modes (manifest sum for Mode A, TMS metadata for Mode B).

gates.check: On any PR, a lint computes each registered prompt template's declared-worst-case input footprint and fails the build when it exceeds the template's declared `runtime_token_budget.input`. The footprint is computed as the template body's static scaffolding token count — the body at `prompts/<template_id>.{md,j2}` (Mode A) or the TMS-resolved body (Mode B), tokenised with the model's own tokenizer named in the manifest `model` field, with `{{variable}}` placeholders excluded — PLUS the sum of each declared variable's `typical_max` from the manifest. The lint also fails the PR for any template whose `runtime_token_budget.input` is absent or zero, since a real ceiling rather than a placeholder is required. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

On any PR that raises a template's `runtime_token_budget.input` or any variable's `typical_max` above its previous committed value (diffed against the merge base), the PR must also add a budget-change rationale — either an entry in the template's `change_history` within the manifest row, or a linked ADR under `docs/adrs/` — recording why the ceiling was raised and the per-call input-token cost delta. A lint detects any upward budget or `typical_max` change without an accompanying rationale entry and fails the PR. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

changes.author: Dheeraj
changes.summary: Initial authoring against AWS GENCOST03-BP01 (Optimize prompt token length), anchored on implementation step 2 (Engineer the prompt to reduce the token count) with step 1 (Identify a verbose prompt) absorbed — a template breaching its ceiling IS the identified verbose prompt, surfaced mechanically. Third principle in the GENCOST pillar; resumed from the paused budget-principle slot now that its substrate (GO3B1-01, authored 2026-06-03) ships the template registry and the runtime_token_budget.input field this principle enforces. step_promotion rubric scored 3/3/3/3 (has_enforceable_artefact: declared ceiling in prompts/manifest.yaml + pre-merge footprint-vs-budget lint + budget-inflation governance lint; architecturally_distinct: GO3B1-01 mandates the budget field EXISTS while this makes the number a binding ceiling — different failure mode, sibling named with disjoint artefacts; in_bp_scope: BP literally named 'Optimize prompt token length', step 2 'reduce the token count' is the operative mandate; not_vendor_menu: Amazon Bedrock Prompt Optimization stripped from steps 3/4 and deferred to framework_mappings notes, generic ceiling-and-gate mandate survives). Steps 3 (separate-LLM / Bedrock Prompt Optimization) and 4 (zero-shot/CoT/ToT/least-to-most technique menu + 'continue testing') not_promoted as vendor/technique menu and process advice respectively (see lens_mapping.md GENCOST03-BP01 ledger). SOLUTION: Option A (declared-worst-case, manifest-derivable: scaffolding tokens via the model tokenizer + sum of declared variable typical_max, gated against runtime_token_budget.input) chosen as the mandated spine after an interactive design discussion; Options B (measured-from-eval-harness-fixtures) and C (runtime alarm on GO3B1-01's per-call actual-vs-declared signal) documented within solution.approach as alternative reference implementations a workload MAY additionally adopt, at the user's direction, so adopters have options — the RI and gates are built on Option A only, keeping gate coverage clean. New focus area P52 — Cost-aware Prompting created to mirror AWS GENCOST03's verified focus-area title; distinct from P51 — Inference Cost Optimization (which maps from GENCOST02 / GC2B2-01) and P41 — Model Selection & Right-sizing (GENCOST01 / GC1B1-01), per the focus_area rubric's one-focus-area-per-AWS-question mapping. Hand-scored against section rubrics: statement 3/2/3/2/3; problem 3/3/3/3/3/3 (every example is a prompt-length cost failure the model-selection / hosting gates and GO3B1-01's existence-only check all miss); solution 3/3/3/3/3/2 (Option A artefacts named with paths + gate points; B/C explicitly optional so coverage_completeness scopes to Option A); gates 3/3/3/3/3/3 (two pre_merge required-status-check gates covering the footprint check + budget-inflation governance). tier rubric D1=no (token-counting is cost discipline, no regulator/audit/litigation exposure) / D2=2 (a footprint lint + budget-governance lint with ongoing tokenizer maintenance reused across projects, thinner than GO3B1-01's D2=3 because it extends GO3B1-01's existing tokenizer + manifest tooling) → recommended_centralise → enterprise; validator project_architect, audit_mode self_attestation_with_mechanical_evidence, arb_role dashboard_and_spot_check parallel to GO3B1-01 and GC2B2-01. maturity_level scaling — pays off at project #2: the gate sits on GO3B1-01's registry substrate (itself scaling) and amortises once a portfolio of prompt templates whose token costs compound exists; foundational rejected because a principle cannot pay off before its scaling-tier substrate is adopted, and the tokenizer-lint build is not first-project capability. depends_on_deployment: no — Option A is manifest-derivable, which is why scaling (not mature) is correct: a small platform team ships it, no ARB. serving_paradigm all four mandatory — AWS verbatim states prompt length drives cost in BOTH managed per-token billing AND self-hosted/provisioned compute time, so unlike GC2B2-01 (self-hosting-only) this applies everywhere. impact_level Medium (matches AWS BP risk rating Medium and the GC1B1-01/GC2B2-01 cost-pillar pattern; prompt-cost failures are recoverable via trimming, not customer regressions or safety events). applicability { llm/rag/agentic: mandatory, ml: nice_to_have } matching the cost siblings (classical ML has no prompt-shaped artefact). AIGP cross-reference Domain III.B (Govern the AI Model Lifecycle — Design & Build), mapping_state unverified, parallel to GC1B1-01/GC2B2-01. explain_prompt compiled with cumulative_drift failure shape (many locally-rational token additions across PRs and templates compounding into a portfolio cost regression no single review catches) — distinguished from GO3B1-01's setup_absence shape because here the registry and budget field DO exist and the failure is drift past a ceiling that was never enforced. RI README at data/ri/GC3B1-01/README.md built on Option A. OPEN ITEMS: (1) JSON parse not run in-session — the sandbox could not reach the workspace (UNC mount); verify with `python3 -c "import json; json.load(open('data/principles.json'))"` from a terminal before pushing. (2) The exact GENCOST03 question-stem wording in framework_mappings.aws.references[0].question is approximated from the verified focus-area title pending a question-page verification pass; BP title and step-2 verbatim_text are verified against the live AWS Lens page fetched 2026-06-04.
