explain_prompt.system:
You are a senior software architect explaining the failure mode that GC3B1-01 — Cap every prompt template at a declared token budget and fail builds whose token footprint exceeds it — prevents, using a single illustrative scenario from a real industry.

THE PRINCIPLE

Every registered prompt template declares an input-token ceiling (the runtime_token_budget.input field GO3B1-01 already requires in the prompt manifest) that must hold a real cap, not a placeholder. A pre-merge gate computes each template's declared-worst-case input footprint — the static scaffolding tokenised with the model's own tokenizer plus the sum of its declared per-variable typical_max ceilings — and fails the build when that footprint exceeds the declared budget. A second gate requires a recorded cost rationale whenever a budget or a variable ceiling is raised, so the ceiling cannot be silently widened to absorb bloat. The mandate is manifest-derivable (no fixtures, no deployed baseline) and keyed on template_id.

THE FAILURE SHAPE

This is a cumulative-drift failure: many individually-reasonable token additions compound, across PRs and across templates, into a portfolio-level cost regression that no single review catches. A team has prompt templates registered (the GO3B1-01 registry is in place — templates are addressable, each carries a declared runtime_token_budget.input). The budgets were set once at registration and never enforced. Over the following months, engineers do locally-rational things: append an edge-case instruction to a system prompt, add two more few-shot exemplars to lift a quality metric, widen a retrieval-context variable ceiling to fix a truncation bug, paste in a longer policy preamble. Each change ships in a PR that reads as a reasonable fix; each adds a few hundred or a few thousand input tokens; none is individually wrong. The declared budget drifts into fiction while the prompt underneath it bloats, and because model-selection and hosting-cost gates never look at prompt length, every other cost control passes clean.

The drift is invisible until a triggering moment forces the aggregate into view: a quarterly cost review flags the workload as a top-N spend item; finance attributes a double-digit inference-cost rise to 'model usage growth' and asks engineering to explain it; a migration to a model with a smaller context window forces a prompt audit that discovers templates running several times their declared ceiling; a per-template token-cost dashboard finally attributes spend and the scaffolding creep becomes legible. At that moment the team cannot point to the PR that 'caused' the regression because no single PR did — the cost is the sum of dozens of reasonable edits across several templates, and there was never a review gate that compared a template's footprint against the ceiling it was supposed to respect.

The principle's value is forcing the ceiling to bind at review time, so prompt-length cost growth fails the PR that introduces it rather than accumulating silently onto the inference bill.

APPLICABILITY

GC3B1-01 is mandatory for LLM Only, RAG, and Agent workloads — anywhere prompts exist as registered templates with a token footprint. It is nice_to_have for traditional ML workloads, which have no prompt-shaped artefact. If the chosen Implementation type is ML, open use_case_background with one italicised sentence noting the principle is most acute for LLM-based and agentic workloads where prompt length is a direct cost driver, and that the example below is illustrative of how the same cumulative-drift cost failure would manifest in the closest analogue (for example unbounded feature-text or context assembly feeding an inference call).

YOUR TASK

You will be given an Industry and an Implementation type. Write an illustrative example for that combination demonstrating GC3B1-01's failure mode.

OUTPUT CONTRACT

Respond with a single valid JSON object of exactly this shape and nothing else:

{
  "use_case_background": "string — 150–250 words. Describe an enterprise with one or more AI workloads in production whose prompts are registered as versioned templates (assume the GO3B1-01 registry is in place; do not labour it). Be specific about the industry and the implementation type. Name two or three templates owned by a team, the integrating systems (with plausible invented names), and the kind of content each template carries (a system instruction block, few-shot exemplars, a retrieval-context variable). Crucially: hint at the drift offhandedly — note that the declared token budgets were set at registration and never revisited, that engineers routinely tweak prompt bodies to fix quality issues, that nobody watches per-template token footprint. The reader should picture a confident team where the registry exists and prompt cost has never been a review-time concern. Do NOT describe the failure here.",
  "issue_detail": "string — 200–350 words. Describe the moment the accumulated prompt-length cost drift surfaces. Walk through chronologically: name the trigger (a quarterly cost review flags the workload; finance attributes an inference-cost rise to 'model usage growth'; a model migration to a smaller context window forces a prompt audit; a per-template token-cost dashboard finally attributes spend). Then describe what the team tries to do and where it falls apart — they cannot point to a single causing PR because the regression is the sum of dozens of reasonable edits across multiple templates; they discover declared budgets that bear no relation to actual footprint; they find a variable ceiling that was widened a quarter ago and never costed. Name roles (the platform engineer building the cost dashboard; the finance partner asking for attribution; the engineer who widened the context ceiling). Make the consequence concrete — months of accumulated per-call overspend across the portfolio, a scramble to retroactively trim prompts, a budget that turns out to be fiction. The failure must demonstrate something a declared-ceiling gate that computes footprint at review time would have caught at the PR that introduced each increment — not a generic 'our bill was high' regression."
}

STYLE RULES

- Concrete over abstract. Name systems, roles, templates. 'The claims-triage classifier template' not 'the AI prompt'.
- Match the implementation type honestly: RAG retrieves before generating; Agent reasons over multiple steps and calls tools; LLM Only is single-turn or prompt-chained without retrieval or tools; ML is classical with no LLM in the loop.
- Match the industry honestly. Use vocabulary the industry actually uses.
- Use specific numbers where they sharpen the story, but signpost invented precision honestly ('roughly 4,000 extra tokens per call', 'about a quarter before the pattern surfaced').
- Do not invent regulatory specifics. Generic framing only ('the firm's quarterly cost review', 'the platform team's cost audit').
- Both sections are prose paragraphs. No bullet lists, no headings, no markdown emphasis other than the optional italicised opening sentence described above.
- Do not include the principle's solution, the fix, or recommendations. Show the problem only.
- Do not write any text outside the JSON object.

standards.u_principle: The same work is never paid for twice.

references.title: Prompt Caching Saves Money Until It Doesn't
references.note: A bill doubles weeks after caching was 'enabled' because an injected timestamp and reordered JSON tool-schema keys silently broke the static prefix — the silent-break and false-positive cache failures this principle's declare-and-recompute gate is designed to catch.

references.title: System prompt section ordering breaks LLM prefix caching for local models
references.note: A concrete bug report of prompt section ordering silently invalidating the cacheable prefix — the mis-declared / broken-prefix case this principle gates against at review time.

dependencies.reason: Needs GO3B1-01's registered, addressable prompt templates, the manifest the cache fields live in, and its per-provider tokenizer adapters; there is no template body or `template_id` to mark a cache checkpoint on without the registry.

Reuses GC3B1-01's tokenizer-integration pattern for static-token counting; GC3B3-01 computes the contiguous cache-prefix specifically (the fixed run before the first variable) rather than total scaffolding tokens, so it extends rather than strictly requires GC3B1-01.

references.question: GENCOST03 — How do you engineer prompts to optimize cost?
references.best_practice: GENCOST03-BP03 — Implement prompt caching to reduce token costs

implementation_step.title: Enable prompt caching for supported models
implementation_step.verbatim_text: Enable prompt caching for supported models: Turn on caching in Amazon Bedrock console; For APIs, set appropriate caching flags; Configure cache checkpoints at optimal locations.

references.note: AWS BP03 verbatim and step list fetched from the live AWS Lens page 2026-06-05. Anchors step 2 (enable caching + configure cache checkpoints) and absorbs step 1 (Identify opportunities for caching — review for repeated prompt components, verify minimum-token thresholds) as the eligibility computation WITHIN the gate: a template whose computed static prefix clears the model's minimum cache size IS the identified caching opportunity, surfaced mechanically rather than by manual review. AWS leaves unspecified: where the cacheable-prefix size is declared, how eligibility is decided against the model's minimum-token threshold, how the declaration is kept honest against the actual prompt body, and how this is enforced as a versioned artefact in CI. This principle concretises all four. AWS's implementation guidance is Bedrock-specific (console toggle, Bedrock cache checkpoints) but the underlying mandate — mark the stable leading prefix of a reused prompt as a cache checkpoint so it bills at the provider's reduced cached-token rate — is provider-neutral (Anthropic `cache_control`, OpenAI automatic prefix caching, Bedrock cache checkpoints all implement the same prefix-reuse contract); vendor specifics are deferred to this note. Steps 3 (Monitor caching metrics — hit/miss rates, cached-vs-uncached cost) and 4 (Optimize cache usage — tune checkpoint placement, balance write vs read cost) are not_promoted as standalone principles: step 3's runtime monitoring is captured as the optional Option C (telemetry alarm on cache hit-rate) documented in solution.approach and properly belongs to the GO3B2 observability family; step 4 is tuning/process advice with no commitable pre-merge artefact. See lens_mapping.md GENCOST03-BP03 ledger.

references.domain: Domain III: Understanding How to Govern AI Development
references.competency: III.B: Govern the AI Model Lifecycle (Design & Build)
references.note: Provisional mapping based on subject-matter proximity — prompt-caching configuration is a build-phase design discipline on the prompt artefact, aligning with III.B's scope, parallel to the cost siblings GC1B1-01 / GC2B2-01 / GC3B1-01. Needs a side-by-side review against the AIGP III.B competency definition to promote to verified.

statement.title: Don't pay twice for the same prompt content
statement.description:
Reused prompt content can be served from cache at a lower rate, but only when its fixed prefix is recognised as reusable.

Cacheable prompts should actually be cached, and broken or mis-declared caching should not slip by unnoticed onto the bill.

problem.description:
Prompt caching bills the stable, reused leading portion of a prompt at a steeply reduced rate (often a fraction of the standard input-token price) once it is marked as a cache checkpoint. Without a discipline that forces eligible templates to be marked, caching is simply never turned on: the workload runs fine, the output is correct, and the only signal that a large fixed prefix is being re-processed and billed at full rate on every call is the inference bill — thirty days late, attributed to finance, absorbed as 'model usage.'

The discipline is also fragile in two further ways:

- A cacheable prefix silently breaks when a variable is inserted ahead of it.
- A declared prefix size drifts into fiction when the body changes underneath it or the number is hand-written to pass a review.

None of these are visible to per-PR review, and no other cost gate looks at prompt-prefix structure — model-selection and hosting right-sizing are blind to it.

Examples below are illustrative — synthesised scenarios; specific numbers are not from cited evidence.
examples: A RAG support assistant prepends a roughly 6,000-token product manual to every query as a stable system block; caching is never enabled, so the manual is re-processed and billed at full input rate across all ~50K daily calls; the waste surfaces only at the quarterly cost review, when someone realises the unchanging manual could have billed at the reduced cached-token rate from day one.

A claims-triage agent carries a roughly 3,000-token policy preamble that is byte-identical on every call; no cache checkpoint is declared; the team pays full input price for that preamble on every invocation for two quarters until a cost audit asks why a static preamble is billed fresh each time.

A template that was cacheable gets a `{{customer_name}}` personalisation variable inserted near the top during a feature PR; everything after it is now uncacheable; the cache hit rate silently collapses and per-call cost climbs, with no review flag because nothing checks that the stable prefix is still contiguous and ahead of the first variable.

A team declares a cache checkpoint but the static prefix is only about 600 tokens — below the model's ~1,024-token minimum — so caching never actually engages; nobody computed the prefix size, so the team believes caching is saving money while every call bills at full rate.

A developer hand-writes `static_prefix_tokens: 4000` in the manifest to clear a cost review, but the real fixed prefix is about 900 tokens because most of the 'fixed' text is actually filled per call; the declared number is fiction, the expected savings never materialise, and no gate recomputes the prefix against the tokenised body to catch the discrepancy.

solution.approach:
The baseline mandate (Option A — declared-and-recomputed, manifest-derivable) reuses the prompt registry GO3B1-01 already requires. Each template's manifest row (Mode A: `prompts/manifest.yaml`; Mode B: TMS metadata) carries a `cache` block: `static_prefix_tokens` (the declared token count of the contiguous fixed leading span before the first `{{variable}}`) and a cache decision — either `checkpoint: true` (a cache checkpoint is marked at the end of the static prefix via the central SDK's provider-neutral caching contract) or `opt_out` with a recorded reason.

A central lint (shipped in the GO3B1-01 llm-sdk lint suite; convention `prompts/cache_check`) enforces three things at pre-merge:

- `cache.static_prefix_tokens` is declared for every template, and the build fails when it is absent.
- For any template whose declared `static_prefix_tokens` is greater than or equal to its model's minimum cache-checkpoint size — read from a central, platform-owned `models/cache_minimums.yaml` keyed by model identifier — the row must set `checkpoint: true` or carry a non-empty `opt_out` reason, and the build fails otherwise.
- The lint recomputes the static prefix itself (the contiguous body span at `prompts/<template_id>.{md,j2}` up to the first `{{variable}}`, tokenised with the model's own tokenizer named in the manifest `model` field, reusing GO3B1-01's per-provider tokenizer adapters) and fails when the declared `static_prefix_tokens` diverges from the recomputed value beyond a declared tolerance (default 10%), which closes both the silent-break case (a variable inserted ahead of the prefix shrinks the computed prefix) and the hand-written-fiction case.

All three checks are manifest-derivable — no fixtures, no deployed baseline, no runtime — so they fire at review time.

One alternative reference implementation is documented for workloads wanting runtime assurance and MAY be adopted in addition to (not instead of) the Option A baseline: **Option C (runtime telemetry alarm)** wires the cache hit-rate and the actual production prefix size — already observable through GO3B2-01's central observability stream — into an alarm that dashboard-flags or pages when a cache-eligible template's production hit rate falls below a threshold or its actual prefix diverges from the declared value. Option C is post-deploy observability, not a pre-merge gate; it catches runtime cache misses (TTL expiry under low traffic, non-contiguous fills) that Option A cannot see, and properly belongs to the GO3B2 observability family, so it complements rather than replaces the CI gate.

The reference implementation at `data/ri/GC3B3-01/README.md` is built on Option A.
key_benefits: Takes the cheap-by-default savings by construction — a large reused prefix that today bills at full input rate on every call cannot merge until it either carries a cache checkpoint or a recorded opt-out, so the saving is the default rather than something a team has to remember to turn on.

The recompute gate stops the declared prefix count from drifting into fiction — a hand-written number that does not match the tokenised body fails the build, closing the gaming loophole the same way GC3B1-01's footprint gate closes the budget-as-placeholder loophole.

Catches the silent cache-break: inserting a variable ahead of the stable prefix shrinks the recomputed prefix, which the gate surfaces at the PR that introduces it instead of as an unexplained per-call cost rise a quarter later.

Catches false eligibility — a declared checkpoint on a sub-minimum prefix (where caching never actually engages) is flagged because the gate compares the recomputed prefix against the model's minimum cache size in `models/cache_minimums.yaml`.

Distinct from GC3B1-01 without overlap and reuses its machinery — GC3B1-01 fails prompts whose footprint is too BIG (creep past a ceiling); GC3B3-01 acts when a big prefix is STABLE and reused (mark it cacheable). Opposite lever on the same token cost, different failure mode (waste-by-not-caching vs creep-past-ceiling), both manifest-derivable on GO3B1-01's registry and tokenizer.

gates.check: On any PR, a lint verifies that every registered prompt template declares `cache.static_prefix_tokens` in its manifest row (Mode A `prompts/manifest.yaml`; Mode B TMS metadata) and fails the build when the field is absent. For any template whose declared `cache.static_prefix_tokens` is greater than or equal to its model's minimum cache-checkpoint size (read from the central, platform-owned `models/cache_minimums.yaml` keyed by the manifest `model` field), the row must set `cache.checkpoint: true` or carry a non-empty `cache.opt_out` reason; the build fails for a cache-eligible template that is neither cached nor opted out. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

On any PR, a lint recomputes each template's static prefix — the contiguous body span at `prompts/<template_id>.{md,j2}` (Mode A) or the TMS-resolved body (Mode B) up to the first `{{variable}}`, tokenised with the model's own tokenizer named in the manifest `model` field — and fails the build when the declared `cache.static_prefix_tokens` diverges from the recomputed value beyond the declared tolerance (default 10%). This catches both a prefix silently broken by a variable inserted ahead of it (recomputed prefix shrinks) and a hand-declared count that does not match the body. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

Fourth principle in the GENCOST pillar, second under GENCOST03 / focus area P52 — Cost-aware Prompting (sibling to GC3B1-01). BP03 verbatim + the full GENCOST03 BP list (BP01–BP04) and the question stem 'How do you engineer prompts to optimize cost?' fetched from the live AWS Lens page 2026-06-05; lens_mapping.md updated the same day to record BP03/BP04 (previously missing from the ledger). step_promotion rubric scored 3/3/3/3 (has_enforceable_artefact: `cache` block in the GO3B1-01 manifest + three pre-merge checks reusing the tokenizer; architecturally_distinct: opposite cost lever from GC3B1-01 — GC3B1-01 fails prompts too BIG, GC3B3-01 acts when a big prefix is STABLE and reused — named with disjoint gates and the `models/cache_minimums.yaml` artefact; in_bp_scope: BP literally 'Implement prompt caching to reduce token costs' under GENCOST03; not_vendor_menu: Bedrock console/checkpoint specifics stripped, the provider-neutral mark-the-stable-prefix-as-a-checkpoint mandate survives — Anthropic cache_control, OpenAI prefix caching, Bedrock checkpoints all implement it). Steps 3 (Monitor caching metrics) and 4 (Optimize cache usage) not_promoted: step 3's runtime monitoring is captured as optional Option C (telemetry alarm) in solution.approach and belongs to the GO3B2 observability family; step 4 is tuning/process advice with no commitable pre-merge artefact. SOLUTION: Option A (declared-and-recomputed, manifest-derivable) is the mandated spine — declare `cache.static_prefix_tokens` + a cache decision (checkpoint or opt_out); a `prompts/cache_check` lint enforces (1) the count is declared, (2) cache-eligible templates (prefix >= model minimum from `models/cache_minimums.yaml`) are cached or opted out, (3) the declared count matches the recomputed contiguous-prefix tokenisation within tolerance. This realises the user's three-check design: (check 1) declare the fixed-prefix count, fail if absent; (check 2) recompute and fail on high variance; (check 3) telemetry on actual size = optional Option C, documented as an additive advanced implementation, not the spine. Hand-scored against section rubrics: statement 3/3/3/2/3; problem 3/3/3/3/3/3 (five examples: never-enabled on a large RAG manual prefix, never-enabled on a policy preamble, silent break by a leading variable, false eligibility below the model minimum, hand-written fiction caught by recompute — each a caching cost failure invisible to the model-selection / hosting / footprint gates); solution 3/3/3/3/3/2 (Option A artefacts named with paths + gate points; Option C explicitly optional so coverage_completeness scopes to Option A); gates 3/3/3/3/3/3 (two pre_merge required-status-check gates: declare+eligibility, and recompute-variance). dependencies (schema v1.10): GO3B1-01 hard (registry + manifest + tokenizer adapters + addressable template_id), GC3B1-01 soft (reuses the tokenizer-integration pattern; computes the contiguous cache-prefix specifically rather than total scaffolding tokens). tier rubric D1=no (caching is cost discipline; no regulator/audit/litigation exposure) / D2=2 (a cache-eligibility lint + a recompute-variance lint with ongoing tokenizer + cache-minimums maintenance, reused across projects, thinner than GO3B1-01's D2=3 because it extends GO3B1-01's existing tokenizer + manifest tooling) → recommended_centralise → enterprise; validator project_architect, audit_mode self_attestation_with_mechanical_evidence, arb_role dashboard_and_spot_check parallel to GC3B1-01 / GO3B1-01. maturity_level scaling — pays off at project #2: the gate sits on GO3B1-01's registry substrate (itself scaling) and amortises once a portfolio of templates with large reusable prefixes exists; the tokenizer/prefix lint is not first-project capability; foundational rejected because a principle cannot pay off before its scaling-tier substrate is adopted. serving_paradigm api_on_demand mandatory + api_provisioned mandatory, self_hosted_managed nice_to_have + self_hosted_unmanaged nice_to_have — AWS BP03 frames the benefit as cached tokens billed at a reduced rate, a managed-API pricing feature; on committed throughput caching frees billed capacity (mandatory), while on self-hosted the benefit is KV-cache recompute/latency rather than a per-token bill line (nice_to_have). This is a deliberate departure from GC3B1-01's all-four-mandatory because token-length cost exists on every paradigm whereas the reduced-cached-rate billing does not. impact_level Medium (matches AWS BP03 risk rating Medium and the GC1B1-01/GC2B2-01/GC3B1-01 cost-pillar pattern; caching cost failures are recoverable by marking the prefix, not customer regressions or safety events). applicability { llm/rag/agentic: mandatory, ml: nice_to_have } matching the cost siblings (classical ML has no prompt-shaped artefact). AIGP cross-reference Domain III.B (Govern the AI Model Lifecycle — Design & Build), mapping_state unverified, parallel to the cost siblings. explain_prompt compiled with setup_absence failure shape (the caching discipline was never set up; a cost review / audit / migration makes the absence visible) with a secondary nod to the silent-break variant — distinguished from GC3B1-01's cumulative_drift shape because the dominant failure is a discrete never-enabled absence rather than gradual creep past a ceiling. RI README at data/ri/GC3B3-01/README.md built on Option A, following the GC3B1-01 template. OPEN ITEMS: (1) JSON parse not run in-session — the sandbox could not reach the workspace (UNC mount); verify with `python3 -c "import json; json.load(open('data/principles.json'))"` from a terminal before pushing. (2) `models/cache_minimums.yaml` per-model minimum-token thresholds must be maintained against provider docs (e.g. Anthropic ~1,024-token minimum for some models); the central platform team owns this table, flagged in the RI.

explain_prompt.system:
You are a senior software architect explaining the failure mode that GC3B3-01 — Mark the cacheable static prefix of every reused prompt template and fail builds that leave a cache-eligible template uncached or mis-declare its prefix size — prevents, using a single illustrative scenario from a real industry.

THE PRINCIPLE

Every registered prompt template declares, in the GO3B1-01 manifest, the token count of its static prefix (the contiguous fixed leading span before the first runtime variable) and a cache decision. Any template whose static prefix clears its model's minimum cache-checkpoint size must mark a cache checkpoint — so the reused prefix bills at the provider's reduced cached-token rate — or carry a recorded opt-out. A pre-merge gate fails the build when the prefix count is undeclared, when a cache-eligible template is neither cached nor opted out, or when the declared prefix count diverges from the value CI recomputes with the model's own tokenizer.

THE FAILURE SHAPE

This is a setup-absence failure: prompt caching is simply never turned on for prompts that are eligible for it, and the absence is invisible until the bill is examined. The team has prompts registered (the GO3B1-01 registry is in place — templates are addressable, bodies are versioned). Many templates carry a large, byte-identical leading block reused on every call: a product manual prepended to every RAG query, a long policy preamble in an agent's system prompt, a fixed instruction-and-schema header. Caching that block would bill it at a fraction of the standard input rate. But nobody marked a cache checkpoint, because nothing required it; the prompt works, the output is correct, and the full-rate reprocessing of the stable prefix on every call shows up only as an undifferentiated line on the inference bill — thirty days late, owned by finance, absorbed as 'model usage growth.'

The absence is forced into view by a triggering event: a quarterly cost review flags the workload as a top-N spend item; a finance partner asks why a static preamble is billed fresh on every call; a migration audit examining prompt structure finds large reused prefixes that were never cached; a per-template cost dashboard finally attributes spend and the un-cached prefixes become legible. A close variant is the silent break — a template that WAS cacheable stops being cacheable when a personalisation variable is inserted ahead of the stable prefix, collapsing the cache hit rate with no review flag — and the false-positive — a declared checkpoint on a prefix below the model's minimum cache size, where caching never actually engages and the team believes it is saving money while paying full rate.

The principle's value is forcing every eligible template to be marked (or explicitly opted out) and forcing the declared prefix size to match the tokenised body at review time, so the cheap-by-default saving is taken by construction and a broken or fictional declaration fails the PR rather than surfacing as an unexplained cost months later.

APPLICABILITY

GC3B3-01 is mandatory for LLM Only, RAG, and Agent workloads on managed-API serving (on-demand or provisioned), where reused prefixes bill at a reduced cached-token rate — RAG workloads with large stable context blocks benefit most. It is nice_to_have on self-hosted serving (the benefit there is KV-cache recompute and latency rather than a per-token bill line) and nice_to_have for traditional ML workloads (no prompt-shaped artefact). If the chosen Implementation type is ML, open use_case_background with one italicised sentence noting the principle is most acute for LLM-based and agentic workloads with large reused prompt prefixes on managed-API serving, and that the example below is illustrative of how the same setup-absence cost failure would manifest in the closest analogue.

YOUR TASK

You will be given an Industry and an Implementation type. Write an illustrative example for that combination demonstrating GC3B3-01's failure mode.

OUTPUT CONTRACT

Respond with a single valid JSON object of exactly this shape and nothing else:

{
  "use_case_background": "string — 150–250 words. Describe an enterprise with one or more AI workloads in production whose prompts are registered as versioned templates (assume the GO3B1-01 registry is in place; do not labour it) and that are served on a managed API. Be specific about the industry and the implementation type. Name a workload, the team, the integrating systems (with plausible invented names), and crucially the large, byte-identical leading block one or more templates carry on every call (a product manual, a policy preamble, a fixed instruction-and-schema header) and roughly how big it is. Hint at the absence offhandedly — caching was never part of the prompt-authoring checklist, nobody tracks per-template token cost, the team assumes the model 'just works.' Do NOT describe the failure here.",
  "issue_detail": "string — 200–350 words. Describe the moment the never-enabled caching surfaces as cost. Walk through chronologically: name the trigger (a quarterly cost review flags the workload; finance asks why a static preamble is billed fresh every call; a model migration audit examines prompt structure; a per-template cost dashboard finally attributes spend). Then describe what the team finds — a large stable prefix re-processed and billed at full input rate on every call across high daily volume, when it could have billed at the reduced cached-token rate; OR the silent-break variant where a leading variable killed an existing cache; OR a declared checkpoint that never engaged because the prefix was below the model's minimum. Name roles (the platform engineer building the cost dashboard; the finance partner asking for attribution; the engineer who added a personalisation variable). Make the consequence concrete — months of accumulated full-rate reprocessing across the portfolio, a scramble to retrofit cache checkpoints, savings that were available from day one and simply never taken. The failure must demonstrate something a declare-plus-recompute caching gate would have caught at the PR — not a generic 'our bill was high' regression."
}

STYLE RULES

- Concrete over abstract. Name systems, roles, templates. 'The policy-preamble block in the claims-triage template' not 'the AI prompt'.
- Match the implementation type honestly: RAG retrieves before generating; Agent reasons over multiple steps and calls tools; LLM Only is single-turn or prompt-chained without retrieval or tools; ML is classical with no LLM in the loop.
- Match the industry honestly. Use vocabulary the industry actually uses.
- Use specific numbers where they sharpen the story, but signpost invented precision honestly ('roughly 6,000 tokens of stable manual', 'about a quarter before anyone looked').
- Do not invent regulatory specifics. Generic framing only ('the firm's quarterly cost review', 'the platform team's cost audit').
- Both sections are prose paragraphs. No bullet lists, no headings, no markdown emphasis other than the optional italicised opening sentence described above.
- Do not include the principle's solution, the fix, or recommendations. Show the problem only.
- Do not write any text outside the JSON object.

standards.u_principle: A system carries no more weight than the job needs.

references.title: Scaling Vector Search: Comparing Quantization and Matryoshka Embeddings for 80% Cost Reduction
references.note: Documents this principle's cost lever directly — vector-store storage and search cost are linear in embedding dimension, and truncating the dimension (alone or with quantization) cuts infrastructure cost up to 80% while retrieval quality holds, the saving an oversized default leaves on the table.

references.title: Gemini Embedding 2: Variants, Dimensions, and Use Cases
references.note: Shows the default-taken-unquestioned failure is avoidable: a current production embedding model can be truncated from 3,072 down to 768 or 256 dimensions without a significant drop in retrieval quality, so taking the 3,072 default sets several times the necessary vector-store cost.

dependencies.reason: The embedding-model choice recorded in GC1B1-01's model-selection ADR determines the vector dimensions available to pick within; GC4B1-01 gates the declared dimension against the deployed index independently of that ADR, so it composes with but does not require it.

references.question: GENCOST04 — How do you optimize vector stores for cost?
references.best_practice: GENCOST04-BP01 — Reduce vector length on embedded tokens

implementation_step.title: Embed data using the smallest vector length
implementation_step.verbatim_text: Embed data using the smallest vector length. You may have to modify the chunk size of the document or introduce overlapping chunks to maintain high relevance on output.

references.note: AWS GENCOST04-BP01 'Reduce vector length on embedded tokens' (Medium risk) tells teams to embed with a smaller vector dimension to cut vector-store storage and computation cost, deliberately trading against retrieval quality — but it leaves the discipline as advice: no declared artefact, no consistency check between the chosen dimension and the deployed index, and no gate. This principle concretises step 2 ('Embed data using the smallest vector length') into an enforceable contract: a declared per-vector-store record (embedding model + chosen dimension + the retrieval-quality result that justified it) plus a pre-merge gate that fails when the declaration is missing, when the deployed index dimension does not match the declared one, or when the dimension is raised without a recorded cost/quality rationale. It absorbs step 1 ('Identify the smallest vector length supported by the selected embedding foundation model') as the gate's input — the smallest-supported dimension is a property the declaration records against, surfaced from the model card rather than hunted manually. Steps 3 ('Perform latency and load testing to verify model response quality is still sufficient') and 4 ('Re-test with increased vector size or modified document chunking strategy') are not_promoted: they are retrieval-quality testing and chunking/search-algorithm tuning — process advice whose substance overlaps the GO1B1 eval-harness family and the GENPERF04 (Vector store optimization) performance focus area, with no commitable pre-merge artefact distinct from the quality-floor number this principle's declaration already records. The vendor-flavoured aside in the BP guidance (compressed vector types as an embedding-model selection factor) is stripped to the generic mandate; it is an input to the dimension/precision choice the declaration captures, not a separate gate. BP title, step verbatim_text, and the GENCOST04 question stem ('How do you optimize vector stores for cost?') all verified against the live AWS Lens pages (BP page + gencost04.html focus-area page fetched 2026-06-06). GENCOST04 ships exactly one BP (BP01), so the focus area is fully covered by this single principle. First principle under GENCOST04; new focus area P53 — Cost-informed Vector Stores created.

references.domain: Domain III: Understanding How to Govern AI Development
references.competency: III.B: Govern the AI Model Lifecycle (Design & Build)
references.note: Provisional mapping based on subject-matter proximity — choosing and committing to an embedding vector dimension is a design-and-build-time governance control over the vector-store cost surface, aligning with III.B's lifecycle scope, parallel to the cost siblings GC1B1-01 / GC2B2-01 / GC3B1-01 / GC3B3-01. Arguably also touches III.A (Govern Data for AI Systems) since the artefact is the data index; III.B chosen for consistency with the cost-pillar pattern. Needs a side-by-side review against the AIGP III.B competency definition to promote to verified.

statement.title: Use no more embedding dimensions than retrieval needs
statement.description:
Larger embedding vectors cost more to store and query, and that cost is effectively locked in once an index is built.

The smallest vector that still retrieves well should be a deliberate choice, preferred over the embedding model's default taken unquestioned.

problem.description:
Vector-store storage and query cost scale roughly linearly with embedding dimension, so the dimension chosen at index-build time sets a recurring cost for the life of the index.

The dominant failure is that nobody chooses it: a team takes whatever dimension the embedding model emits by default (often 1536 or 3072), embeds the whole corpus, builds the index, and ships — and the oversized default silently bills several times the necessary storage and per-query compute, with the only signal a vector-database line item that finance absorbs as infrastructure cost. The choice is then effectively irreversible: re-dimensioning means re-embedding every chunk and rebuilding the index, a multi-week migration nobody funds once the system is live, so the default is locked in.

The discipline is fragile in two further ways:

- A declared dimension drifts into fiction when the index is built at a different value than the design intended (a default parameter never overridden).
- A dimension is quietly raised 'to improve quality' with no measurement, tripling cost for a quality gain nobody verified.

None of these are visible to per-PR review, and no other cost gate looks at index dimension — model-selection and hosting right-sizing are blind to it.

Examples below are illustrative — synthesised scenarios; specific numbers are not from cited evidence.
examples: A customer-support RAG assistant over roughly 5 million help-desk chunks is embedded at the model's 3,072-dimension default; nobody tested smaller sizes; a later audit finds 1,024 dimensions clears the team's top-5 retrieval bar at near-identical quality, meaning the index has been billing roughly three times the necessary vector-store storage and query cost since launch.

The embedding model is a Matryoshka/MRL model that can be safely truncated to 768 or 256 dimensions, but the team uses the full 3,072 because nobody read the model card; a trivial truncation at embed time would have cut storage and search cost with negligible recall loss, and the saving was available from day one.

A design doc records the embedding dimension as 768, but the index was actually created at 1,536 because a default constructor argument was never overridden; the declared number is fiction, no gate compares the deployed index dimension against the declared one, and the team believes it is paying for 768 while paying for double.

An engineer raises the dimension from 1,024 to 3,072 in a PR 'to improve answer quality'; no retrieval measurement accompanies the change; vector-store cost triples; the PR passes review as a routine index-config edit, and a quarter later nobody can say whether the quality actually improved.

A team realises mid-life that its 3,072-dimension index is wasteful, but re-embedding 8 million chunks and rebuilding the index is a multi-week migration that never gets prioritised against feature work, so the oversized index stays in production and the cost is locked in — a choice that a build-time gate would have forced before the corpus was ever embedded.

key_benefits: Forces the dimension to be a deliberate, recorded choice before the corpus is embedded — the oversized default that today silently sets vector-store cost for the life of the index cannot merge without a declaration naming the chosen dimension and the quality result that justified it.

Config-derivable and deterministic — the gate reads the declaration and the index configuration, so it bites at review time with no fixtures, no deployed baseline, and no retrieval runtime dependency, catching the choice while it is still cheap to change.

The deployed-vs-declared consistency check stops the declared dimension from drifting into fiction — an index actually built at 1,536 while the declaration claims 768 fails the build, the same way GC3B1-01's footprint gate closes the budget-as-placeholder loophole.

The dimension-inflation governance check closes the quietly-raised dodge — a dimension cannot be bumped 'to improve quality' without a recorded cost/quality rationale, so a cost-tripling change leaves an auditable trail instead of passing as a routine config edit.

Builds on the cost siblings without overlap and covers a cost blind spot — GC1B1-01 records the model choice, GC2B2-01 right-sizes hosting, GC3B1-01/GC3B3-01 act on prompt tokens; none of them look at embedding dimension, which is a distinct cost lever on the vector store (storage + index + query), independent of model choice (Matryoshka/MRL models make dimension a free-standing knob) and largely irreversible once indexed.

gates.check: On any PR touching a vector store, a lint verifies that the store's declaration (convention `vectorstore/embedding.yaml` or equivalent manifest row) exists and populates `embedding_model`, `vector_dimension`, `quality_floor`, `quality_result`, and `checked_on`, and fails the build when the declaration is absent or any field is blank. The same lint parses the vector-store index configuration (the IaC resource or index-creation definition: Pinecone / pgvector / OpenSearch / Weaviate) and fails when the dimension of the actually-provisioned index differs from the declared `vector_dimension`. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

On any PR that raises a vector store's declared `vector_dimension` above its previous committed value (diffed against the merge base), the PR must also add a rationale — either a `change_history` entry in the declaration or a linked ADR under `docs/adrs/` — recording why the dimension was raised and the vector-store cost delta. A lint detects any upward dimension change without an accompanying rationale entry and fails the PR. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

changes.summary: Initial authoring against AWS GENCOST04-BP01 (Reduce vector length on embedded tokens), anchored on implementation step 2 (Embed data using the smallest vector length) with step 1 (Identify the smallest vector length supported by the selected embedding foundation model) absorbed as the gate's input — the smallest-supported dimension is a property the declaration records against, surfaced from the model card rather than hunted manually. First principle under GENCOST04 / GenCost Q4; first principle in the new focus area P53 — Cost-informed Vector Stores (created this session to mirror AWS GENCOST04's verified focus-area title 'Cost-informed vector stores', distinct from P51 Inference Cost Optimization / P52 Cost-aware Prompting / P41 Model Selection & Right-sizing per the one-focus-area-per-AWS-question mapping). 14th principle in the catalogue. BP title and step-2 verbatim_text verified against the live AWS Lens page (fetched 2026-06-06); GENCOST04 question stem approximated from the verified focus-area title pending a question-page verification pass. step_promotion rubric scored 3/3/3/3 (has_enforceable_artefact: declared vector-store record + pre-merge declaration/consistency lint + dimension-inflation governance lint; architecturally_distinct: a cost lever on the VECTOR STORE — storage/index/query — not on generation inference tokens, independent of model choice via Matryoshka/MRL truncatable dimensions, and largely irreversible once indexed, so not absorbed by GC1B1-01's model-selection ADR and disjoint from the P52 prompt-cost siblings; in_bp_scope: GENCOST04 is literally 'Cost-informed vector stores' and BP01 literally 'Reduce vector length on embedded tokens'; not_vendor_menu: verbatim is a generic dimension mandate, the compressed-vector-types aside stripped to a neutral input to the dimension/precision choice). Steps 3 (latency/load testing to verify response quality) and 4 (re-test with larger dimension / modified chunking / different search algorithm) not_promoted: retrieval-quality testing and chunking/search tuning — process advice overlapping the GO1B1 eval-harness family and the GENPERF04 (Vector store optimization) performance focus area, with no commitable pre-merge artefact distinct from the quality-floor number the declaration already records (see lens_mapping.md GENCOST04-BP01 ledger). SOLUTION: Option A (declared-and-verified, config-derivable) is the mandated spine — declare embedding_model + vector_dimension + quality_floor + quality_result + checked_on; a `vectorstore/dimension_check` lint enforces (1) declaration completeness, (2) deployed-index-vs-declared-dimension consistency by parsing the index IaC/config, (3) dimension-inflation governance requiring a recorded rationale for any upward change. The gate enforces that a quality number is RECORDED and the deployed dimension is real and stable; it does NOT re-run retrieval evaluation, which leans on a retrieval-eval harness (GENPERF04 territory). Option B (measured-from-retrieval-eval: gate the chosen dimension against the smallest that clears the quality_floor across a harness sweep) documented within solution.approach as an additive reference implementation a workload MAY adopt; the RI and gates are built on Option A only, keeping gate coverage clean — parallel to GC3B1-01's Option A/B/C treatment. STATEMENT TITLE deliberately kept plain ('Use the smallest embedding vector that still retrieves well') per the user's working-style rule committed to data/CLAUDE.md this session (plain spoken titles, no stacked clauses); this trades the statement rubric's names_artefact_and_enforcement dimension down in favour of the explicit user override, with the artefact + enforcement carried in statement.description instead. problem 3/3/3/3/3/3 (five examples: oversized default never chosen, unused Matryoshka truncation, declared-vs-deployed fiction, quietly-raised-for-quality, irreversibility lock-in — each a vector-store cost failure invisible to the model-selection / hosting / prompt-cost gates); solution 3/3/3/3/3/2 (Option A artefacts named with paths + gate points; Option B explicitly optional so coverage_completeness scopes to Option A); gates 3/3/3/3/3/3 (two pre_merge required-status-check gates: declaration+consistency, and dimension-inflation governance). dependencies (schema v1.10): GC1B1-01 soft (the embedding-model choice GC1B1-01's selection ADR records determines the dimension range picked within; the dimension gate operates on the vector-store config independently of that ADR) — parallel to GC2B2-01's soft dependency on GC1B1-01. references (schema v1.11): two real-world links populated at authoring — Towards Data Science (vector-store cost linear in dimension; dimension reduction cuts infra cost up to 80% with retrieval quality held) and MindStudio/Gemini Embedding 2 (3,072 truncatable to 768/256 without significant retrieval-quality drop). tier rubric D1=no (embedding dimension is cost discipline; no regulator/audit/litigation exposure) / D2=2 (a declaration+consistency lint with a multi-backend index-config parser + an inflation-governance lint, reused across projects with ongoing parser maintenance) → recommended_centralise → enterprise; validator project_architect, audit_mode self_attestation_with_mechanical_evidence, arb_role dashboard_and_spot_check parallel to the cost siblings. maturity_level scaling — pays off at project #2: the deployed-vs-declared dimension lint amortises once a portfolio of vector stores exists, and the parser is not first-project capability; lighter than GC2B2-01 (mature) because it reads a single scalar (index dimension) from config rather than GC2B2-01's full GPU/instance/precision/deployed-alarm verification; foundational rejected because the multi-backend parser is not first-project capability. serving_paradigm all four mandatory — vector-store storage/query cost and embedding-output-token cost both exist regardless of how the generation model is served (the dimension drives cost on every paradigm), unlike GC3B3-01's managed-API-only cached-rate benefit. impact_level Medium (matches AWS BP01 risk rating Medium and the GC1B1-01/GC2B2-01/GC3B1-01/GC3B3-01 cost-pillar pattern; over-dimensioning is recoverable, if painfully, via re-embed — not a customer regression or safety event). applicability { rag: mandatory, agentic: mandatory, ml: nice_to_have }; llm OMITTED because a pure LLM-only workload with no retrieval has no vector store and the principle does not materially apply — the first cost principle to narrow applicability by omission (parallel to GC2B2-01 narrowing serving_paradigm by omission).
