explain_prompt.system:
You are a senior software architect explaining the failure mode that GC4B1-01 — Use the smallest embedding vector that still retrieves well — prevents, using a single illustrative scenario from a real industry.

THE PRINCIPLE

Every vector store declares, next to its index configuration, the embedding model it uses, the vector dimension it embeds at, the retrieval-quality bar that dimension must clear, and the measured quality result that justified it. The declared dimension must be a deliberate choice — the smallest that clears the recorded quality bar — not the embedding model's default taken unquestioned. A pre-merge gate fails the build when the declaration is missing or incomplete, when the dimension of the actually-deployed index does not match the declared dimension, or when a later change raises the dimension without a recorded cost/quality rationale.

THE FAILURE SHAPE

This is a setup-absence failure with an irreversibility twist: the dimension is taken at the embedding model's default and never deliberately chosen, and because re-dimensioning means re-embedding and rebuilding the whole index, the oversized default becomes effectively permanent. The team builds a retrieval system: documents are chunked, run through an embedding model, and loaded into a vector store. The embedding model emits some default dimension (often 1536 or 3072). Nobody tests whether a smaller dimension would retrieve almost as well — the default works, the answers look right, the system ships. Vector-store storage and per-query cost scale roughly linearly with dimension, so the oversized default quietly bills several times what a smaller dimension would, and the only signal is a vector-database line item finance absorbs as infrastructure cost.

The absence is forced into view by a triggering event: a quarterly cost review flags the vector database as a top-N spend item; a finance partner asks why the index costs what it does; a migration or audit examining the embedding setup finds the index was built at the model default with no smaller-dimension test on record; someone notices the embedding model was a Matryoshka/MRL model that could have been truncated to a third of the dimensions with negligible recall loss. And then the twist: fixing it means re-embedding the entire corpus and rebuilding the index — a multi-week migration that competes with feature work and usually loses — so the team keeps paying for the oversized index because the choice was never made at build time when it was cheap. Close variants: a declared dimension that does not match the dimension the index was actually built at (a default constructor argument never overridden), and a dimension quietly raised 'to improve quality' with no measurement, tripling cost for a gain nobody verified.

The principle's value is forcing the dimension to be a recorded, justified choice before the corpus is embedded — so an oversized default fails the PR rather than locking in a recurring vector-store cost for the life of the index.

APPLICABILITY

GC4B1-01 is mandatory for RAG and Agent workloads — anywhere a vector store of embedded content exists; RAG workloads over large corpora benefit most. It is nice_to_have for traditional ML workloads (embeddings-as-features / similarity search exist but vector-store cost is rarely the dominant ML failure) and does not materially apply to pure LLM-only workloads with no retrieval. If the chosen Implementation type is LLM Only or ML, open use_case_background with one italicised sentence noting the principle applies to workloads that build a vector store of embedded content (RAG and agentic retrieval), and that the example below is illustrative of how the same over-dimensioning cost failure would manifest in the closest analogue (for example a similarity-search or semantic-index component).

YOUR TASK

You will be given an Industry and an Implementation type. Write an illustrative example for that combination demonstrating GC4B1-01's failure mode.

OUTPUT CONTRACT

Respond with a single valid JSON object of exactly this shape and nothing else:

{
  "use_case_background": "string — 150–250 words. Describe an enterprise with a retrieval workload in production: a corpus of documents chunked, embedded, and loaded into a named vector store. Be specific about the industry and the implementation type. Name the workload, the team, the embedding model and roughly how many chunks/vectors are in the index, and the vector store (with a plausible invented or real name). Crucially, hint at the absence offhandedly — the embedding dimension is whatever the model emits by default, nobody tested a smaller size, vector-store cost has never been a review-time concern, and the index has been live long enough that re-embedding would be a project in itself. The reader should picture a confident team that took the default and moved on. Do NOT describe the failure here.",
  "issue_detail": "string — 200–350 words. Describe the moment the oversized-default cost surfaces. Walk through chronologically: name the trigger (a quarterly cost review flags the vector database; a finance partner asks why the index costs what it does; a migration audit examines the embedding setup; someone discovers the model supported truncation all along). Then describe what the team finds — the index was built at the model's default dimension with no smaller-size test on record; a smaller dimension would have cleared their retrieval bar at a fraction of the storage and query cost; OR the declared/intended dimension does not match what the index was actually built at; OR a dimension someone raised for 'quality' that was never measured. Then the twist: fixing it requires re-embedding the entire corpus and rebuilding the index, a multi-week migration that competes with feature work and stalls, so the cost stays locked in. Name roles (the platform engineer building the cost dashboard; the finance partner asking for attribution; the engineer who took the default). Make the consequence concrete — months or quarters of accumulated over-cost on the vector store, a saving that was available at build time and simply never taken. The failure must demonstrate something a declare-and-verify dimension gate would have caught at the PR before the corpus was embedded — not a generic 'our bill was high' regression."
}

STYLE RULES

- Concrete over abstract. Name systems, roles, the embedding model, the vector store. 'The policy-document index in Pinecone' not 'the AI database'.
- Match the implementation type honestly: RAG retrieves before generating; Agent reasons over multiple steps and calls tools (and may query a vector store as a retrieval tool or memory); LLM Only is single-turn or prompt-chained without retrieval; ML is classical with no LLM in the loop.
- Match the industry honestly. Use vocabulary the industry actually uses.
- Use specific numbers where they sharpen the story, but signpost invented precision honestly ('roughly 8 million vectors', 'about a third of the dimensions', 'a quarter before anyone looked').
- Do not invent regulatory specifics. Generic framing only ('the firm's quarterly cost review', 'the platform team's cost audit').
- Both sections are prose paragraphs. No bullet lists, no headings, no markdown emphasis other than the optional italicised opening sentence described above.
- Do not include the principle's solution, the fix, or recommendations. Show the problem only.
- Do not write any text outside the JSON object.

standards.u_principle: A system's cost and reach stay inside bounds set in advance.

references.title: The $47,000 Agent Loop: Why Token Budget Alerts Aren't Budget Enforcement
references.note: Documents this principle's exact failure mode and the alerts-are-not-enforcement gap it closes: four cooperating LangChain agents ping-ponged in an unterminated but otherwise-valid reasoning loop for eleven days and burned roughly $47,000 — no agent malfunction, simply no external stopping condition to halt the loop, with monitoring dashboards staying green because after-the-fact alerting is not pre-execution enforcement.

references.title: Preventing Runaway AI Agent Costs and Token Spirals
references.note: Records a single misbehaving research-agent session burning roughly $48,000 of model spend in about fourteen hours, and names the root-cause pattern this principle enforces against — no per-agent run ceiling and no mechanism to terminate a session, so an iterative agent that never converges bills without bound until it is killed by hand.

references.question: GENCOST05 — How do you optimize agent workflows for cost?
references.best_practice: GENCOST05-BP01 — Create stopping conditions to control long-running workflows

implementation_step.title: Implement stopping conditions that enable an agent to run to the maximum duration
implementation_step.verbatim_text: Implement stopping conditions that enable an agent to run to the maximum duration. Stopping conditions may be a timeout mechanism like the one in Amazon Bedrock. Alternatively, stopping conditions may be implemented in the prompt flow layer or within a software abstraction layer.

references.note: AWS GENCOST05-BP01 'Create stopping conditions to control long-running workflows' (High risk) tells teams to develop controls that limit agents from running for extended periods without stopping, so that the maximum cost of an agent's runtime becomes predictable from the implemented stopping conditions — but it leaves the discipline as advice: no declared artefact, no check that a stopping condition is actually wired into the agent, and no gate. This principle concretises step 2 ('Implement stopping conditions that enable an agent to run to the maximum duration') into an enforceable contract: a declared per-agent run-limits record (max steps + max wall-clock + max per-run token/cost budget + the terminal action + the runtime estimate that justified the ceilings) plus a pre-merge gate that fails when the declaration is missing, when the agent entrypoint is constructed without the declared ceilings actually bound, or when a ceiling is raised without a recorded cost rationale. It absorbs step 1 ('Estimate the maximum time needed for an agent to complete its runtime', including model-response, tool-execution, and network time) as the gate's input — the estimate is recorded in the declaration's estimated_max field, surfaced as a deliberate number rather than hunted. Step 3 ('Re-architect your workflows to facilitate stopping conditions') is not_promoted: its tool/Lambda/API-endpoint timeouts and 'prompts understand how to handle timeout responses' substance is reliability-error-handling that belongs to a future GENREL walk (cross-pillar), and its 'set token limits on model responses' sub-bullet is already covered by GO3B1-01's per-call max_tokens cap / GC3B1-01's output-budget — sibling-absorbed, with nothing architecturally distinct surviving for the cost pillar. The vendor flavour in step 2 (the Amazon Bedrock timeout mechanism) is one option among the explicitly named generic alternatives ('the prompt flow layer or within a software abstraction layer'), so the generic mandate survives stripping the Bedrock callout. BP title, step verbatim_text, and the GENCOST05 question stem ('How do you optimize agent workflows for cost?') all verified against the live AWS Lens pages (BP page + gencost05.html focus-area page fetched 2026-06-06). GENCOST05 ships exactly one BP (BP01), so the focus area is fully covered by this single principle. First principle under GENCOST05; new focus area P54 — Cost-informed Agents created. AWS risk rating is High — higher than the Medium of every GENCOST01–04 BP — which this principle carries through to impact_level High.

references.domain: Domain III: Understanding How to Govern AI Development
references.competency: III.B: Govern the AI Model Lifecycle (Design & Build)
references.note: Provisional mapping based on subject-matter proximity — committing declared run ceilings and a terminal condition into an agent at build time is a design-and-build-time governance control over the agent's cost and runaway-behaviour surface, aligning with III.B's lifecycle scope, parallel to the cost siblings GC1B1-01 / GC2B2-01 / GC3B1-01 / GC3B3-01 / GC4B1-01. Arguably also touches a deployment/operations competency since the control bounds runtime behaviour; III.B chosen for consistency with the cost-pillar pattern. Needs a side-by-side review against the AIGP III.B competency definition to promote to verified.

statement.title: Give every agent a limit it cannot run past
statement.description:
An agent with no bound can loop, overspend, or run away, and the cost is only discovered after the fact.

Every agent should have a deliberate ceiling on how far a single run can go, so its worst case is predictable rather than found on an invoice.

problem.description:
Agents are built to iterate — they retry, re-plan, call tools, and re-confirm — and every step costs tokens and often external calls, so an agent with no terminal condition can bill without bound.

The dominant failure is that no ceiling is ever set: a team ships an agent on the framework's default (or absent) recursion/step limit, with no wall-clock cap and no per-run token budget, and one day a normal-looking task fails to converge — two agents ping-pong, a tool returns malformed data the agent keeps retrying, a plan recurses into sub-plans — and the run loops for hours or days, billing tens of thousands of dollars before a human notices. The signal is weakest exactly when it matters: cost dashboards and budget alerts are after-the-fact telemetry, not pre-execution enforcement, so they stay green while a single session spirals.

The discipline is fragile in two further ways:

- A ceiling declared in a design doc but never wired into the agent's construction is fiction — the agent loops past a limit it was supposed to have.
- A ceiling quietly raised 'so the task finishes' with no rationale multiplies the worst-case run cost with no trail.

None of these are visible to per-PR review, and no other cost gate looks at agent run-length — model selection, hosting right-sizing, prompt-token budgets, and vector-store dimension are all blind to how many times an agent loops or how long a run lasts.

Examples below are illustrative — synthesised scenarios; specific numbers are not from cited evidence.
examples: A market-research agent built from four cooperating sub-agents ships with each sub-agent on its framework default step limit and no wall-clock or token ceiling; in production an analyzer and a verifier begin ping-ponging requests neither treats as final, the loop runs unattended across a long weekend, and the run bills tens of thousands of dollars before a Monday spend alert surfaces it — an otherwise-valid reasoning loop that nothing was built to terminate.

A customer-support agent calls an order-status tool that starts returning malformed payloads; with no max_steps ceiling the agent treats each failure as a reason to retry and re-calls the tool indefinitely, and a single stuck conversation accumulates thousands of model calls until an on-call engineer kills the process by hand.

A research agent has no wall-clock cap; one user request triggers a deep multi-hop plan that recursively spawns sub-tasks, the run executes for fourteen hours, and the session alone costs roughly a hundred times an ordinary request — a cost discovered from the invoice, not predicted from any declared ceiling.

An agent's design doc records a 25-step recursion limit, but the orchestration graph is constructed without the recursion limit actually set, so the declared number is fiction; no gate compares the wired-in construction against the declaration, the agent loops well past 25 steps, and the team believes a ceiling is protecting them while none is.

An engineer raises an agent's step ceiling from 25 to 250 in a PR to get a stubborn task to finish; no cost rationale accompanies the change, the worst-case per-run cost rises roughly tenfold, the PR passes review as a routine config tweak, and a quarter later nobody can say why the ceiling sits where it does.

solution.approach:
The baseline mandate (Option A — declared-and-wired, config/code-derivable) requires every agent to commit a run-limits declaration alongside its agent definition (convention `agent/run_limits.yaml`, or an equivalent manifest row) carrying:

- `max_steps` — the maximum number of reasoning/tool-call iterations in a single run.
- `max_wallclock_seconds` — the maximum wall-clock duration of a run.
- `max_run_token_budget` — the maximum total tokens (or cost) a single run may consume.
- `on_limit` — the terminal action when any ceiling is reached: halt, return-partial, or escalate.
- `estimated_max` — the expected maximum runtime that justified the ceilings, derived per AWS step 1 from model-response, tool-execution, and network time.
- `checked_on` — the date the ceilings were last reviewed.

A central lint (convention `agent/stop_check`) enforces three things at pre-merge:

- **Declaration completeness** — the declaration exists and every field is populated; the build fails when it is absent or any field is blank, forcing the ceilings to be a recorded decision rather than a framework default or an omission.
- **Wired-in consistency** — it parses the agent's construction (the orchestration-framework limit: LangGraph `recursion_limit`, CrewAI `max_iter` / `max_execution_time`, a Bedrock agent timeout, or a custom loop guard) and fails when the agent entrypoint is constructed without the declared ceilings actually bound, closing the declared-but-not-enforced case where a limit exists on paper but nothing stops the loop.
- **Ceiling-relaxation governance** — any PR that raises a ceiling above its previous committed value (diffed against the merge base) must carry a recorded cost rationale (a `change_history` entry in the declaration or a linked ADR under `docs/adrs/`); the lint fails an upward change without one, closing the quietly-raised-to-make-it-finish case.

All three checks read the repo (the declaration plus the agent construction code) — no deployed runtime, no fixtures — so they fire at review time, before an unbounded agent ever reaches production. The gate deliberately enforces that ceilings are declared and structurally wired in; it does NOT prove the agent halts at exactly the budget under live load, because that is runtime behaviour.

One reference implementation is documented for workloads wanting live enforcement and MAY be adopted in addition to (not instead of) the Option A baseline: **Option B (runtime circuit-breaker)** instruments the run with a cost/step/duration meter that hard-stops the session when actual consumption crosses the declared ceiling and emits the stop event to the observability pipeline — closer to guaranteeing the predictable-maximum-cost outcome AWS names, at the cost of coupling to runtime telemetry (GO3B2 observability territory). The reference implementation at `data/ri/GC5B1-01/README.md` is built on Option A.
key_benefits: Forces a deliberate, recorded ceiling before an agent ships — the unbounded default that today lets a single non-converging run bill tens of thousands of dollars cannot merge without declared step / duration / token ceilings and a terminal action, so the agent's maximum cost is predictable rather than discovered after a runaway.

Config/code-derivable and deterministic — the gate reads the run-limits declaration and the agent's own construction code, so it bites at review time with no deployed runtime and no fixtures, catching an unbounded agent before its first production session rather than after the invoice; this is the pre-execution enforcement that cost dashboards and budget alerts, being after-the-fact, cannot provide.

The wired-in consistency check stops the ceiling from being fiction — an agent whose graph is constructed without the declared recursion/step limit bound fails the build, the same way GC4B1-01's deployed-vs-declared dimension check and GC2B2-01's code-vs-record lint close the declared-but-not-real loophole.

The relaxation-governance check closes the quietly-raised dodge — a step, duration, or budget ceiling cannot be multiplied 'to make the task finish' without a recorded cost rationale, so a change that raises worst-case run cost leaves an auditable trail instead of passing as a routine config edit.

Covers a cost blind spot no sibling addresses and is distinct from per-call token caps — GO3B1-01 caps `max_tokens` on a single response and GC3B1-01 caps a prompt's token footprint, but neither bounds how many times an agent loops or how long a run lasts; the cost lever here is the number of steps and the duration of an agent run, the one dimension that makes an agent's maximum spend predictable.

gates.check: On any PR touching an agent definition or its run-limits declaration, a lint verifies that the declaration (convention `agent/run_limits.yaml` or equivalent manifest row) exists and populates `max_steps`, `max_wallclock_seconds`, `max_run_token_budget`, `on_limit`, `estimated_max`, and `checked_on`, and fails the build when the declaration is absent or any field is blank. The same lint parses the agent's construction (the orchestration-framework limit: LangGraph `recursion_limit`, CrewAI `max_iter` / `max_execution_time`, a Bedrock agent timeout, or a custom loop guard) and fails when the agent entrypoint is constructed without the declared ceilings actually bound. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

On any PR that raises a declared ceiling (`max_steps`, `max_wallclock_seconds`, or `max_run_token_budget`) above its previous committed value (diffed against the merge base), the PR must also add a rationale — either a `change_history` entry in the declaration or a linked ADR under `docs/adrs/` — recording why the ceiling was raised and the per-run cost delta. A lint detects any upward ceiling change without an accompanying rationale entry and fails the PR. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

changes.author: Dheeraj
changes.summary: Initial authoring against AWS GENCOST05-BP01 (Create stopping conditions to control long-running workflows), anchored on implementation step 2 (Implement stopping conditions that enable an agent to run to the maximum duration) with step 1 (Estimate the maximum time needed for an agent to complete its runtime) absorbed as the gate's input — the runtime estimate is recorded in the declaration's estimated_max field, surfaced as a deliberate number rather than hunted manually. First principle under GENCOST05 / GenCost Q5; first principle in the new focus area P54 — Cost-informed Agents (created this session to mirror AWS GENCOST05's verified focus-area title 'Cost-informed agents', distinct from P41 Model Selection & Right-sizing / P51 Inference Cost Optimization / P52 Cost-aware Prompting / P53 Cost-informed Vector Stores per the one-focus-area-per-AWS-question mapping). 15th principle in the catalogue (9 GENOPS + 6 GENCOST). BP title, step-2 verbatim_text, and the GENCOST05 question stem ('How do you optimize agent workflows for cost?') all verified against the live AWS Lens pages (gencost05-bp01.html + gencost05.html fetched 2026-06-06); GENCOST05 ships exactly one BP (BP01), so the focus area is closed by this single principle. step_promotion rubric scored 3/3/3/3 (has_enforceable_artefact: declared per-agent run-limits record + pre-merge declaration/wired-in-consistency lint + ceiling-relaxation governance lint; architecturally_distinct: the cost lever is the NUMBER OF STEPS and the DURATION of an agent run, not a single response's token cap (GO3B1-01's max_tokens) nor a prompt's token footprint (GC3B1-01) nor vector-store dimension (GC4B1-01) — no catalogue principle bounds agent loop count or run duration; in_bp_scope: GENCOST05 is literally 'Cost-informed agents' and BP01 literally 'Create stopping conditions to control long-running workflows'; not_vendor_menu: step-2 verbatim names the Amazon Bedrock timeout as ONE option but explicitly offers the generic 'prompt flow layer or software abstraction layer' alternatives, so a vendor-neutral mandate survives extraction). Step 3 (Re-architect your workflows to facilitate stopping conditions) NOT_PROMOTED: its tool/Lambda/API-endpoint timeouts and 'prompts understand how to handle timeout responses' substance is reliability-error-handling belonging to a future GENREL walk (cross-pillar), and its 'set token limits on model responses' sub-bullet is already covered by GO3B1-01's per-call max_tokens cap and GC3B1-01's output budget (sibling-absorbed) — nothing architecturally distinct survives for the cost pillar (see lens_mapping.md GENCOST05-BP01 ledger). SOLUTION: Option A (declared-and-wired, config/code-derivable) is the mandated spine — declare max_steps + max_wallclock_seconds + max_run_token_budget + on_limit + estimated_max + checked_on; an `agent/stop_check` lint enforces (1) declaration completeness, (2) wired-in consistency by parsing the agent's orchestration-framework construction (LangGraph recursion_limit / CrewAI max_iter+max_execution_time / Bedrock agent timeout / custom loop guard) so a declared-but-unbound limit fails, (3) ceiling-relaxation governance requiring a recorded cost rationale for any upward change. The gate enforces ceilings are declared and structurally wired in; it does NOT prove the agent halts at the budget under live load (runtime behaviour). Option B (runtime circuit-breaker: a cost/step/duration meter that hard-stops a run when actual consumption crosses the declared ceiling and emits the event to observability) documented within solution.approach as an additive reference implementation a workload MAY adopt; the RI and gates are built on Option A only, keeping gate coverage clean — parallel to GC4B1-01's Option A/B treatment and GC3B1-01's Option A/B/C treatment. STATEMENT TITLE deliberately kept plain ('Give every agent a hard stop') per the user's working-style rule committed to data/CLAUDE.md (plain spoken titles, no stacked clauses); this trades the statement rubric's names_artefact_and_enforcement dimension down in favour of the override, with the artefact + enforcement carried in statement.description instead. Hand-scored against section rubrics: statement is_prescriptive 3 (imperative 'Give') / derives_from_aws_verbatim 3 (AWS step 2 'implement stopping conditions … run to the maximum duration', plus the BP's 'predictable maximum cost' desired outcome) / names_artefact_and_enforcement 1 (deliberately, per the plain-title override — recovered in description) / scope_match 3 / parallel_form_with_siblings 2 (plainer than the GC3xxx sibling titles by design, matches the GC4B1-01 plain-title register); problem 3/3/3/3/3/3 (five examples: never-set ceiling / four-agent ping-pong runaway, tool-retry loop, no-wall-clock multi-hop runaway, declared-vs-wired fiction, quietly-raised ceiling — each an agent-run-length cost failure invisible to the model-selection / hosting / prompt-cost / vector-dimension gates and to after-the-fact cost alerts); solution 3/3/3/3/3/2 (Option A artefacts named with paths + three gate behaviours; Option B explicitly optional so coverage_completeness scopes to Option A); gates 3/3/3/3/3/3 (two pre_merge required-status-check gates: declaration+wired-in-consistency, and ceiling-relaxation governance). dependencies (schema v1.10): [] — standalone; the per-call max_tokens substrate (GO3B1-01) is adjacent but not a substrate dependency (this principle bounds run length, not per-call output), and the run-limits declaration + construction lint operate without any other principle's artefact. references (schema v1.11): two real-world links populated at authoring from a web sweep — DEV Community/Waxell (four LangChain agents looping eleven days for ~$47,000; alerts-are-not-enforcement) and n1n.ai (~$48,000 in fourteen hours from one session; no per-agent ceiling / no termination mechanism root cause). tier rubric D1=no (agent cost discipline; no regulator/audit/litigation exposure) / D2=2 (a declaration-completeness lint plus a framework-aware wired-in-consistency parser across LangGraph / CrewAI / Bedrock / custom loop guards plus a relaxation-governance lint, reused across projects with ongoing maintenance as orchestration frameworks evolve — significant but lighter than GC2B2-01's full multi-cloud IaC parse) → recommended_centralise → enterprise; validator project_architect, audit_mode self_attestation_with_mechanical_evidence, arb_role dashboard_and_spot_check parallel to the cost siblings. maturity_level FOUNDATIONAL — pays off at project #1: the gate reads the agent's own construction code in the workload repo (no deployed artefact, no baseline, no production telemetry), so depends_on_deployment is clean, and a single unbounded agent run can be catastrophic on its very first production session (AWS High risk; the cited incidents are five- and six-figure single events), so skipping it compounds as immediate risk/debt — matches the GO1B1-01/02/03 per-PR-repo-local-lint foundational pattern and the maturity rubric's 'per-PR repo-local lint labelled scaling = FAIL' anchor; principled outlier from the GENCOST cost siblings (GC4B1-01 scaling, GC2B2-01 mature) because those parse DEPLOYED resources (the live vector index, the cloud IaC + monitoring), whereas this parses the agent's source construction available at PR time. impact_level HIGH — matches AWS BP01's High risk rating (the first GENCOST principle at High; GC1B1-01/GC2B2-01/GC3B1-01/GC3B3-01/GC4B1-01 are all Medium matching their Medium-rated BPs) and the failure shape: a runaway agent spikes cost rapidly and unpredictably (the BP's whole desired outcome is cost PREDICTABILITY), unlike the slow recoverable over-spend of the Medium cost siblings. applicability { agentic: mandatory } only — llm / rag / ml OMITTED because a stopping condition on agent iteration only exists where an agent loops; a single-shot LLM call, a fixed retrieve-then-generate RAG pipeline, and a classical ML workload have no agent loop to bound (parallel to GO1B1-01 and GO1B1-05's agentic-only narrowing, and consistent with GC4B1-01's precedent of narrowing applicability by omission). serving_paradigm all four mandatory — a runaway agent loop bills regardless of how the generation model is served (API or self-hosted; loops cost tokens/compute either way). AIGP cross-reference Domain III.B (Govern the AI Model Lifecycle — Design & Build), mapping_state unverified, parallel to the cost siblings. explain_prompt compiled with a setup_absence failure shape (the stopping-condition discipline was never set up; a runaway run or a cost review makes the absence visible) with a runaway-spike trigger emphasis distinguishing it from GC4B1-01's slow-default-cost shape — the agent failure is a discrete, fast, potentially catastrophic loop rather than a gradual over-bill. RI README at data/ri/GC5B1-01/README.md built on Option A, following the GC4B1-01 / GC3B1-01 template. OPEN ITEMS: (1) JSON parse not run in-session — the sandbox cannot reach the WSL mount; verify with `python3 -c "import json; json.load(open('data/principles.json'))"` from a terminal before pushing. (2) Frontend — P54 is a new focus_area string; confirm the runtime renders it with no registry change needed (focus_area is free-text, so it should); run `npm run build` from `s3-json-viewer/` if in doubt. (3) AIGP III.B mapping_state unverified — promote to verified after a side-by-side review against the AIGP competency definition.

explain_prompt.system:
You are a senior software architect explaining the failure mode that GC5B1-01 — Give every agent a hard stop — prevents, using a single illustrative scenario from a real industry.

THE PRINCIPLE

Every agent declares, alongside its agent definition, the ceilings that bound a single run — the maximum number of reasoning/tool-call steps, the maximum wall-clock duration, and a maximum per-run token-or-cost budget — plus the terminal action taken when any ceiling is reached, and the runtime estimate that justified the ceilings. The agent must be constructed so those ceilings are actually wired in and the run terminates when one is hit. A pre-merge gate fails the build when the declaration is missing or incomplete, when the agent is constructed without the declared ceilings bound, or when a ceiling is raised without a recorded cost rationale.

THE FAILURE SHAPE

This is a setup-absence failure with a runaway-spike trigger: the stopping condition is never set up — the agent ships on a framework default or no limit at all — and because agents iterate (retry, re-plan, re-confirm), one day a run fails to converge and loops without bound, billing a large, fast, unpredictable amount before anyone intervenes. The team builds an agent: it reasons over steps, calls tools, and decides when it is done. In normal operation it converges quickly, so nobody sets a hard ceiling on steps, wall-clock time, or per-run token spend. Then a triggering case arrives — two agents ping-pong responses neither treats as final; a tool starts returning malformed data the agent keeps retrying; a deep plan recurses into sub-plans; a single customer session sends the agent down a path with no exit. The run loops for hours or days. Cost dashboards and budget alerts stay green or lag, because they are after-the-fact telemetry, not a pre-execution stop. The bill — often five or six figures from a single incident — is discovered after the fact.

The absence is forced into view by the runaway itself or by a review: a spend alert finally fires after a weekend; a finance partner asks why one session cost what a month of normal traffic does; a post-incident review finds the agent had no step limit, no wall-clock cap, and no per-run budget, or finds a limit that was written in a design doc but never wired into the agent's construction, or a ceiling someone quietly raised 'to get a task to finish' that multiplied the worst-case cost with no record. The principle's value is forcing the ceilings to be a declared, wired-in, justified choice before the agent ships — so an unbounded agent fails the PR rather than discovering its maximum cost from an invoice after a runaway.

APPLICABILITY

GC5B1-01 is mandatory for Agent workloads — anywhere an agent iterates over steps and decides when it is done. It does not materially apply to single-shot LLM-only calls, fixed retrieve-then-generate RAG pipelines, or classical ML workloads, none of which have an agent loop to bound. If the chosen Implementation type is LLM Only, RAG, or ML, open use_case_background with one italicised sentence noting the principle applies to agentic workloads that loop over reasoning/tool-call steps, and that the example below is illustrative of how the same unbounded-run cost failure would manifest in the closest agentic analogue (for example a tool-calling or multi-step orchestration component added to the workload).

YOUR TASK

You will be given an Industry and an Implementation type. Write an illustrative example for that combination demonstrating GC5B1-01's failure mode.

OUTPUT CONTRACT

Respond with a single valid JSON object of exactly this shape and nothing else:

{
  "use_case_background": "string — 150–250 words. Describe an enterprise with an agentic workload in production: an agent that reasons over multiple steps and calls tools to accomplish a task. Be specific about the industry and the implementation type. Name the workload, the team, the orchestration framework (e.g. LangGraph, CrewAI, a Bedrock agent, or a custom loop), the model behind it, and roughly how many steps a normal run takes. Crucially, hint at the absence offhandedly — the agent converges quickly in testing, so nobody set a hard ceiling on steps, wall-clock time, or per-run token spend; the only cost guardrail is a dashboard or a monthly budget alert. The reader should picture a confident team whose agent 'just works' and who never imagined a single run could spiral. Do NOT describe the failure here.",
  "issue_detail": "string — 200–350 words. Describe the moment the unbounded run surfaces. Walk through chronologically: name the trigger (two agents or an agent-and-verifier ping-ponging; a tool returning malformed data the agent keeps retrying; a deep plan recursing into sub-tasks; a single session sent down a path with no exit). Then describe the run looping for hours or days while after-the-fact cost alerts stay green or lag. Then the discovery — a spend alert finally fires; a finance partner asks why one session cost what it did; a post-incident review finds no step limit, no wall-clock cap, and no per-run budget — OR a limit recorded in a design doc but never wired into the agent's construction so it stopped nothing — OR a ceiling someone raised 'to get the task to finish' that was never measured. Name roles (the on-call engineer who killed the process by hand; the finance partner asking for attribution; the engineer who took the framework default). Make the consequence concrete — a five- or six-figure cost from a single incident, or a recurring exposure that was one runaway away the whole time. The failure must demonstrate something a declare-and-wire-in stopping-condition gate would have caught at the PR before the agent shipped — not a generic 'our bill was high' regression and not a model-quality problem."
}

STYLE RULES

- Concrete over abstract. Name systems, roles, the orchestration framework, the model, the tools. 'The claims-triage agent on LangGraph calling the policy-lookup tool' not 'the AI system'.
- Match the implementation type honestly: Agent reasons over multiple steps and calls tools and decides when it is done (this is the native case). For LLM Only / RAG / ML, follow the APPLICABILITY note and frame the closest agentic analogue.
- Match the industry honestly. Use vocabulary the industry actually uses.
- Use specific numbers where they sharpen the story, but signpost invented precision honestly ('a normal run is about 6 steps', 'roughly $40,000 before anyone noticed', 'looped for most of a weekend').
- Do not invent regulatory specifics. Generic framing only ('the firm's quarterly cost review', 'the platform team's incident review').
- Both sections are prose paragraphs. No bullet lists, no headings, no markdown emphasis other than the optional italicised opening sentence described above.
- Do not include the principle's solution, the fix, or recommendations. Show the problem only.
- Do not write any text outside the JSON object.

standards.u_principle: No one should learn through an AI system what they couldn't learn directly.

references.title: The Permission Layer Problem: Why Your Enterprise RAG Is a Security Time Bomb
references.note: Documents this principle's exact failure mode: when documents from Confluence/SharePoint/wikis are embedded into a vector store they lose their source-system permissions, so the retriever has no way to distinguish a confidential contract from a public FAQ and serves whatever is nearest — the cross-authorization-boundary retrieval this principle's identity-scoped filter prevents.

references.title: RAG Systems are Leaking Sensitive Data
references.note: Maps the two dataset-leakage modes this principle addresses — sensitive data leaking to end users and confidential content reaching unauthorized internal users through a shared store with no per-query authorization.

references.title: Secure RAG Pipelines: Prevent Data Leakage with Zero-Trust
references.note: Argues for zero-trust, identity-scoped retrieval over a multi-tenant vector store — the access-controlled namespace / per-query permission filter (OWASP LLM cross-tenant leakage) this principle mandates.

dependencies.reason: Retrieval-authorization decisions (the allowed/denied chunk sets per query) are emitted through GO3B2-01's observability SDK to give the read-side audit trail; the access-control gate and the identity-scoped filter function without it, so the dependency strengthens rather than enables.

references.question: GENSEC01 — How do you manage access to generative AI endpoints?
references.best_practice: GENSEC01-BP03 — Implement least privilege access permissions for foundation models accessing data stores

implementation_step.title: Create least-privilege access policies for foundation model and generative AI workloads
implementation_step.verbatim_text: Create least-privilege access policies for foundation model and generated AI workloads. This Policy Document should contain resource identifiers granting explicit access to specific data in the vector datastore.

references.note: GENSEC01-BP03 (High risk) tells teams to treat foundation models as privileged users when granting data access, and warns that a single vector database may hold data for several use cases with different authorizations so 'controls at the foundation model layer alone are insufficient.' Most of the BP is generic data-access discipline owned by the base WAF Security pillar (it cross-references SEC03-BP01/02 least-privilege, SEC07-BP01/02 data classification, SEC08-BP04 protect-data-at-rest) — VPC deployment, IAM/ABAC roles, PII obfuscation at ingestion, curated-prompt testing. This principle concretises the one GenAI-distinct, sibling-unowned slice: retrieval-time authorization. It anchors step 4 (least-privilege access policies with resource identifiers granting explicit access to specific data in the vector store), absorbs step 1 (classify data by usage — the classification becomes the acl label) and step 2 (role-based access on the store), and operationalises the implementation-guidance line AWS leaves as advice ('Utilizing metadata filtering capabilities in vector stores and knowledge bases can enable more granular access control, allowing for data segregation based on user roles or project requirements'). Steps 5 (test access with curated prompts) and 6 (same for training data) are not_promoted — step 5 is the GO1B1 eval harness pointed at security (testing, not a commitable artefact distinct from the harness), step 6 is training-data governance for the minority who train/fine-tune (cross-touches GENSEC data-poisoning and the base WAF). The ingestion-time PII-removal half of step 3 is data-protection (a future GENSEC data-protection walk), distinct from the acl-labeling half this principle uses. What AWS does NOT specify and this principle adds: documents carry acl labels at ingestion; all retrieval routes through a central retrieval SDK that derives the requesting identity's entitlements and applies a pre-filter (the dev never supplies the filter, closing the over-broad-filter dodge); a pre-merge AST lint bans direct vector-store clients against multi-tenant stores; and — because no static lint can verify a label is CORRECT, only that it is present — a periodic security review samples labels against source-system classification. The honest enforcement boundary (lint enforces the plumbing; correct labels still depend on developer discipline) is recorded in data/enforcement_limits.md.

references.domain: Domain IV: Understanding How to Govern AI Deployment and Use
references.competency: IV.C: Establish Security, Access Control, and Incident Management
references.note: Primary mapping. Per-query identity-scoped authorization over a shared vector store, central retrieval-SDK enforcement, and read-side audit of retrieval decisions directly serve IV.C's access-control scope. Needs side-by-side review against the AIGP IV.C competency definition to promote to verified.

references.domain: Domain III: Understanding How to Govern AI Development
references.competency: III.A: Govern Data for AI Systems
references.note: Secondary mapping. Labelling each ingested document with its access-control metadata and segregating data by role inside the vector store is data governance for AI systems, aligning with III.A. Needs review to verify.

references.control_ref: Control #6 — RAG / retrieval-context governance (GenAI slice of Art 10)
references.note: PRIMARY. The gate (retrieval pre-filtered to the caller's entitlements; 0 unauthorised retrievals) concretises the Art 10 obligation to segregate data by role/use inside a shared store. Asserted from the 2026-06-13 EU AI Act system-level-controls funnel (paid_workshop/part_2.md); not yet read side-by-side against the Article 10 text.

CROSS-REFERENCE. Preventing unauthorised retrieval improves confidentiality, but the gate runs no cybersecurity probes (no injection/poisoning tests). Coverage contribution to Art 15, not an obligation this standard discharges.

statement.title: Never let a user retrieve what they aren't allowed to see
statement.description:
Retrieval should respect the asking user's permissions, handing the model only what that user could have read directly.

A shared knowledge store must never become a way around the access controls that protect its contents.

problem.description:
When documents are embedded into a vector store they lose the permissions they had in their source system — a confidential contract from SharePoint and a public FAQ become two vectors that look identical, and the retriever has no mathematical way to tell them apart.

The dominant failure is the shared store with no per-query authorization: several use cases or tenants are indexed into one vector database, retrieval runs an unfiltered top-k, and a user is handed — and the model answers from — chunks the user was never allowed to read. IAM does not catch this: IAM decides whether the *application* can reach the store at all, not which *chunks within* it a given end-user's query may surface. Classification at the model layer does not catch it either, because the leak happens at retrieval, before the model reasons.

The discipline is fragile in ways a naive gate misses:

- A filter the developer builds by hand can be silently over-broad (an `$in` over every role), so 'a filter is present' is not 'the right rows were excluded'.
- A retrieval call that bypasses the central path and hits the store client directly escapes filtering entirely.
- Even with filtering wired in correctly, a document mislabelled at ingestion (a confidential file tagged `public`) is served to everyone — the filter faithfully enforces the wrong label.

The failure is invisible to per-PR review and surfaces at the worst time — a red-team test, an internal user seeing another department's data in an answer, or an auditor asking who could retrieve what.

Examples below are illustrative — synthesised scenarios; specific numbers are not from cited evidence.
examples: A support assistant indexes handbooks, customer-service material, and — by an ingestion-scope oversight — a folder of HR and finance documents into one vector store; a marketing user asks a benign question, the unfiltered top-k retrieval surfaces a salary-band document as 'relevant context', and the model quotes it back in the answer.

A multi-tenant SaaS RAG product puts every customer's documents in one shared index for cost reasons; tenant A's query retrieves tenant B's uploaded contract because retrieval has no per-tenant filter, and the breach is found only when a customer recognises a competitor's data in a generated summary.

A developer adds a metadata filter but writes `acl.allowed_roles $in [finance, hr, marketing, exec]` — effectively everything — to make local testing pass; a shape-only lint sees a filter argument present and goes green, while the filter excludes nothing.

An engineer, debugging latency, swaps the central retrieval SDK call for a direct `pinecone.query(...)` against the shared index; the identity filter the SDK applied is gone, the direct call ships, and every query now retrieves across all tenants until a red-teamer finds it.

Ingestion tags a confidential board-deck chunk `acl: [public]` by copy-paste error; the central SDK and its identity filter work perfectly and therefore serve the chunk to every user, because the gate can prove the label exists but never that it is correct.

solution.approach:
The baseline mandate (Option A — labelled-and-routed, config/code-derivable) has two committed halves plus a human backstop for the part no lint can reach.

First, every document written to a shared store carries an `acl` block at ingestion — `allowed_roles` (or groups) and an optional `classification` — describing who may see it by role, never a list of named users (which would not scale and would rot). The classification AWS step 1 calls for becomes this label.

Second, all retrieval routes through a central retrieval SDK (convention `retrieval_sdk.retrieve(query, caller_identity)`): the SDK resolves the caller's entitlements from the identity provider and applies the filter as a pre-filter during the nearest-neighbour search, so unauthorized chunks never enter the candidate set. The workload passes the caller's identity, never the filter — this is what makes enforcement real rather than honor-system, because a developer cannot supply an over-broad filter they do not supply at all.

A central lint (convention `retrieval/access_check`) enforces three things at pre-merge, all configured as required status checks:

- **Ingestion labelling** — any write path to a store flagged multi-tenant must populate the `acl` block; a missing label fails the build.
- **Routed retrieval** — an AST lint bans direct vector-store client calls (`pinecone.query`, `opensearch.search`, equivalents) against a multi-tenant store; the central SDK is the only permitted retrieval path, the same mechanism GO3B1-01 uses to ban inline model calls.
- **SDK version floor** — the workload pins the central retrieval SDK at or above the floor, so deprecated filtering logic cannot reach production.

The honest boundary (see `enforcement_limits.md`): these checks prove retrieval is identity-scoped and documents are labelled — the plumbing. They cannot prove a label is *correct*; a mislabelled document is served faithfully to the wrong audience. So the principle pairs the mechanical gates with a **periodic security review** (`quarterly_review`) in which the security team samples ingested documents' `acl` labels against their source-system classification and signs off — the residual developer-discipline gap handled by a named human control rather than wished away. This is why the principle's audit_mode is central_review_at_gate and validator is enterprise_security.

One reference implementation is documented and MAY be adopted in addition to the Option A baseline: **Option B (source-system permission mirroring)** synchronises each document's `acl` from its source system's ACL at ingestion (Confluence/SharePoint/Drive permissions copied onto the chunk) so labels track the source automatically and drift less — stronger on the correctness gap, at the cost of building and maintaining per-source connectors. The RI at `data/ri/GS1B3-01/README.md` is built on Option A.
key_benefits: Closes a leak no other control catches — IAM gates whether the app can reach the store, model-layer classification acts after retrieval; only a per-query identity filter stops a user being handed chunks they could not have read directly, the OWASP LLM cross-tenant / broken-access-control failure for RAG.

Enforcement is real, not honor-system — because the central SDK derives and applies the filter from the caller's identity and the workload never supplies it, the over-broad-filter dodge is structurally impossible, and the AST lint stops a direct store call from bypassing the SDK (the GO3B1-01 no-inline-call pattern applied to retrieval).

Labels travel with the data — tagging acl at ingestion means a document's authorization is a property of the chunk, so the same shared store can serve marketing, finance, and HR users different candidate sets from one index without separate stores per tenant.

Honest about its boundary — the gate enforces the plumbing (identity-scoped retrieval + labels present) and the principle says plainly it cannot verify a label is correct, backing that residual gap with a periodic security label-audit instead of false confidence; recorded as a cross-cutting note in enforcement_limits.md.

Reuses the catalogue's observability substrate — retrieval-authorization decisions emit through GO3B2-01's SDK, so denied-access events and anomalous cross-role retrieval attempts become signals on the existing trace stream rather than a separate logging build.

gates.check: On any PR touching an ingestion path that writes to a vector store flagged multi-tenant (in `retrieval/stores.yaml` or equivalent), a lint verifies every written document populates an `acl` block (`allowed_roles`/groups, optional `classification`) and fails the build when the label is absent or empty. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch (typically `develop` in git-flow setups, `main` in trunk-based setups) via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

On any PR, an AST lint scans for direct vector-store client calls (`pinecone.query`, `opensearch.search`, `weaviate` / `pgvector` / equivalent query calls, or raw HTTP to the store) against any store flagged multi-tenant, and fails when retrieval does not route through the central retrieval SDK (`retrieval_sdk.retrieve(query, caller_identity)`), which derives the caller's entitlements and applies the identity pre-filter. The workload must pass caller identity, not a self-built filter. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

Lint verifies the workload pins the central retrieval SDK at a version ≥ the floor declared in `retrieval/sdk_floor.txt` (maintained by the platform/security team, bumped at each filtering-logic or acl-schema cutover), failing when the pinned version is below the floor so deprecated filtering logic cannot reach production. Configured as a required status check on the integration branch via branch protection — advisory CI runs do not satisfy this gate. Failure blocks merge.

The enterprise security team samples ingested documents' `acl` labels against their source-system classification (or the data-classification register) for each workload backing a multi-tenant store, confirming labels are correct — the property no static lint can verify. Binary sign-off; an unresolved mislabelling finding blocks continued operation of the affected index until corrected. This is the named human control for the residual developer-discipline gap recorded in enforcement_limits.md.

artefacts.name: Per-store multi-tenancy + acl-label coverage report
artefacts.location: CI artefact from the `retrieval/access_check` lint, per workload repo
artefacts.check: Every multi-tenant store's ingestion paths write the acl block; coverage is 100% with no unlabelled write path.

artefacts.name: Routed-retrieval lint log
artefacts.location: CI artefact on the integration branch
artefacts.check: No direct vector-store client call against a multi-tenant store; all retrieval goes through the central retrieval SDK at or above the version floor.

artefacts.name: Quarterly acl label-audit sample
artefacts.check: Sampled document acl labels match their source-system classification; mislabelling findings are tracked to closure.

changes.author: Dheeraj
changes.summary: Initial authoring against AWS GENSEC01-BP03 (Implement least privilege access permissions for foundation models accessing data stores), anchored on implementation step 4 (Create least-privilege access policies for FM/GenAI workloads with resource identifiers granting explicit access to specific data in the vector store), absorbing step 1 (classify data by usage → the acl label) and step 2 (role-based access on the store), and operationalising the BP's implementation-guidance line on vector-store metadata filtering for data segregation by user role. FIRST principle in the Security pillar (P2) and first under GENSEC; new focus area P21 — Endpoint Security created to mirror AWS GENSEC01's verified focus-area title 'Endpoint security' (question 'How do you manage access to generative AI endpoints?', verified 2026-06-07 from gensec01.html). 16th principle in the catalogue (9 GENOPS + 6 GENCOST + 1 GENSEC). Walked interactively with the user across GENSEC01 BP01/BP02 (both not_promoted as generic base-WAF IAM/network discipline) before reaching BP03; the user directed PROMOTE on the retrieval-time-authorization slice after the enforcement mechanism was worked out. step_promotion 3/3/3/3 (has_enforceable_artefact: acl-at-ingestion label + central-retrieval-SDK identity filter + pre-merge labelling/routed-retrieval/version-floor lints; architecturally_distinct: retrieval-time per-query authorization is unowned by any sibling — IAM gates store reachability not per-chunk retrieval, GO3B2-02 governs trace read access not retrieval, GO1B1-06 is model versioning, GO3B1-01 is the prompt registry — and is GenAI-specific because only RAG has a retrieval step; in_bp_scope: GENSEC01-BP03 is literally FM access to data stores; not_vendor_menu: the metadata-filtering + identity-scoped-retrieval mandate is provider-neutral, Bedrock Knowledge Bases / OpenSearch fine-grained access control / pgvector are implementations of the same rule). GENSEC01-BP01 (least-priv IAM to endpoints) and BP02 (private network) NOT_PROMOTED earlier this session as generic base-WAF Security discipline (SEC02/03/05) with the only GenAI-distinct slice (approved-models) already owned by GO1B1-06; BP04 (access monitoring) still UNMAPPED. Steps 5 (curated-prompt access testing — GO1B1 harness pointed at security) and 6 (training-data governance — minority self-train, cross-touches data-poisoning) NOT_PROMOTED; the ingestion-PII-removal half of step 3 is a future GENSEC data-protection concern, distinct from the acl-labelling half used here (see lens_mapping.md GENSEC01-BP03 ledger). ENFORCEMENT-LIMIT EMPHASIS (user-directed): the principle states plainly, in statement.description and solution.approach, that the lint enforces the plumbing (identity-scoped retrieval + labels present) but cannot verify a label is CORRECT — a mislabelled document is served faithfully to the wrong audience — so correct labels remain a developer-discipline responsibility backed by a periodic security label-audit. This is the first principle to make that boundary explicit and is recorded as a new cross-cutting catalogue note at data/enforcement_limits.md (created this session at the user's request, cataloguing the same plumbing-not-correctness boundary across GC5B1-01 wired-in-not-runtime, GC2B2-01/GC4B1-01 declared-vs-real, GO3B1-01/GO1B1-06 route-not-wisdom, and the audit_mode field). OWNERSHIP: first D1=yes principle in the catalogue — cross-tenant/PII data-access carries direct legal/regulatory exposure (GDPR, data residency, broken access control) → mandatory_centralise → tier enterprise; and the first to use audit_mode central_review_at_gate with a populated evidence block and validator enterprise_security, because the residual label-correctness gap is a security-owned human review, not pure self-attestation. arb_role dashboard_and_spot_check. GATES: three pre_merge required-status-check lints (ingestion acl labelling; routed-retrieval AST lint banning direct multi-tenant store calls / forcing the central SDK; SDK version floor) plus one quarterly_review security label-audit with binary sign-off (the human control for the part the lint cannot reach). evidence lists the three artefacts the security validator inspects. SOLUTION: Option A (labelled-and-routed, config/code-derivable) is the mandated spine; Option B (source-system permission mirroring — sync acl from Confluence/SharePoint/Drive ACLs at ingestion so labels track the source) documented as an additive RI a workload MAY adopt, parallel to the Option A/B treatment in GC4B1-01 / GC5B1-01. STATEMENT TITLE kept plain ('Never retrieve what the user isn't allowed to see') per the user's working-style rule in data/CLAUDE.md — trades the statement rubric's names_artefact_and_enforcement dimension down (artefact + enforcement carried in description). applicability { rag: mandatory, agentic: mandatory } — llm OMITTED (no retrieval step on a single-shot LLM call), ml OMITTED (no FM-over-vector-store retrieval); agentic included because agentic RAG retrieves from shared stores with the same per-query authorization risk (narrow-by-omission precedent: GC4B1-01, GC5B1-01). serving_paradigm all four mandatory — the retrieval-authorization failure is a property of the vector store and the requesting identity, independent of how the generation model is served. maturity_level SCALING — the shared-store-with-mixed-authorization failure emerges at multi-team / multi-tenant scale (roughly project #2+), and the enforcement substrate (central retrieval SDK + IdP entitlement resolution + per-source labelling) is platform work that amortises like GO3B2-01/02 (both scaling); the high-risk-regulated-single-app case that would pull to foundational is noted but the dominant shape is scaling. references (schema v1.11): three real-world links populated at authoring from a web sweep — ragaboutit 'The Permission Layer Problem' (embedding strips source permissions; retriever cannot tell confidential from public), we45 'RAG Systems are Leaking Sensitive Data' (the two dataset-leakage modes), Kiteworks 'Secure RAG Pipelines / zero-trust' (identity-scoped retrieval over multi-tenant stores). dependencies (schema v1.10): [{ GO3B2-01: soft }] — retrieval-authorization decisions emit through GO3B2-01's observability SDK for the read-side audit; the access-control gate and identity filter function without it. AIGP IV.C (Security, Access Control, Incident Management) primary + III.A (Govern Data) secondary, both unverified. explain_prompt compiled with a permission-evaporation / broken-access-control failure shape (documents lose source permissions on embedding; a shared store with no per-query filter serves cross-boundary chunks; surfaces at a red-team test / an internal user seeing another team's data / an audit). RI at data/ri/GS1B3-01/README.md built on Option A. OPEN ITEMS: (1) JSON parse not run in-session (sandbox cannot reach the WSL mount) — verify with `python3 -c "import json; json.load(open('data/principles.json'))"` from a terminal before pushing. (2) Frontend — P2 — Security pillar and P21 — Endpoint Security focus_area are new free-text strings; confirm the runtime renders them (focus_area/pillar are free-text, so no registry change expected); run `npm run build` from `s3-json-viewer/` if in doubt. (3) AIGP IV.C / III.A mapping_state unverified — promote after a side-by-side review. (4) GENSEC01-BP04 (access monitoring) still to walk to close GENSEC01.
